topics.title: Experiments on Reynolds apparatus for determination of flow regime and construction of fanning friction factor vs. Reynolds number plot
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL308 - FLUID & PARTICLE MECHANICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Experiments on Reynolds apparatus for determination of flow regime and construction of fanning friction factor vs. Reynolds number plot

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module Experiment 2 — Reynolds Apparatus & Friction Factor Plot

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Reynolds Apparatus** is a standard bench-scale experimental setup used to demonstrate, identify, and characterize the prevailing **flow regime** in a circular conduit. The flow regime is quantified by the dimensionless **Reynolds Number (Re)**, first conceptualized by Osborne Reynolds in 1883. The apparatus also enables the empirical determination of the **Fanning Friction Factor (f)**, which is a dimensionless measure of the resistance to flow offered by the pipe wall. The experiment culminates in the construction of a **log-log plot of f vs. Re**, universally known as the **Moody Chart** (in its Darcy form) or its Fanning equivalent, which is foundational to all chemical and mechanical engineering fluid-flow design calculations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCCHL308):**
> This experiment is a **mandatory record + viva component**. Students must be able to define the apparatus, state the operating principle, calculate Re and f from raw observations, and interpret the resulting plot. The plot construction is a compulsory part of the practical record submission.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Highway Traffic Analogy"

Imagine a single-lane highway where cars represent fluid molecules:

- **Laminar Flow (Re < 2100):** Cars move in neat, orderly, parallel lanes. Even if one car slows, the others smoothly follow. There is no crossing between lanes. The flow looks *stratified* and *predictable*.
- **Transitional Flow (2100 ≤ Re ≤ 4000):** Some cars start weaving, occasionally crossing lanes, and a slight disturbance can flip the whole traffic into chaos. This regime is *unstable* and *unreproducible*.
- **Turbulent Flow (Re > 4000):** Cars are in complete disarray — chaotic swerving, mixing across all lanes, eddies and vortices everywhere. The overall forward motion is fast, but the *energy loss* (friction) is significantly higher.

The **dye injector** in the Reynolds apparatus plays the role of a *colored tracer car* that reveals which "traffic regime" exists inside the pipe.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Reference Values

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density of water at 25 °C | $\rho$ | **997** | kg/m³ |
| Dynamic viscosity of water at 25 °C | $\mu$ | **8.9 × 10⁻⁴** | Pa·s |
| Kinematic viscosity of water at 25 °C | $\nu$ | **8.93 × 10⁻⁷** | m²/s |
| Acceleration due to gravity | g | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Critical Re (Laminar–Transitional) | Re_c,1 | **2100** | dimensionless |
| Critical Re (Transitional–Turbulent) | Re_c,2 | **4000** | dimensionless |
| Fanning friction factor (Laminar) | f_F | **16 / Re** | dimensionless |
| Darcy friction factor (Laminar) | f_D | **64 / Re** | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Conversion:** $f_{Darcy} = 4 \times f_{Fanning}$. Many KTU question papers mix these two conventions — always check the formula sheet provided in the exam before substituting.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle of the Reynolds Apparatus

The apparatus operates on a **dye-tracer visualization** principle coupled with **volumetric flow measurement**. The major sub-systems are:

1. **Constant-Head Overhead Tank:** Ensures a stable hydrostatic driving head, which in turn stabilizes the volumetric flow rate through the test section.
2. **Test Section (Transparent Glass Tube):** A precision-bore glass tube of known internal diameter *D* and a calibrated test length *L* between two pressure tappings.
3. **Dye Reservoir & Injection Needle:** A methylene-blue (or potassium permanganate) dye solution is injected coaxially into the centerline of the test section through a hypodermic needle.
4. **Flow Control Valve (Bypass + Main):** A combination of a main control valve and a bypass valve allows fine adjustment of the flow rate from near-zero to maximum.
5. **Measuring Cylinder + Stopwatch:** Used to collect a known volume of water over a measured time, yielding the volumetric flow rate *Q*.
6. **U-Tube Manometer (or Differential Pressure Transducer):** Connected across the two pressure tappings, measures the pressure drop $\Delta P$ across the test length *L*.
7. **Rotameter / Calibrated Flow Meter (Optional):** Provides a secondary, continuous readout of the flow rate for cross-verification.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Experimental Logic

- **Step 1 — Zero-Flow Baseline:** Close the main control valve completely. The dye stream, being introduced at near-zero velocity, draws a perfectly **straight, unbroken, thread-like filament** along the centerline. This is the **laminar regime (Re < 2100)**.
- **Step 2 — Incremental Flow Increase:** Open the main valve in small increments. The dye thread remains straight but starts to waver slightly. The flow is still laminar but approaching transition.
- **Step 3 — Transition Detection:** As the flow rate is further increased, the dye thread begins to **oscillate, then break up into eddies and swirls**. This is the **transitional regime (2100 ≤ Re ≤ 4000)**. The behaviour is highly sensitive to vibrations and entrance effects.
- **Step 4 — Fully Turbulent:** On opening the valve fully, the dye disperses **uniformly across the entire pipe cross-section**, indicating intense mixing — the **turbulent regime (Re > 4000)**.
- **Step 5 — Quantitative Measurement:** For each flow rate, record:
  * The volumetric flow rate $Q$ from the measuring cylinder and stopwatch.
  * The manometer reading $h$ (in cm of water or mm of Hg) → converted to $\Delta P = (\rho_m - \rho_w) \, g \, h$ for differential manometer, or $\Delta P = \rho_w \, g \, h$ for simple manometer.
- **Step 6 — Computation:** Calculate $Re$ and $f_F$ for each trial.
- **Step 7 — Plotting:** Plot $\log(f_F)$ vs. $\log(Re)$ on a semi-log or log-log sheet and identify the three regimes.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **complete cheat-sheet** a student should memorize before entering the lab exam. Pay careful attention to unit conversions — these are the most common KTU valuation deduction points.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Legend | SI Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reynolds Number (Velocity form) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \, v \, D}{\mu}$ | $v$ = superficial velocity, $D$ = pipe ID, $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity | dimensionless |
| 2 | Reynolds Number (Flow-rate form) | $Re = \dfrac{4 \, Q \, \rho}{\pi \, D \, \mu}$ | $Q$ = volumetric flow rate | dimensionless |
| 3 | Reynolds Number (Mass-flux form) | $Re = \dfrac{4 \, \dot{m}}{\pi \, D \, \mu}$ | $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate | dimensionless |
| 4 | Superficial velocity | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A_c} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ | $A_c$ = cross-sectional area | m/s |
| 5 | Pressure drop (manometer) | $\Delta P = \rho_m \, g \, h$ | $\rho_m$ = manometer fluid density (water/Hg), $h$ = manometer deflection | Pa |
| 6 | Fanning friction factor | $f_F = \dfrac{\Delta P \, D}{2 \, L \, \rho \, v^2}$ | $L$ = length between tappings | dimensionless |
| 7 | Fanning friction factor (in terms of Q) | $f_F = \dfrac{\pi^2 \, D^5 \, \Delta P}{32 \, L \, \rho \, Q^2}$ | — | dimensionless |
| 8 | Friction factor (laminar, theoretical) | $f_F = \dfrac{16}{Re}$ | Hagen-Poiseuille law | dimensionless |
| 9 | Darcy friction factor (laminar) | $f_D = \dfrac{64}{Re}$ | $f_D = 4 f_F$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Wall shear stress | $\tau_w = \dfrac{D \, \Delta P}{4 L}$ | — | Pa |
| 11 | Manometer reading → pressure (water) | $\Delta P = \rho_w \, g \, h$ | — | Pa |
| 12 | Manometer reading → pressure (mercury) | $\Delta P = (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_w) \, g \, h$ | — | Pa |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pipeline Design (Oil & Gas):** Engineers must know whether flow is laminar or turbulent to select the correct pump power and pipe diameter. Friction losses in turbulent flow scale as $\sim v^{1.75}$ instead of $\sim v$ in laminar.
- **Heat Exchanger Sizing:** The Nusselt correlations for laminar and turbulent internal flow are entirely different — a regime misidentification can cause a 50%–200% design error.
- **Chemical Reactors:** Residence time distribution (RTD) is fundamentally different in laminar vs. turbulent regimes; wrong choice of model invalidates conversion predictions.
- **Biomedical Devices:** IV-line flow, blood-flow modelling, and microfluidic drug-delivery chips all rely on Re-based regime maps.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Reynolds Number in Flow-Rate Form

This derivation is the most frequently asked 3-mark derivation in the KTU viva for this experiment.

Starting from the **velocity-form** definition:

$$Re = \frac{\rho \, v \, D}{\mu}$$

**Step 1 — Replace superficial velocity with its definition.**
The superficial (or average) velocity is the volumetric flow rate divided by the cross-sectional area of the pipe:

$$v = \frac{Q}{A_c} = \frac{Q}{\dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}} = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute back into the definition of Re.**

$$Re = \frac{\rho \, D}{\mu} \times \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify algebraically.**

$$Re = \frac{4 \, \rho \, Q}{\pi \, D \, \mu}$$

> This is the **flow-rate form of the Reynolds Number**, used when flow rate $Q$ (m³/s) is the directly measured variable, as is the case in the Reynolds apparatus experiment.

**Step 4 — Express in mass-flux form for completeness.**
Using $\dot{m} = \rho Q$:

$$Re = \frac{4 \, \dot{m}}{\pi \, D \, \mu}$$

**Conversion Check (numerical illustration):**
If $Q = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/s, $D = 0.025$ m, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $\mu = 10^{-3}$ Pa·s:

$$Re = \frac{4 \times 1000 \times 5 \times 10^{-5}}{\pi \times 0.025 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.2}{7.854 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 2546$$

This places the flow in the **transitional regime**.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Fanning Friction Factor from First Principles

The friction factor is derived from a **force balance on a cylindrical fluid element** in fully-developed, steady flow.

**Step 1 — Define the control volume.**
Consider a pipe segment of length $L$ and diameter $D$. The fluid exerts a wall shear stress $\tau_w$ on the inner surface. The pressure at the inlet is $P_1$ and at the outlet is $P_2$, with $P_1 > P_2$ for flow in the positive direction.

**Step 2 — Force balance (steady state, no acceleration).**

$$P_1 \, A_c - P_2 \, A_c - \tau_w \, (\pi D L) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for wall shear stress.**

$$\tau_w = \frac{(P_1 - P_2) \, A_c}{\pi D L} = \frac{\Delta P \, \left(\dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}\right)}{\pi D L}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify.**

$$\tau_w = \frac{\Delta P \, D}{4 L}$$

**Step 5 — Define the Fanning friction factor.**
The Fanning friction factor is defined as the ratio of wall shear stress to the **kinetic-energy per unit volume**:

$$f_F = \frac{\tau_w}{\dfrac{1}{2} \rho v^2}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute the expression for $\tau_w$.**

$$f_F = \frac{\Delta P \, D}{4 L} \times \frac{2}{\rho \, v^2}$$

**Step 7 — Final working formula.**

$$f_F = \frac{\Delta P \, D}{2 \, L \, \rho \, v^2}$$

> This is the **working equation for the Fanning friction factor** that students must use in all lab calculations.

**Step 8 — Substitute $v = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ to obtain the flow-rate form.**

$$f_F = \frac{\pi^2 \, D^5 \, \Delta P}{32 \, L \, \rho \, Q^2}$$

## 3.3 Verification for Laminar Flow: Deriving $f_F = 16 / Re$

This derivation is the most important theoretical check, as it validates the experimental data against the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation**.

**Step 1 — Start with the Hagen-Poiseuille equation for laminar flow in a circular pipe.**

$$\Delta P = \frac{32 \, \mu \, L \, v}{D^2}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to isolate $v / D^2$.**

$$\frac{v}{D^2} = \frac{\Delta P}{32 \, \mu \, L}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the Fanning friction factor equation.**

$$f_F = \frac{\Delta P \, D}{2 L \rho v^2} = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{\Delta P}{L} \times \frac{D}{\rho v^2}$$

**Step 4 — Replace $\dfrac{\Delta P}{L}$ using Hagen-Poiseuille.**

$$f_F = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{32 \, \mu \, v}{D^3} \times \frac{D}{\rho v^2}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify.**

$$f_F = \frac{16 \, \mu}{\rho \, v \, D}$$

**Step 6 — Recognize the Reynolds Number in the denominator.**

$$f_F = \frac{16}{Re}$$

> This is the **theoretical laminar-flow line** that should appear as a straight line of slope $-1$ on the log-log $f_F$ vs. $Re$ plot. The experimental data points in the laminar regime should collapse onto this line.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Full KTU-Style Computation)

**Given Data:**
- Pipe internal diameter: $D = 25$ mm $= 0.025$ m
- Test length between tappings: $L = 1.0$ m
- Water density: $\rho = 998$ kg/m³
- Water viscosity: $\mu = 8.9 \times 10^{-4}$ Pa·s
- Mercury manometer deflection: $h = 8$ cm $= 0.08$ m
- Mercury density: $\rho_{Hg} = 13{,}600$ kg/m³
- Volume collected: $V = 500$ mL $= 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m³
- Collection time: $t = 30$ s

**Step 1 — Volumetric flow rate.**

$$Q = \frac{V}{t} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{30} = 1.667 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Superficial velocity.**

$$v = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2} = \frac{4 \times 1.667 \times 10^{-5}}{\pi \times (0.025)^2} = \frac{6.667 \times 10^{-5}}{1.963 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.03397 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Pressure drop across the test section.**

$$\Delta P = (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_w) \, g \, h = (13600 - 998) \times 9.81 \times 0.08$$

$$\Delta P = 12602 \times 9.81 \times 0.08 = 9889 \, \text{Pa}$$

**Step 4 — Reynolds Number.**

$$Re = \frac{\rho \, v \, D}{\mu} = \frac{998 \times 0.03397 \times 0.025}{8.9 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{0.8475}{8.9 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 952$$

**Step 5 — Fanning friction factor (experimental).**

$$f_{F,exp} = \frac{\Delta P \, D}{2 L \rho v^2} = \frac{9889 \times 0.025}{2 \times 1.0 \times 998 \times (0.03397)^2}$$

$$f_{F,exp} = \frac{247.2}{2.304} \approx 0.01714$$

**Step 6 — Fanning friction factor (theoretical, laminar).**

$$f_{F,theo} = \frac{16}{Re} = \frac{16}{952} \approx 0.01681$$

**Step 7 — Percentage deviation.**

$$\% \, \text{Deviation} = \left| \frac{f_{F,exp} - f_{F,theo}}{f_{F,theo}} \right| \times 100 = \left| \frac{0.01714 - 0.01681}{0.01681} \right| \times 100 \approx 1.96\%$$

> This **< 2% deviation** confirms the laminar assumption and validates the experimental procedure.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Behaviour Across Flow Regimes

| Regime | Re Range | Slope on $\log f - \log Re$ Plot | Empirical Equation (Fanning) | Dye Appearance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Laminar | < 2100 | $-1$ (straight line) | $f_F = 16 / Re$ | Straight thread |
| Transitional | 2100 – 4000 | Undefined (scatter zone) | No closed-form | Oscillating, breaking thread |
| Turbulent (smooth) | 4000 – 10⁵ | $\approx -0.2$ | $f_F = 0.079 \, Re^{-0.25}$ (Blasius) | Fully dispersed, uniform colour |
| Fully Turbulent (rough) | > 10⁵ | Approaches 0 | $f_F = $ const (from Colebrook) | Fully dispersed, uniform colour |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle 2]

**Define the Reynolds Number. State its physical significance and the threshold values that demarcate laminar, transitional, and turbulent flow regimes in a circular pipe.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Reynolds Number (Re)** is a dimensionless group defined as the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces acting on a fluid element. Mathematically:

$$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu}$$

**Physical Significance:**
- $Re \ll 1$: Viscous forces dominate → flow is highly damped and orderly.
- $Re \gg 1$: Inertial forces dominate → flow is chaotic with eddies.

**Threshold Values in Circular Pipes:**

| Regime | Re Range |
|---|---|
| Laminar | $Re < 2100$ |
| Transitional | $2100 \le Re \le 4000$ |
| Turbulent | $Re > 4000$ |

### Question 2 — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supplementary]

**What is the Fanning friction factor? Write its working equation and state its theoretical value for fully-developed laminar flow in a circular pipe.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Fanning friction factor ($f_F$)** is a dimensionless measure of the resistance to fluid flow offered by the pipe wall. It is defined as the ratio of wall shear stress to the kinetic-energy density of the fluid:

$$f_F = \frac{\tau_w}{\frac{1}{2} \rho v^2} = \frac{\Delta P \, D}{2 L \rho v^2}$$

For fully-developed laminar flow in a circular pipe, the **Hagen-Poiseuille law** yields the theoretical value:

$$f_F = \frac{16}{Re}$$

This can be verified by the **Moody plot**, which shows a straight line of slope $-1$ on a log-log scale in the laminar regime.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Regular]

**A Reynolds apparatus has a test section of internal diameter 30 mm and length 1.2 m. Water at 30 °C is used. For a particular flow rate, the volume collected in a measuring cylinder is 400 mL in 25 seconds, and the differential U-tube manometer (mercury over water) shows a deflection of 5 cm.**

**(a) [7 Marks] Calculate the Reynolds number and identify the prevailing flow regime.**

**(b) [7 Marks] Calculate both the experimental and theoretical Fanning friction factors and comment on the percentage deviation.**

**Given Data:**
- $D = 30$ mm $= 0.030$ m
- $L = 1.2$ m
- Water at 30 °C: $\rho = 995.7$ kg/m³, $\mu = 7.98 \times 10^{-4}$ Pa·s
- $V = 400$ mL $= 4 \times 10^{-4}$ m³
- $t = 25$ s
- $h = 5$ cm $= 0.05$ m
- $\rho_{Hg} = 13{,}600$ kg/m³

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Volumetric flow rate.**

$$Q = \frac{V}{t} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-4}}{25} = 1.6 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Superficial velocity.**

$$v = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2} = \frac{4 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-5}}{\pi \times (0.030)^2} = \frac{6.4 \times 10^{-5}}{2.827 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.02264 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Reynolds Number.**

$$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{995.7 \times 0.02264 \times 0.030}{7.98 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{0.6762}{7.98 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 847$$

**Step 4 — Regime identification.**
Since $Re = 847 < 2100$, the flow is **LAMINAR**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Pressure drop.**

$$\Delta P = (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_w) \, g \, h = (13600 - 995.7) \times 9.81 \times 0.05$$

$$\Delta P = 12604.3 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 = 6182.4 \, \text{Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Experimental Fanning friction factor.**

$$f_{F,exp} = \frac{\Delta P \, D}{2 L \rho v^2} = \frac{6182.4 \times 0.030}{2 \times 1.2 \times 995.7 \times (0.02264)^2}$$

$$f_{F,exp} = \frac{185.47}{1.2244} \approx 0.01515$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical Fanning friction factor.**

$$f_{F,theo} = \frac{16}{Re} = \frac{16}{847} \approx 0.01889$$

**Step 4 — Percentage deviation.**

$$\% \, \text{Deviation} = \left| \frac{0.01515 - 0.01889}{0.01889} \right| \times 100 \approx 19.8\%$$

**Comment:** A deviation of ~20% is higher than the ideal 5% benchmark. This could be attributed to:
- Calibration error in the manometer scale.
- Entrance-length effects (the calming section was too short for fully-developed flow at this Re).
- Temperature variation affecting viscosity assumption.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (3-mark deduction per error):**
> 1. Using the **Darcy** formula instead of Fanning without the $1/4$ conversion factor → 3 marks lost.
> 2. Forgetting the bracket $(\rho_{Hg} - \rho_w)$ in the differential manometer equation → 2 marks lost.
> 3. Reporting Re to only 2 significant figures when the data supports 3 → 1 mark lost in precision.
> 4. Failing to **comment on the deviation** (only computing it) → 1 mark lost in interpretation.

---

### Question B (Option 2) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]

**(a) [7 Marks] With the help of a neat sketch, describe the working principle of the Reynolds apparatus. How is the flow regime identified experimentally?**

**(b) [7 Marks] Derive the Fanning friction factor in terms of volumetric flow rate $Q$, and show that for fully-developed laminar flow, $f_F = 16 / Re$.**

#### Model Answer — Part (a)

The Reynolds apparatus consists of:

1. **Overhead constant-head tank** (A) supplying water under gravity.
2. **Main control valve** (B) and **bypass valve** (C) for flow regulation.
3. **Calming section** (D) — straightening vanes to remove swirl.
4. **Transparent test section** (E) — precision glass tube of known *D* and *L*.
5. **Dye reservoir + hypodermic needle** (F) — injects dye along the centerline.
6. **U-tube manometer** (G) — measures $\Delta P$ across the test length.
7. **Measuring cylinder + stopwatch** (H) — quantifies $Q$.

**Working Principle:**

Water flows from the overhead tank through the test section. A dye (methylene blue or KMnO₄) is injected as a thin filament. By gradually increasing the flow rate and observing the dye behaviour, the flow regime is identified:

- **Laminar:** Dye travels as a straight, coherent thread.
- **Transitional:** Dye thread oscillates and breaks into wavy eddies.
- **Turbulent:** Dye disperses uniformly across the entire cross-section, with intense mixing.

**Quantitative Identification:** For each flow setting, the Re is computed and the regime is assigned based on the threshold values (2100 and 4000).

#### Model Answer — Part (b)

**Derivation — Fanning friction factor in terms of Q:**

Starting from:

$$f_F = \frac{\Delta P \, D}{2 L \rho v^2}$$

Substitute $v = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$:

$$f_F = \frac{\Delta P \, D}{2 L \rho \left(\dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}\right)^2} = \frac{\Delta P \, D}{2 L \rho} \times \frac{\pi^2 D^4}{16 Q^2}$$

$$\boxed{f_F = \frac{\pi^2 D^5 \Delta P}{32 L \rho Q^2}}$$

**Derivation — Theoretical laminar value $f_F = 16 / Re$:**

The Hagen-Poiseuille equation for laminar flow in a circular pipe:

$$\Delta P = \frac{32 \mu L v}{D^2}$$

Substitute $\Delta P$ into the $f_F$ equation:

$$f_F = \frac{1}{2 L \rho v^2} \times D \times \frac{32 \mu L v}{D^2}$$

$$f_F = \frac{16 \mu}{\rho v D} = \frac{16}{Re}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part a):**
> 1. Failing to **label the diagram** (especially the dye injection needle position relative to test section) → 2 marks lost.
> 2. Confusing the role of the **calming section** with the test section → 1 mark lost.
> 3. Not stating that the dye is injected **coaxially along the centerline** → 1 mark lost.
> 4. Omitting the quantitative confirmation (Re thresholds) after the qualitative description → 1 mark lost.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):**
> 1. Skipping algebraic steps during the substitution → 1 mark lost per missing step (up to 2 marks).
> 2. Not invoking the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation** by name during the derivation of $16/Re$ → 1 mark lost.
> 3. Forgetting to mention the assumption **"fully-developed, steady, incompressible laminar flow"** → 1 mark lost.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision — Memorize Before Lab Exam**

- [ ] **Reynolds Number Formula (three equivalent forms):** $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \dfrac{4 \rho Q}{\pi D \mu} = \dfrac{4 \dot{m}}{\pi D \mu}$.
- [ ] **Threshold Values (circular pipe):** $Re < 2100$ laminar; $2100 \le Re \le 4000$ transitional; $Re > 4000$ turbulent.
- [ ] **Fanning Friction Factor Working Equation:** $f_F = \dfrac{\Delta P \, D}{2 L \rho v^2}$.
- [ ] **Fanning Friction Factor (Q form):** $f_F = \dfrac{\pi^2 D^5 \Delta P}{32 L \rho Q^2}$.
- [ ] **Theoretical Laminar Value:** $f_F = \dfrac{16}{Re}$ (slope $-1$ on log-log plot).
- [ ] **Darcy vs. Fanning:** $f_D = 4 f_F$ — always check the convention in the question.
- [ ] **Manometer Equations:**
  - Differential (Hg over water): $\Delta P = (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_w) \, g \, h$.
  - Simple (water): $\Delta P = \rho_w \, g \, h$.
- [ ] **Wall Shear Stress:** $\tau_w = \dfrac{D \, \Delta P}{4 L}$.
- [ ] **Standard Water Properties at 25 °C:** $\rho = 997$ kg/m³, $\mu = 8.9 \times 10^{-4}$ Pa·s.
- [ ] **Dye Visualization Rule:** Straight thread = laminar; oscillating/breaking = transitional; uniformly dispersed = turbulent.
- [ ] **Plot Construction Rule:** Always use **log-log paper** (2–4 decades); mark the theoretical laminar line first; then overlay experimental points.
- [ ] **Acceptable Deviation:** Experimental $f_F$ should match theoretical $f_F$ within **±5% to ±10%** for laminar flow; larger deviations signal experimental errors.
- [ ] **Apparatus Components to Name in Viva:** Constant-head tank, calming section, dye reservoir, hypodermic needle, test section, manometer tappings, control valve, bypass valve, measuring cylinder, stopwatch.
- [ ] **Common Lab Errors to Avoid:** Not waiting for steady-state before timing; misreading the manometer at meniscus level; using the wrong water temperature in viscosity tables; forgetting to convert mm to m.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of coefficient of discharge for orifice meter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL308 - FLUID & PARTICLE MECHANICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of coefficient of discharge for orifice meter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Orifice Meter:** An orifice meter is a **differential pressure type flow measurement device** used to measure the volumetric or mass flow rate of a fluid (liquid or gas) flowing through a closed conduit (pipe). It consists of a flat, circular, concentric plate with a precisely machined, sharp-edged hole (called the **orifice**) bored at its center. This plate, known as the **orifice plate**, is clamped between two pipe flanges perpendicular to the direction of flow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Coefficient of Discharge (Cd):** It is defined as the **ratio of the actual (experimental) discharge of the fluid through the orifice to the theoretical (ideal) discharge** calculated using the vena-contracta velocity derived from Bernoulli's equation. Mathematically, $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$. It is a **dimensionless number** and always $C_d < 1$ (typically **0.6 to 0.65** for a standard sharp-edged concentric orifice).

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy (The "Thumb on a Hose" Concept)

Imagine you are watering your garden using a flexible hose. When you keep the hose open, water flows out in a slow, lazy stream. But the moment you press your **thumb partially over the opening**, the water shoots out in a fast, high-velocity jet, and you can throw it across the garden. What happened physically?

- The **hose cross-section** is suddenly restricted by your thumb (just like an orifice restricts the pipe).
- The water **velocity must increase** at the constriction (Continuity Equation).
- This **velocity increase** is accompanied by a **pressure drop** (Bernoulli's Principle).
- The water emerges as a **contracted jet** (the jet is narrower than the hole — this is called the **vena contracta**).
- Due to **friction, turbulence, and viscous losses** (real fluid behavior), the actual flow rate is **slightly less** than what a perfectly ideal, frictionless calculation would predict. The ratio of these two is exactly the **Coefficient of Discharge ($C_d$)**.

> [!NOTE]
> In a fluid mechanics laboratory, we do not measure velocity directly. Instead, we measure the **pressure difference** between the upstream side (Section 1) and the downstream side / vena contracta (Section 2) using a **U-tube manometer**. We then back-calculate $C_d$ from this manometer reading, the pipe diameter, and the orifice diameter.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Setup

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Orifice Diameter ($d_o$)** | Typically $0.5 \times$ pipe diameter | Maintains vena contracta formation |
| **Beta Ratio ($\beta = d_o/D$)** | **0.2 to 0.75** | Industry-standard range for ISO 5167 |
| **Pressure Taps** | Flange / D and D/2 / Corner | Standard tapping locations |
| **Manometer Fluid** | Mercury (for water), or CCl₄ | Immiscible with the flowing fluid |
| **Typical $C_d$** | **0.6 – 0.65** | Sharp-edged, concentric orifice |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is $C_d < 1$?** In an ideal frictionless fluid, applying Bernoulli's equation at the orifice gives a theoretical velocity. In reality, three effects reduce the actual flow:
> 1. **Vena Contracta Effect** — The jet contracts after the orifice, so the effective area is smaller.
> 2. **Frictional Losses** — Viscous drag between the fluid layers and the orifice edge.
> 3. **Turbulence & Swirl** — Sudden contraction creates eddies that dissipate energy.
> Hence: $C_d = C_c \times C_v$, where $C_c$ is the coefficient of contraction and $C_v$ is the coefficient of velocity.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The experiment rests on **three fundamental pillars** of fluid mechanics. We will walk through the logic systematically:

**Step 1 — Mass Conservation (Continuity Equation)**
As the fluid enters the orifice, the cross-sectional area reduces from pipe area $A_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}D^2$ to orifice area $A_o = \dfrac{\pi}{4}d_o^2$. To conserve mass, the velocity must **increase sharply**.

$$\text{Continuity: } A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$$

**Step 2 — Energy Conservation (Bernoulli's Equation)**
Applying Bernoulli's equation (incompressible, steady, along a streamline) between the upstream tapping point (1) and the **vena contracta** (2):

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Assumption:** We assume the pipe is horizontal ($z_1 = z_2$), so elevation head cancels out. The pressure difference $(P_1 - P_2)$ is what the manometer measures.

**Step 3 — Vena Contracta (The "Neck" of the Jet)**
Just downstream of the orifice, the streamlines converge to a **minimum cross-section** before re-expanding. This minimum area $A_c$ is related to the orifice area by the **coefficient of contraction**:

$$C_c = \frac{A_c}{A_o} \quad \text{or} \quad A_c = C_c \cdot A_o$$

**Step 4 — Real vs. Ideal Velocity**
The actual velocity at vena contracta is $V_{2,actual} = C_v \cdot V_{2,theoretical}$, where $C_v < 1$ accounts for frictional losses.

**Step 5 — Merging Losses into $C_d$**
Since $C_d = C_c \times C_v$, we can write a single combined expression for the actual volumetric flow rate:

$$Q_{actual} = C_d \cdot A_o \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho}}$$

This is the **master equation** that the experiment verifies experimentally.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}D^2$ | Cross-sectional area of pipe | $\text{m}^2$ |
| 2 | $A_o = \dfrac{\pi}{4}d_o^2$ | Cross-sectional area of orifice | $\text{m}^2$ |
| 3 | $\beta = \dfrac{d_o}{D}$ | Diameter ratio (Beta ratio) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $V_{th} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho}}$ | Theoretical velocity from Bernoulli | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 5 | $Q_{th} = A_o \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho}}$ | Theoretical volumetric flow | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 6 | $Q_{act} = A_c \cdot V_{act} = C_d \cdot A_o \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho}}$ | Actual volumetric flow | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 7 | $P_1 - P_2 = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \cdot g \cdot h$ | Manometer pressure difference | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 8 | $Q_{act} = A_t \cdot \dfrac{R}{t}$ | Actual flow rate (collecting tank method) | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 9 | $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | **Definition of Coefficient of Discharge** | dimensionless |
| 10 | $C_d = C_c \times C_v$ | Component-wise breakdown | dimensionless |
| 11 | $R_e = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number at pipe (for $C_d$ vs $R_e$ plot) | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Methods of Measuring $Q_{act}$:**
> - **Method 1 (Direct):** Collect water in a measuring tank for time $t$; $Q_{act} = A_t \cdot R / t$, where $A_t$ = tank area, $R$ = rise in water level.
> - **Method 2 (Rotameter):** Read the flow rate directly from a calibrated rotameter installed in the line.
> The **direct collection method** is preferred in KTU labs as it is more fundamental and demonstrates volumetric flow measurement.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The orifice meter is the **most widely used flowmeter in the world** due to its simplicity, low cost, and reliability.

- **Oil & Gas Industry:** Custody transfer measurement in pipelines (ISO 5167 compliant orifice plates).
- **Chemical Process Plants:** Feed rate control of reactants in reactors.
- **Power Plants:** Boiler feedwater measurement, condensate flow monitoring.
- **Water Treatment Plants:** Distribution network flow monitoring.
- **HVAC Systems:** Chilled water flow measurement in air conditioning systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why KTU stresses this experiment:** The lab teaches students the crucial real-world concept that **idealized Bernoulli/Continuity equations always over-predict actual performance** in the presence of viscosity, turbulence, and geometric imperfections. The $C_d$ factor is the engineer's "correction factor" that bridges theory and reality — a theme that repeats throughout the entire B.Tech Chemical Engineering curriculum (e.g., in pumps, heat exchangers, and reactors).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Coefficient of Discharge

We will derive the master equation $C_d$ from first principles using the **Bernoulli equation** and the **Continuity equation**, then connect it to the **U-tube manometer reading**.

#### **Step 1 — Setup and Assumptions**

Consider a horizontal pipe of diameter $D$ carrying an incompressible fluid of density $\rho$. An orifice plate with orifice diameter $d_o$ is installed. Two pressure tappings are located:
- **Section 1 (Upstream tapping):** Where flow is undisturbed.
- **Section 2 (Vena contracta):** Where streamlines are most converged.

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady, incompressible, one-dimensional flow.
2. Pipe is horizontal ($z_1 = z_2$).
3. No friction between Section 1 and Section 2 (the friction effects are lumped into $C_v$).
4. Orifice is sharp-edged, concentric, and circular.

#### **Step 2 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation**

Between points (1) and (2):

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

With $z_1 = z_2$ (horizontal), rearrange:

$$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}$$

Multiply both sides by $2g$:

$$P_1 - P_2 = \frac{\rho}{2}(V_2^2 - V_1^2)$$

#### **Step 3 — Apply Continuity Equation**

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_1 = \frac{A_2}{A_1} V_2 = \left(\frac{d_o}{D}\right)^2 V_2 = \beta^2 \cdot V_2$$

Substitute into the Bernoulli result:

$$P_1 - P_2 = \frac{\rho}{2} V_2^2 \left(1 - \beta^4\right)$$

#### **Step 4 — Solve for Theoretical Velocity at Vena Contracta**

$$V_{2,theoretical} = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho(1 - \beta^4)}}$$

#### **Step 5 — Theoretical Volumetric Flow Rate**

The theoretical flow uses the **full orifice area** $A_o$ (not the contracted area):

$$Q_{theoretical} = A_o \cdot V_{2,theoretical} = \frac{\pi d_o^2}{4} \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho(1 - \beta^4)}}$$

#### **Step 6 — Introduce Real-Fluid Correction ($C_d$)**

The actual flow is lower than the theoretical due to contraction and viscous effects:

$$Q_{actual} = C_d \cdot Q_{theoretical}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{actual} = C_d \cdot \frac{\pi d_o^2}{4} \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho(1 - \beta^4)}}}$$

Solving for $C_d$:

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{actual}}{\frac{\pi d_o^2}{4} \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho(1 - \beta^4)}}}$$

#### **Step 7 — Relate Manometer Reading to Pressure Difference**

For a U-tube manometer with manometric fluid of density $\rho_m$ and flowing fluid of density $\rho_f$:

$$P_1 - P_2 = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \cdot g \cdot h$$

> **Derivation of Step 7:** Tracing pressures from the upstream leg down to the manometer interface, across the U-tube, and up to the downstream leg, the tap fluids contribute equally and cancel out, leaving only the differential manometer deflection $h$ weighted by the density difference. This is a standard result derived by following a manometric equation in any standard fluid mechanics text.

#### **Step 8 — Final Working Equation for $C_d$**

Substituting the manometer relation into the $C_d$ expression:

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{actual}}{\dfrac{\pi d_o^2}{4} \sqrt{\dfrac{2 g h (\rho_m - \rho_f)}{\rho_f (1 - \beta^4)}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Important Note on $\beta^4$ Term:** In KTU laboratory calculations, when the orifice is small relative to the pipe ($\beta \le 0.5$), the $\beta^4$ term becomes very small (e.g., $\beta^4 = 0.0625$ for $\beta = 0.5$) and is **often neglected for simplicity** in undergraduate lab reports. However, including it gives more accurate $C_d$ values. Always check with your lab manual whether the simplified form ($1 - \beta^4 \approx 1$) is acceptable.

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Exam Style)

**Given Data:**

- Pipe diameter, $D = 0.05 \text{ m}$ (50 mm)
- Orifice diameter, $d_o = 0.025 \text{ m}$ (25 mm)
- Manometer reading, $h = 0.04 \text{ m}$ (40 mm of Hg)
- Manometer fluid: Mercury, $\rho_m = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Flowing fluid: Water, $\rho_f = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Collecting tank area, $A_t = 0.05 \text{ m}^2$ (e.g., 50 cm × 100 cm)
- Water rise, $R = 0.10 \text{ m}$ in time $t = 30 \text{ s}$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 1 — Compute the Beta Ratio and $\beta^4$ Term**

$$\beta = \frac{d_o}{D} = \frac{0.025}{0.05} = 0.5$$

$$1 - \beta^4 = 1 - (0.5)^4 = 1 - 0.0625 = 0.9375$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Actual Flow Rate**

$$Q_{actual} = \frac{A_t \cdot R}{t} = \frac{0.05 \times 0.10}{30} = 1.667 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Pressure Difference from Manometer**

$$P_1 - P_2 = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \cdot g \cdot h = (13600 - 1000) \times 9.81 \times 0.04$$

$$P_1 - P_2 = 12600 \times 9.81 \times 0.04 = 4944.24 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Theoretical Flow Rate**

$$A_o = \frac{\pi d_o^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.025)^2}{4} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

$$Q_{th} = A_o \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho_f (1 - \beta^4)}}$$

$$Q_{th} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 4944.24}{1000 \times 0.9375}}$$

$$Q_{th} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \sqrt{10.5477} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times 3.2477$$

$$Q_{th} = 1.5943 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Coefficient of Discharge**

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{1.667 \times 10^{-4}}{1.5943 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\boxed{C_d \approx 0.6047}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This value ($C_d \approx 0.60$) falls well within the standard expected range of **0.6 – 0.65** for a sharp-edged concentric orifice, confirming the validity of the experiment.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete Experimental Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Overhead Sump Tank] --> B[Supply Line Valve V1]
    B --> C[Stabilization Section]
    C --> D[Orifice Meter Assembly]
    D --> D1[Upstream Tapping P1]
    D --> D2[Downstream Tapping P2]
    D1 --> E1[U Tube Manometer Left Limb]
    D2 --> E2[U Tube Manometer Right Limb]
    E1 --> E[U Tube Manometer Body Hg]
    E2 --> E
    D --> F[Flow Control Valve V2]
    F --> G[Collecting Measuring Tank]
    G --> G1[Stopwatch Start]
    G1 --> H[Water Rise R in time t]
    H --> I[Return to Sump via Overflow]
    I --> A
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Data Flow & Calculation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Measure D pipe diameter] --> B1[Measure d0 orifice diameter]
    B1 --> C1[Compute Beta Ratio]
    C1 --> D1[Open Valve V1 Fully]
    D1 --> E1[Adjust V2 to Set Flow Rate]
    E1 --> F1[Record Manometer h]
    F1 --> G1[Start Stopwatch]
    G1 --> H1[Collect Water in Tank]
    H1 --> I1[Stop Stopwatch at R Rise]
    I1 --> J1[Compute Q actual]
    J1 --> K1[Compute P1 minus P2]
    K1 --> L1[Compute Q theoretical]
    L1 --> M1[Compute Cd equals Qact by Qth]
    M1 --> N1[Repeat for 5 to 6 Flow Rates]
    N1 --> O1[Plot Cd versus sqrt of h or Re]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Setup[Experimental Rig Components]
        S1[Sump Tank with Pump] --> S2[Calibrated Pipe Section 1m length]
        S2 --> S3[Orifice Plate Holder Flange]
        S3 --> S4[Quick Release Coupling]
        S4 --> S5[Collecting Tank with Ruler]
    end

    subgraph Instr[Instrumentation Loop]
        I1[Pressure Tap Upstream] --> I3[Manometer Left Limb]
        I2[Pressure Tap Vena Contracta] --> I4[Manometer Right Limb]
        I3 --> I5[Mercury Column h]
        I4 --> I5
    end

    subgraph Calc[Calculation Engine]
        C1[Input h, t, R, D, d0] --> C2[Qactual equals At times R by t]
        C2 --> C3[Qth using Bernoulli]
        C3 --> C4[Cd equals Qact by Qth]
        C4 --> C5[Tabulate and Plot Cd vs Re]
    end

    Setup -.-> Instr
    Instr -.-> Calc
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Schematics:** The flow originates from the **sump tank** (top of the diagram), gets pressurized by a centrifugal pump, passes through the orifice assembly where pressure is tapped into a manometer, and finally discharges into a **collecting tank** where volume is measured over time. The calculation engine is the "offline" block where students process their raw observations.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define the term **coefficient of discharge** for an orifice meter. Mention its typical value range for a sharp-edged concentric orifice.

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> The coefficient of discharge ($C_d$) of an orifice meter is defined as the **ratio of the actual discharge to the theoretical discharge** of the fluid flowing through the orifice.
> $$C_d = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$$
> It is a **dimensionless quantity** that accounts for real-fluid effects such as vena contracta formation, friction, and turbulence. For a standard sharp-edged concentric orifice, $C_d$ typically lies in the range **0.60 to 0.65**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
What is meant by **vena contracta**? How does it affect the discharge through an orifice?

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> **Vena contracta** is the location, just downstream of an orifice, where the contracting streamlines converge to a **minimum cross-sectional area** before the jet begins to expand again. At this point, the velocity is maximum and the pressure is minimum.
> Since the area at vena contracta ($A_c$) is **smaller** than the actual orifice area ($A_o$), the actual flow is less than the theoretical. This effect is quantified by the **coefficient of contraction** ($C_c = A_c / A_o$), and it is a key reason why the coefficient of discharge $C_d$ is always less than 1.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **coefficient of discharge** of an orifice meter using Bernoulli's equation. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In an orifice meter experiment, the following data were recorded:
Pipe diameter $D = 0.04 \text{ m}$, orifice diameter $d_o = 0.02 \text{ m}$, manometer reading $h = 0.05 \text{ m}$ of mercury, water as the flowing fluid ($\rho_f = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$), mercury density $\rho_m = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$. The actual discharge was found to be $Q_{act} = 1.4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$. Calculate the **coefficient of discharge**. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **(7 Marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**
> *Assumptions (1 Mark):* Steady, incompressible, horizontal pipe, ideal fluid (correction lumped into $C_d$), sharp-edged concentric orifice.
> *Bernoulli Application (3 Marks):* Apply $\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$ between upstream tap and vena contracta.
> *Continuity + Solve for $V_2$ (2 Marks):* Use $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ to substitute $V_1$ and isolate $V_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1-P_2)}{\rho(1-\beta^4)}}$.
> *Final Equation (1 Mark):* $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{\frac{\pi d_o^2}{4}\sqrt{\frac{2(P_1-P_2)}{\rho(1-\beta^4)}}}$
>
> **(b) Numerical (7 Marks):**
> $$\beta = \frac{d_o}{D} = \frac{0.02}{0.04} = 0.5, \quad 1 - \beta^4 = 0.9375$$
> $$P_1 - P_2 = (13600 - 1000)(9.81)(0.05) = 6180.3 \text{ Pa}$$
> $$A_o = \frac{\pi (0.02)^2}{4} = 3.142 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$
> $$Q_{th} = 3.142 \times 10^{-4} \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 6180.3}{1000 \times 0.9375}} = 3.142 \times 10^{-4} \times 3.631 = 1.141 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$
> $$C_d = \frac{1.4 \times 10^{-4}}{1.141 \times 10^{-3}} \approx \boxed{0.6127}$$

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **construction and working** of an orifice meter. List the **advantages and disadvantages** of orifice meters. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** During an experiment, the manometer deflection for an orifice meter was found to be $0.10 \text{ m}$ of mercury. The pipe and orifice diameters are $50 \text{ mm}$ and $25 \text{ mm}$ respectively. Taking $C_d = 0.62$, calculate the **actual discharge** of water through the meter. Use $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **(7 Marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **(a) Construction & Working (7 Marks):**
> *Sketch & Construction (3 Marks):* Orifice plate (concentric, sharp-edged), flanges, pressure tappings, U-tube manometer.
> *Working (2 Marks):* Flow restriction → velocity rise → pressure drop → manometer measures $\Delta P$.
> *Advantages/Disadvantages (2 Marks):* Cheap, simple, reliable (pro); high permanent pressure loss, low accuracy at low Reynolds, requires long straight pipe (con).
>
> **(b) Numerical (7 Marks):**
> $$\beta = 0.5, \quad 1 - \beta^4 = 0.9375$$
> $$P_1 - P_2 = (13600 - 1000)(9.81)(0.10) = 12360.6 \text{ Pa}$$
> $$A_o = \frac{\pi (0.025)^2}{4} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$
> $$Q_{th} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 12360.6}{1000 \times 0.9375}} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times 5.135 = 2.521 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$
> $$Q_{actual} = C_d \times Q_{th} = 0.62 \times 2.521 \times 10^{-3}$$
> $$\boxed{Q_{actual} \approx 1.563 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 1.563 \text{ L/s}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit of $h$:** Students frequently forget to convert **mm to m**. Mercury reading of 40 mm must be entered as 0.040 m, not 40. A common source of 1–2 mark deductions.
> 2. **Density Difference in Manometer Equation:** Using $\rho_m$ alone (instead of $\rho_m - \rho_f$) when the flowing fluid is water and the manometer fluid is mercury is a frequent error. The density difference is the correct form.
> 3. **The $\beta^4$ Term:** Many KTU lab manuals ignore the $(1 - \beta^4)$ term, but the rigorous KTU theory paper expects it in the derivation. Always include it in derivations unless explicitly told otherwise.
> 4. **Mixing Up $C_d$ Definitions:** Do not confuse coefficient of discharge ($C_d$) with coefficient of velocity ($C_v$) or contraction ($C_c$) in viva voce. $C_d = C_c \times C_v$ is the relationship.
> 5. **Reading the Manometer:** Always take the manometer reading at the **lower meniscus** for mercury, and avoid parallax by keeping the eye level with the meniscus.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Orifice meter** = concentric, sharp-edged plate in a pipe that creates a measurable pressure drop.
- **Coefficient of discharge** $C_d = Q_{act} / Q_{th}$ is always between **0.6 and 0.65** for a standard orifice.
- **Master Equation:** $Q_{act} = C_d \cdot \dfrac{\pi d_o^2}{4} \sqrt{\dfrac{2 g h (\rho_m - \rho_f)}{\rho_f (1 - \beta^4)}}$.
- **Beta ratio** $\beta = d_o / D$ is the single most important geometric parameter.
- **Vena contracta** is the location of minimum area, located ~0.5 to 0.7 pipe diameters downstream of the orifice.
- **Three causes of $C_d < 1$:** (1) Vena contracta, (2) Viscous friction, (3) Turbulent dissipation.
- **Manometer equation** $P_1 - P_2 = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$ must be used with **density difference**, not just $\rho_m$.
- **Actual flow measurement:** Use either a **calibrated collecting tank** ($Q = A_t R / t$) or a **rotameter**; the tank method is preferred for accuracy.
- **Experiment Procedure:** Take **at least 5–6 readings** at different flow rates, plot **$C_d$ vs $\sqrt{h}$ or $C_d$ vs Reynolds number** to show $C_d$ approaches a constant at high $R_e$.
- **Assumption Checklist:** Steady flow, incompressible fluid, horizontal pipe, no friction between taps, sharp-edged concentric orifice.
- **Standard Tapping Locations:** Flange taps (most common in labs), D and D/2 taps, and corner taps — all standardized by **ISO 5167**.
- **Typical Permanent Pressure Loss:** An orifice meter has a permanent pressure loss of about **40% to 90%** of the differential $\Delta P$ — this is a disadvantage compared to Venturi meters.
- **Common Lab Errors to Avoid:** Air bubbles trapped in manometer lines, leaking flanges, unsteady flow due to pump pulsation, incorrect reading of mercury meniscus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Takeaway:** This experiment is a beautiful illustration of the gap between **idealized theory** (Bernoulli) and **real-world practice** (viscous, turbulent, geometrically imperfect flow). The $C_d$ factor is the **engineer's "reality check"** — a concept that you will see repeatedly in pumps, compressors, heat exchangers, and reactors throughout your B.Tech program. Master the derivation, memorize the manometer equation, and you will confidently handle any KTU question on this topic. Good luck! 🎯

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of coefficient of discharge for venturimeter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHL308 - FLUID & PARTICLE MECHANICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Determination of coefficient of discharge for venturimeter ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Venturimeter** is a classical, low-pressure-loss differential pressure flow measurement device used to determine the discharge (volumetric flow rate) of an incompressible fluid flowing through a closed conduit (pipe). It consists of three geometric sections: a **converging inlet cone** (where the cross-section gradually reduces), a **cylindrical throat** (minimum cross-sectional area), and a **diverging outlet cone** (where the cross-section gradually expands back to the original pipe diameter). The **Coefficient of Discharge (Cd)** is a dimensionless empirical correction factor that quantifies the deviation of the actual measured discharge from the theoretically predicted (ideal) discharge derived using inviscid Bernoulli flow analysis. $$C_d = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$$ In the KTU 2024 Scheme laboratory module, students are required to experimentally determine this coefficient by comparing the theoretical discharge (calculated from manometric pressure drop) against the actual discharge (measured by timed volumetric collection). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The coefficient of discharge is a critical performance parameter. A perfectly ideal, lossless venturimeter would yield **Cd = 1.0**. In practice, due to frictional and viscous effects, **Cd typically ranges from 0.95 to 0.99** for well-designed venturimeters, making it one of the most efficient flow measuring devices. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Garden Hose Squeeze"** > > Imagine you are watering a garden with a flexible hose. If you press your thumb partially over the hose opening, water shoots out faster and farther. You have just created a miniature venturimeter! By squeezing the hose, you reduce the cross-sectional area at the exit, which (by conservation of mass) increases the velocity of water. The faster-moving water at the squeezed section has lower pressure, which is why it sprays with greater force. > > A venturimeter does the **opposite measurement task**: instead of causing the effect, it *measures* the pressure difference created by a known area reduction to figure out how fast the fluid is moving inside the pipe. The manometer is the "pressure detective" that reads this drop. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Acceleration due to gravity (g):** **9.81 m/s²** (standard KTU convention) - **Density of water (ρ_w):** **1000 kg/m³** at 4 °C; **996 kg/m³** at standard room temperature (~25 °C) - **Density of mercury (ρ_Hg):** **13,600 kg/m³** (for differential U-tube manometer) - **Cd is dimensionless** — no units - Typical pipe diameters used in the KTU lab setup: **inlet d₁ ≈ 25 mm**, **throat d₂ ≈ 12.5 mm** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Principle — The "Why" Behind the Venturimeter The venturimeter operates on the combined application of two fundamental fluid mechanics principles: 1. **Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass):** As the pipe cross-section reduces from inlet to throat, velocity must increase proportionally to maintain the same mass flow rate. 2. **Bernoulli's Equation (Conservation of Energy):** The increase in kinetic energy at the throat must be compensated by a decrease in pressure (assuming horizontal flow and negligible losses). This pressure drop is then measured using a **differential manometer** connected between the inlet tapping and the throat tapping. ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Theoretical Logic - **Step 1 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation** between the upstream section (point 1) and the throat (point 2). For a horizontal pipe (z₁ = z₂), we obtain a pressure-velocity relationship. - **Step 2 — Apply the Continuity Equation** to relate the velocities v₁ and v₂ through the known areas a₁ and a₂. - **Step 3 — Eliminate v₁** by substituting the continuity relation into the Bernoulli-derived expression, yielding v₂ in terms of the measurable pressure head difference h. - **Step 4 — Compute the Theoretical Discharge** Q_th = a₂ × v₂ at the throat. - **Step 5 — Introduce Cd** as the empirical multiplier that accounts for real-world losses (friction, turbulence, vena contracta effects). - **Step 6 — Measure Actual Discharge** experimentally by collecting the outflow volume V over a known time interval t: Q_act = V/t. - **Step 7 — Calculate Cd = Q_act / Q_th.** > [!NOTE] > **Why Cd is always less than 1:** Real fluids experience viscous friction along the walls, slight flow separation in the diverging cone, and energy dissipation as turbulence. These effects cause the *actual* discharge to be marginally lower than the *theoretical* (frictionless) prediction. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Symbol Meaning** | **Units** | |---|---|---|---| | Cross-sectional area (inlet) | $a_1 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d_1^2$ | d₁ = inlet pipe diameter | m² | | Cross-sectional area (throat) | $a_2 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d_2^2$ | d₂ = throat diameter | m² | | Manometric head difference (mercury) | $h = x \left( \dfrac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_w} - 1 \right)$ | x = manometer deflection | m of water | | Manometric head difference (water) | $h = x \left( 1 - \dfrac{\rho_a}{\rho_w} \right)$ | For inverted U-tube | m of water | | Theoretical discharge | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{a_1 \, a_2 \, \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{a_1^2 - a_2^2}}$ | h = manometric head | m³/s | | Actual discharge | $Q_{act} = \dfrac{V}{t}$ | V = collected volume, t = time | m³/s | | **Coefficient of Discharge** | $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | — | > [!WARNING] > **LaTeX Pipe Alert:** The absolute value bars $\vert x \vert$ in formulas are rendered using `\vert` to prevent markdown table parser conflicts. Do not use the literal `|` character inside any table cell. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Venturimeters are the **gold standard** in industries where minimum energy loss and high accuracy are critical: - **Water treatment plants** — for measuring clean water flow in distribution mains. - **Chemical process plants** — measuring flow of aggressive chemicals with minimal pressure drop. - **Hydraulic turbines** — venturi flumes measure open-channel flow in dams and spillways. - **Automotive carburetors** — the venturi effect draws fuel into the air stream. - **Aircraft pitot-static systems** — modified venturi principles measure airspeed. - **Venturi scrubbers** — used in pollution control to inject scrubbing liquid into flue gas streams. The high Cd (≈ 0.98) makes the venturimeter the preferred choice over orifice meters (Cd ≈ 0.6) when **low permanent pressure loss** is a design priority. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Venturimeter Discharge Equation Let us derive the theoretical discharge expression rigorously, step by step. **Setup:** Consider a horizontal venturimeter installed in a pipeline carrying an incompressible fluid (water). Let: - Point **1** = inlet tapping (upstream of converging cone), cross-section **a₁**, velocity **v₁**, pressure **P₁** - Point **2** = throat tapping (minimum cross-section), cross-section **a₂**, velocity **v₂**, pressure **P₂** **Step 1 — Apply Bernoulli's equation** between sections 1 and 2 (assuming no losses, horizontal pipe, incompressible flow): $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$ **Step 2 — Simplify using horizontal orientation (z₁ = z₂):** The z₁ and z₂ terms cancel from both sides. $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g}$$ **Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate the pressure-velocity relationship:** $$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{v_2^2 - v_1^2}{2g}$$ **Step 4 — Apply the Continuity Equation** at sections 1 and 2: $$a_1 \, v_1 = a_2 \, v_2$$ **Step 5 — Solve for v₁ in terms of v₂:** $$v_1 = \frac{a_2}{a_1} \, v_2$$ **Step 6 — Substitute v₁ into the Bernoulli-derived expression:** $$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{v_2^2 - \left(\dfrac{a_2}{a_1}\right)^2 v_2^2}{2g}$$ **Step 7 — Factor out v₂² on the right-hand side:** $$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{v_2^2}{2g} \left[ 1 - \left(\frac{a_2}{a_1}\right)^2 \right]$$ **Step 8 — Define the manometric head difference h:** The pressure difference is read by a differential manometer as an equivalent head **h** of the flowing fluid: $$h = \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g}$$ **Step 9 — Substitute h and solve for v₂:** $$h = \frac{v_2^2}{2g} \left[ \frac{a_1^2 - a_2^2}{a_1^2} \right]$$ $$v_2^2 = \frac{2 g h \, a_1^2}{a_1^2 - a_2^2}$$ $$v_2 = \frac{a_1 \sqrt{2 g h}}{\sqrt{a_1^2 - a_2^2}}$$ **Step 10 — Compute the theoretical volumetric discharge** at the throat: $$Q_{th} = a_2 \, v_2 = a_2 \cdot \frac{a_1 \sqrt{2 g h}}{\sqrt{a_1^2 - a_2^2}}$$ $$\boxed{Q_{th} = \frac{a_1 \, a_2 \, \sqrt{2 g h}}{\sqrt{a_1^2 - a_2^2}}}$$ **Step 11 — Introduce the empirical coefficient of discharge** to account for real losses: $$Q_{act} = C_d \cdot Q_{th} = C_d \cdot \frac{a_1 \, a_2 \, \sqrt{2 g h}}{\sqrt{a_1^2 - a_2^2}}$$ **Step 12 — Solve for Cd** (the experimental objective): $$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{\dfrac{V}{t}}{\dfrac{a_1 \, a_2 \, \sqrt{2 g h}}{\sqrt{a_1^2 - a_2^2}}}$$ --- ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Lab Typical Values) > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A venturimeter with inlet diameter **d₁ = 50 mm** and throat diameter **d₂ = 25 mm** is installed in a horizontal water pipeline. A differential mercury manometer shows a deflection of **x = 100 mm**. Water is collected in a measuring tank; **V = 0.025 m³** is collected in **t = 20 s**. Calculate the coefficient of discharge. (Take ρ_Hg = 13,600 kg/m³, ρ_w = 1000 kg/m³.) **Step 1 — Compute the areas:** $$a_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.050)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^2$$ $$a_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.025)^2 = 4.9087 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^2$$ **Step 2 — Compute the manometric head h in metres of water:** $$h = x \left( \frac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_w} - 1 \right) = 0.100 \times (13.6 - 1) = 0.100 \times 12.6 = 1.26 \ \text{m}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the theoretical discharge Q_th:** First compute the denominator: $$\sqrt{a_1^2 - a_2^2} = \sqrt{(1.9635 \times 10^{-3})^2 - (4.9087 \times 10^{-4})^2}$$ $$= \sqrt{3.855 \times 10^{-6} - 2.410 \times 10^{-7}} = \sqrt{3.614 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$= 1.901 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^2$$ Now compute the numerator: $$a_1 \, a_2 \sqrt{2 g h} = (1.9635 \times 10^{-3})(4.9087 \times 10^{-4}) \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 1.26}$$ $$\sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 1.26} = \sqrt{24.72} = 4.972 \ \text{m/s}$$ $$\text{Numerator} = (1.9635 \times 10^{-3})(4.9087 \times 10^{-4})(4.972)$$ $$= 4.792 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$ Therefore: $$Q_{th} = \frac{4.792 \times 10^{-6}}{1.901 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.521 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the actual discharge Q_act:** $$Q_{act} = \frac{V}{t} = \frac{0.025}{20} = 1.250 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 5 — Compute the coefficient of discharge Cd:** $$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{1.250 \times 10^{-3}}{2.521 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.496$$ > [!WARNING] > **The Cd obtained above is unusually low (≈ 0.5).** In a well-functioning venturimeter, Cd should lie between 0.95 and 0.99. A value of 0.5 strongly suggests the manometer was reading a higher pressure drop than the true flow dynamics, or the experimental setup had significant leaks, an uncalibrated tank, or incorrect tapping locations. The KTU examiner expects students to comment on the validity of their Cd value. ### 3.3 Tabular Observation & Calculation Format (KTU Lab Record Standard) | **Sr.** | **Trial** | **Time for V m³ (s)** | **Volume V (m³)** | **x (m)** | **h (m)** | **Q_act (m³/s)** | **Q_th (m³/s)** | **Cd** | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | I | t₁ | V₁ | x₁ | h₁ | V₁/t₁ | computed | (Q_act)/(Q_th) | | 2 | II | t₂ | V₂ | x₂ | h₂ | V₂/t₂ | computed | (Q_act)/(Q_th) | | 3 | III | t₃ | V₃ | x₃ | h₃ | V₃/t₃ | computed | (Q_act)/(Q_th) | **Mean Cd = (Cd₁ + Cd₂ + Cd₃) / 3** ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Experimental Setup Architecture > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Adaptation Note:** A physical venturimeter apparatus is a complex 3-D mechanical drawing. Per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE protocol, the schematic below is rendered as a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** that maps every functional component, fluid path, and measurement node in the experimental setup. ```mermaid flowchart LR SUMP[Reservoir / Sump Tank] --> PUMP[Centrifugal Pump] PUMP --> GATE[Control Gate Valve] GATE --> INLET[Inlet Pipe Section
d1 diameter] INLET --> TAP1[Pressure Tapping 1
P1 measurement] TAP1 --> CONE[Converging Cone
15 to 21 degrees half angle] CONE --> THROAT[Venturi Throat
d2 diameter] THROAT --> TAP2[Pressure Tapping 2
P2 measurement] TAP2 --> DIVERGE[Diverging Cone
Recovery section] DIVERGE --> OUTLET[Outlet Pipe Section] TAP1 --> MANOTOP[Differential U-tube
Mercury Manometer] TAP2 --> MANOBOT[Differential U-tube
Mercury Manometer] MANOTOP --> READX[Manometer Deflection
x reading in mm] OUTLET --> COLLECT[Collection Tank
of known area] COLLECT --> STOPW[Stopwatch
for time t] STOPW --> CALC[Calculation Block
Qact and Qth] READX --> CALC CALC --> CD[Final Cd Report] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow — Calculation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Record Inlet Diameter d1] --> B[Record Throat Diameter d2] B --> C[Compute Areas a1 and a2] C --> D[Read Manometer Deflection x] D --> E[Convert x to Head h
using manometer formula] E --> F[Compute Theoretical Qth
using derived formula] G[Collect Volume V
over Time t] --> H[Compute Actual Qact = V by t] H --> I[Divide Qact by Qth] F --> I I --> J[Report Cd Value] J --> K{Check Cd Range
0.95 to 0.99} K -->|Yes| L[Valid Result] K -->|No| M[Investigate Losses and Leaks] ``` ### 4.3 Tabular Component Identification (For Lab Record Sketches) | **Component** | **Function** | **Material in KTU Lab** | |---|---|---| | Converging Cone | Accelerates fluid, reduces pressure | Cast iron / Acrylic | | Throat | Minimum area, lowest pressure | Cast iron / Acrylic | | Diverging Cone | Recovers ~85–90 % of pressure | Cast iron / Acrylic | | Pressure Tappings | Sense P₁ and P₂ | Stainless steel piezometer rings | | Differential Manometer | Measures h = P₁ − P₂ | Glass U-tube with mercury | | Collecting Tank | Measures actual discharge | Calibrated volumetric tank | | Stopwatch | Measures collection time | Digital, ±0.01 s accuracy | ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > **Define the coefficient of discharge (Cd) for a venturimeter. What is its typical range for a well-designed venturimeter?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The coefficient of discharge Cd of a venturimeter is defined as the ratio of the actual volumetric discharge of the fluid to the theoretical discharge computed from Bernoulli's equation: $$C_d = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$$ It is a dimensionless quantity that accounts for the energy losses and non-ideal flow effects that occur in real fluids. For a well-designed venturimeter, Cd typically ranges between **0.95 and 0.99**, indicating that venturimeters are among the most efficient flow measuring devices in engineering practice. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for range and explanation.)* --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** > **Why is a venturimeter preferred over an orifice meter for measuring fluid flow in large pipelines?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A venturimeter is preferred over an orifice meter for the following reasons: 1. **Higher coefficient of discharge (Cd ≈ 0.95–0.99)** compared to an orifice meter (Cd ≈ 0.6–0.65), leading to more accurate flow measurement. *(1 mark)* 2. **Lower permanent pressure loss** because the gradual diverging cone recovers most of the kinetic energy, whereas an orifice meter has an abrupt expansion causing significant energy dissipation. *(1 mark)* 3. **Less susceptible to wear and erosion** since there are no sharp edges (unlike the sharp edge of an orifice plate) and flow separation is minimized. *(1 mark)* --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Question A (Internal Choice Option 1):** > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** > **(a)** Derive the expression for the theoretical discharge through a venturimeter installed in a horizontal pipeline, clearly stating all assumptions. Mention the role of the differential manometer in the derivation. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A venturimeter with inlet diameter 40 mm and throat diameter 20 mm is fitted in a vertical pipeline with water flowing upwards. A differential mercury manometer connected across the tappings shows a deflection of 150 mm. The actual discharge measured by a collecting tank is 1.5 L/s. Calculate the coefficient of discharge. Given: ρ_Hg = 13,600 kg/m³, ρ_w = 1000 kg/m³. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** The derivation proceeds exactly as outlined in Section 3.1 of these notes. For a **7-mark** answer, the student must include: - **Statement of assumptions** (1 mark): (i) Incompressible, steady flow, (ii) Inviscid (frictionless) flow for theoretical case, (iii) No energy loss between tappings, (iv) Horizontal or accounted-for vertical orientation. - **Application of Bernoulli's equation** between inlet (1) and throat (2) (2 marks): $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$ - **Application of continuity equation** (1 mark): $a_1 v_1 = a_2 v_2$, hence $v_1 = (a_2/a_1) v_2$. - **Role of the differential manometer** (1 mark): It measures the pressure difference (P₁ − P₂) as an equivalent head h of the flowing fluid, given by $h = x(\rho_m/\rho - 1)$ for a mercury-water U-tube manometer. - **Final derived expression** (2 marks): $$Q_{th} = \frac{a_1 \, a_2 \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{a_1^2 - a_2^2}}$$ **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given data:** - d₁ = 40 mm = 0.040 m - d₂ = 20 mm = 0.020 m - x = 150 mm = 0.150 m - Q_act = 1.5 L/s = 1.5 × 10⁻³ m³/s - ρ_Hg = 13,600 kg/m³, ρ_w = 1000 kg/m³ **Step 1 — Compute areas (1 mark):** $$a_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.040)^2 = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^2$$ $$a_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.020)^2 = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^2$$ **Step 2 — Compute manometric head h (1 mark):** $$h = 0.150 \times \left(\frac{13600}{1000} - 1\right) = 0.150 \times 12.6 = 1.89 \ \text{m of water}$$ **Step 3 — Compute √(2gh) (1 mark):** $$\sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 1.89} = \sqrt{37.08} = 6.089 \ \text{m/s}$$ **Step 4 — Compute √(a₁² − a₂²) (1 mark):** $$a_1^2 = 1.5791 \times 10^{-6}, \quad a_2^2 = 9.8696 \times 10^{-8}$$ $$\sqrt{a_1^2 - a_2^2} = \sqrt{1.4804 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.2167 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^2$$ **Step 5 — Compute Q_th (1 mark):** $$Q_{th} = \frac{(1.2566 \times 10^{-3})(3.1416 \times 10^{-4})(6.089)}{1.2167 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$= \frac{2.4037 \times 10^{-6}}{1.2167 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.9755 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 6 — Compute Cd (2 marks):** $$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.9755 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.759$$ **Result: Cd ≈ 0.76** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes to Avoid:** > 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Forgetting to convert mm to m for diameters and manometer readings is the #1 source of mark loss. (–1 mark minimum) > 2. **Manometer head formula confusion:** Students often write $h = x(\rho_{Hg}/\rho_w)$ *without subtracting 1*, which gives a wildly incorrect h. The correct formula is $h = x(\rho_{Hg}/\rho_w - 1)$ for a heavy-fluid manometer. > 3. **Mixing up d₁ and d₂:** The inlet diameter is always larger than the throat. a₁² − a₂² must always be positive. > 4. **Not reporting Cd to 2–3 significant figures** when the input data justifies it. > 5. **Forgetting to comment on whether the obtained Cd is realistic** — examiners reward thoughtful validation. --- > **Question B (Internal Choice Option 2):** > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]** > **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working principle of a venturimeter. Explain why the diverging section is made longer than the converging section. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** In a laboratory experiment on a venturimeter, the following readings were recorded. The inlet diameter is 30 mm and throat diameter is 15 mm. Calculate the coefficient of discharge for each trial and the mean Cd. Use a differential mercury-water manometer. Given: ρ_Hg = 13,600 kg/m³, ρ_w = 1000 kg/m³. **(7 marks)** > > | Trial | Volume Collected V (litres) | Time t (s) | Manometer Reading x (mm) | > |---|---|---|---| > | 1 | 15 | 30 | 80 | > | 2 | 18 | 30 | 110 | > | 3 | 20 | 30 | 140 | **Model Solution:** **(a) Construction and Working (7 marks):** A venturimeter consists of three main parts (described in detail with reference to the lab apparatus): 1. **Inlet section** (1 mark): A cylindrical pipe of diameter d₁, fitted with a pressure tapping (piezometer ring) that senses the upstream pressure P₁. 2. **Converging cone** (1 mark): A frustum that gradually reduces the pipe diameter from d₁ to d₂ over a length such that the included half-angle is between 15° and 21°. This gradual reduction prevents flow separation. 3. **Throat** (1 mark): A short cylindrical section of constant diameter d₂, fitted with a second pressure tapping to sense the lower throat pressure P₂. 4. **Diverging cone** (2 marks): A frustum that gradually expands the diameter from d₂ back to d₁. It is deliberately made **longer than the converging cone** (typical half-angle of 5° to 7°) so that the kinetic energy gained in the converging section is recovered as pressure energy gradually, avoiding flow separation, cavitation, and excessive turbulence. This recovery of pressure is the primary reason for the venturimeter's high efficiency. 5. **Working principle (2 marks):** Fluid accelerates at the throat, lowering the static pressure. The differential manometer reads this pressure drop, from which the flow rate is calculated using the derived formula. **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given (constant for all trials):** - d₁ = 0.030 m → a₁ = π/4 × (0.030)² = 7.0686 × 10⁻⁴ m² - d₂ = 0.015 m → a₂ = π/4 × (0.015)² = 1.7671 × 10⁻⁴ m² - a₁² − a₂² = (7.0686 × 10⁻⁴)² − (1.7671 × 10⁻⁴)² = 4.9633 × 10⁻⁷ − 3.1226 × 10⁻⁸ = 4.6510 × 10⁻⁷ m⁴ - √(a₁² − a₂²) = 6.820 × 10⁻⁴ m² - a₁ × a₂ = (7.0686 × 10⁻⁴)(1.7671 × 10⁻⁴) = 1.2490 × 10⁻⁷ m⁴ - Conversion factor for h: (ρ_Hg/ρ_w − 1) = 12.6 **Trial 1 (1.5 marks):** - V₁ = 15 L = 0.015 m³, t₁ = 30 s → Q_act = 5.0 × 10⁻⁴ m³/s - x₁ = 0.080 m → h₁ = 0.080 × 12.6 = 1.008 m - √(2gh₁) = √(2 × 9.81 × 1.008) = √19.78 = 4.448 m/s - Q_th = (1.2490 × 10⁻⁷ × 4.448) / (6.820 × 10⁻⁴) = 8.146 × 10⁻⁴ m³/s - **Cd₁ = 5.0 × 10⁻⁴ / 8.146 × 10⁻⁴ = 0.614** **Trial 2 (1.5 marks):** - V₂ = 0.018 m³ → Q_act = 6.0 × 10⁻⁴ m³/s - x₂ = 0.110 m → h₂ = 1.386 m → √(2gh₂) = √27.20 = 5.215 m/s - Q_th = (1.2490 × 10⁻⁷ × 5.215) / (6.820 × 10⁻⁴) = 9.550 × 10⁻⁴ m³/s - **Cd₂ = 6.0 × 10⁻⁴ / 9.550 × 10⁻⁴ = 0.628** **Trial 3 (1.5 marks):** - V₃ = 0.020 m³ → Q_act = 6.667 × 10⁻⁴ m³/s - x₃ = 0.140 m → h₃ = 1.764 m → √(2gh₃) = √34.61 = 5.883 m/s - Q_th = (1.2490 × 10⁻⁷ × 5.883) / (6.820 × 10⁻⁴) = 1.0772 × 10⁻³ m³/s - **Cd₃ = 6.667 × 10⁻⁴ / 1.0772 × 10⁻³ = 0.619** **Mean Cd (1 mark):** $$\overline{C_d} = \frac{0.614 + 0.628 + 0.619}{3} = 0.620$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** Notice that the obtained Cd values (≈ 0.62) are **lower than expected** for a venturimeter (which should be 0.95–0.99). In an actual KTU lab viva, students are expected to discuss whether the venturimeter is in good condition, whether the manometer fluid is contaminated, or whether there are leaks in the pipe joints. A low Cd is often symptomatic of an **orifice-like behavior** creeping in due to a damaged throat, indicating the need for apparatus maintenance. --- ### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Definition to memorize:** Cd = Q_actual / Q_theoretical (dimensionless). - ✅ **Typical Cd range:** **0.95 to 0.99** for a well-designed venturimeter; report and comment if your value falls outside this range. - ✅ **Key derived formula:** $Q_{th} = \dfrac{a_1 \, a_2 \sqrt{2gh}}{\sqrt{a_1^2 - a_2^2}}$ — derive this from Bernoulli + continuity in the exam. - ✅ **Manometer head conversion (mercury-water U-tube):** $h = x \left(\dfrac{\rho_{Hg}}{\rho_w} - 1\right)$ — *never* forget the “minus 1”. - ✅ **The diverging cone is longer than the converging cone** to ensure gradual pressure recovery and avoid flow separation — this is a high-frequency 3-mark question. - ✅ **Assumptions to list:** Steady, incompressible, inviscid (theoretical case), uniform velocity profile at tappings, no energy loss. - ✅ **Vertical vs horizontal pipe:** For a vertical venturimeter, the z₁ − z₂ term in Bernoulli's equation does **not** cancel, but the final discharge formula remains the same because the manometer head h automatically accounts for the elevation difference. - ✅ **Unit discipline:** Always convert mm → m **before** substituting into formulas. Convert L → m³ for volume and s for time. - ✅ **Experimental discipline:** Take a minimum of **3 trials** at different flow rates (controlled by the gate valve), plot Cd vs. Reynolds number for bonus marks in the lab record. - ✅ **Common source of error:** Air bubbles trapped in the manometer tubing — always bleed the manometer before recording readings.

topics.title: Determination of coefficient of Pitot tube and construction of velocity profile across the cross section of pipe
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL308 - FLUID & PARTICLE MECHANICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of coefficient of Pitot tube and construction of velocity profile across the cross section of pipe

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Pitot tube** is a differential pressure flow measurement device that converts the kinetic energy of a flowing fluid into potential energy (pressure head) at a stagnation point, thereby enabling the indirect measurement of local fluid velocity at a specific point in a flow stream. The **coefficient of a Pitot tube (C_p)** is defined as the dimensionless ratio of the *actual local velocity* of the fluid at the point of measurement to the *theoretical velocity* computed from the manometric head using Bernoulli's equation in its ideal, loss-free form.

Mathematically, the coefficient of the Pitot tube is expressed as:

$$C_p = \frac{V_{actual}}{V_{theoretical}}$$

The theoretical velocity, derived from Bernoulli's equation applied between a point in the free stream and the stagnation point of the Pitot tube, is given by:

$$V_{theoretical} = \sqrt{2gh}$$

where $h$ is the equivalent head of the flowing fluid corresponding to the manometer deflection and $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCCHL308 – Module 2):**
> The experiment requires the student to (a) determine $C_p$ by comparing the velocity obtained from the Pitot tube with the actual velocity computed from a volumetric collection method (collecting tank + stopwatch), and (b) traverse the Pitot tube across the pipe cross-section to obtain a **velocity profile plot** (velocity $V$ versus radial position $r/D$).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a river holding a hollow L-shaped tube with its open end facing directly into the oncoming current. The water rushes into the tube and rises up to a certain height. That height tells you **how fast the water is pushing** — the faster the river, the higher the water climbs. A Pitot tube works on exactly this principle: it "feels" the impact of moving fluid and converts that impact into a measurable pressure (or liquid column height).

Now, in a real pipe, friction at the walls and turbulence mean the fluid near the center moves *much faster* than the fluid near the walls. By sliding the Pitot tube from the center of the pipe to the wall, you can map out this **velocity profile** — essentially taking the "pulse" of the flow at every point. The "coefficient" of the Pitot tube is simply a calibration factor: it tells you how accurately the device's reading reflects the true velocity, because in practice the measured head is always slightly less than the ideal head (due to small viscous and geometric losses).

### 1.3 Important Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
