The superficial velocity is $u_s = 0.025$ m/s. Calculate the pressure drop using the Ergun equation and verify the dominant flow regime. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Procedure — 7 Marks:**

1. **Apparatus Setup (2 Marks):** A vertical glass column of known diameter $D_c$ is packed uniformly with glass spheres of known diameter $d_p$ to a measured bed height $L$. Water from a sump is pumped through a rotameter (for flow measurement), a control valve, and enters at the bottom of the column. A **U-tube differential manometer** (mercury–water) is connected between the inlet and outlet pressure tappings.

2. **Procedure (3 Marks):**
   - Ensure the bed is **uniformly packed** with no air pockets (tap the column gently).
   - Open the control valve fully, then gradually reduce flow to flush out trapped air from manometer lines.
   - Set a flow rate on the rotameter, wait for steady state, and record the **manometer reading $h$** (difference in mercury levels).
   - Repeat for **at least 5–6 different flow rates** spanning the laminar-to-turbulent range.

3. **Observations and Formulae (2 Marks):**
   - Record: $D_c$, $L$, $d_p$, $Q$ (or rotameter reading), $h$, fluid temperature.
   - Calculate:
     $$u_s = \frac{Q}{A_c}, \quad \Delta P_{exp} = (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_{w}) g h, \quad Re_p = \frac{d_p u_s \rho}{\mu}, \quad f_p = \frac{\Delta P}{L} \cdot \frac{d_p}{\rho u_s^2} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon^3}{1-\varepsilon}$$
   - **Verify** by plotting $f_p$ vs $Re_p$ on log-log paper and overlaying the Ergun curve $f_p = 150/Re_p + 1.75$.

---

**(b) Calculation — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Compute Viscous Term**

$$\left(\frac{\Delta P}{L}\right)_{visc} = \frac{150 \times (0.89 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.025}{(4 \times 10^{-3})^{2}} \times \frac{(1-0.42)^{2}}{(0.42)^{3}}$$

$$= \frac{150 \times 0.89 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.025}{1.6 \times 10^{-5}} \times \frac{0.3364}{0.074088}$$

$$= \frac{3.3375 \times 10^{-3}}{1.6 \times 10^{-5}} \times 4.540$$

$$= 208.59 \times 4.540 = 947.0 \text{ Pa/m}$$

**Step 2: Compute Inertial Term**

$$\left(\frac{\Delta P}{L}\right)_{inert} = \frac{1.75 \times 997 \times (0.025)^{2}}{4 \times 10^{-3}} \times \frac{(1-0.42)}{(0.42)^{3}}$$

$$= \frac{1.75 \times 997 \times 6.25 \times 10^{-4}}{4 \times 10^{-3}} \times \frac{0.58}{0.074088}$$

$$= \frac{1.090}{4 \times 10^{-3}} \times 7.829$$

$$= 272.5 \times 7.829 = 2133.7 \text{ Pa/m}$$

**Step 3: Total Pressure Drop Gradient**

$$\left(\frac{\Delta P}{L}\right)_{total} = 947.0 + 2133.7 = 3080.7 \text{ Pa/m}$$

**Step 4: Total Pressure Drop**

$$\boxed{\Delta P = 3080.7 \times 0.5 = 1540.4 \text{ Pa} \approx 1.54 \text{ kPa}}$$

**Step 5: Verify Flow Regime**

$$Re_p = \frac{d_p u_s \rho}{\mu} = \frac{(4 \times 10^{-3})(0.025)(997)}{0.89 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.0997}{0.89 \times 10^{-3}} = 112.0$$

**Conclusion:** $Re_p = 112$ lies in the **intermediate regime** ($10 < Re_p < 1000$). The **inertial term (2133.7 Pa/m) is ~2.25 times the viscous term (947.0 Pa/m)**, confirming that kinetic energy losses are starting to dominate but both terms are significant — exactly the regime where the **Ergun superposition is most valuable**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the significance of the modified friction factor $f_p$ in verifying the Ergun equation. Show that the Ergun equation reduces to the form $f_p = \dfrac{150}{Re_p} + 1.75$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** During a lab verification, the following data were obtained for water ($\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $\mu = 10^{-3}$ Pa·s) flowing through a packed bed ($d_p = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $\varepsilon = 0.38$, $L = 0.25$ m, $D_c = 0.04$ m):

| $Q$ (m³/s) | $h$ (m) |
|---|---|
| 2.5 × 10⁻⁵ | 0.020 |
| 5.0 × 10⁻⁵ | 0.052 |
| 7.5 × 10⁻⁵ | 0.095 |
| 1.0 × 10⁻⁴ | 0.150 |

(Manometer fluid: CCl₄, $\rho_{CCl_4} = 1600$ kg/m³)

For the last data point, calculate $Re_p$, $f_p$ (experimental), and $f_p$ (theoretical from Ergun), and compute the percentage deviation. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Significance of Modified Friction Factor — 7 Marks:**

The **modified friction factor** $f_p$ is a dimensionless group that, when plotted against the particle Reynolds number $Re_p$, allows data from **all packed beds** (different particles, fluids, sizes) to collapse onto a **single universal curve**. This is the packed-bed analog of the Moody diagram for pipe flow. The **derivation** (as shown in Section 3.1 above) gives:

$$f_p = \frac{150}{Re_p} + 1.75$$

**Physical meaning:**
- At **low $Re_p$** (viscous regime): $f_p \approx 150/Re_p$ → slope of $-1$ on log-log plot.
- At **high $Re_p$** (inertial regime): $f_p \approx 1.75$ → horizontal asymptote.
- The **transition** occurs around $Re_p \approx 100$–$1000$.

Verification is complete when the experimental data points lie on or near this theoretical curve within acceptable deviation (±15%).

---

**(b) Calculation for Last Data Point — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Cross-sectional Area**

$$A_c = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.04)^{2} = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Superficial Velocity**

$$u_s = \frac{Q}{A_c} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-4}}{1.2566 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.07958 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Experimental ΔP from Manometer (CCl₄–water manometer)**

$$\Delta P_{exp} = (\rho_{CCl_4} - \rho_{w}) \cdot g \cdot h$$

$$= (1600 - 1000) \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.150 = 600 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.150 = 882.9 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 4: Particle Reynolds Number**

$$Re_p = \frac{d_p u_s \rho}{\mu} = \frac{(2 \times 10^{-3})(0.07958)(1000)}{10^{-3}} = 159.16$$

**Step 5: Experimental Modified Friction Factor**

$$f_{p,exp} = \frac{\Delta P_{exp}}{L} \cdot \frac{d_p}{\rho u_s^{2}} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon^{3}}{1-\varepsilon}$$

$$= \frac{882.9}{0.25} \cdot \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{1000 \cdot (0.07958)^{2}} \cdot \frac{(0.38)^{3}}{1 - 0.38}$$

$$= 3531.6 \cdot \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{6.333} \cdot \frac{0.054872}{0.62}$$

$$= 3531.6 \cdot 3.158 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 0.08850$$

$$f_{p,exp} = 3531.6 \cdot 2.795 \times 10^{-5} = 0.0987$$

**Step 6: Theoretical Modified Friction Factor (Ergun)**

$$f_{p,theo} = \frac{150}{Re_p} + 1.75 = \frac{150}{159.16} + 1.75 = 0.9425 + 1.75 = 2.6925$$

**Step 7: Percentage Deviation**

$$\% \text{ Deviation} = \frac{\vert f_{p,theo} - f_{p,exp} \vert}{f_{p,theo}} \times 100 = \frac{\vert 2.6925 - 0.0987 \vert}{2.6925} \times 100 = 96.3\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Manometer fluid selection** (3 marks): The student MUST subtract the working-fluid density from the manometer-fluid density. Using only $\rho_{CCl_4} \cdot g \cdot h$ is a common 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Unit consistency** (2 marks): $d_p$ must be in **meters** (not mm or cm). The $\varepsilon^3$ term magnifies small numerical errors by ~10×.
> 3. **Superficial vs interstitial velocity** (2 marks): $u_s$ uses the **empty column** cross-section, NOT the void area. Interstitial velocity $u_i = u_s/\varepsilon$ is sometimes confused.
> 4. **Air bubble in manometer** (1 mark): Always flush manometer lines before recording. A trapped bubble gives artificially low $\Delta P$.
> 5. **Channeling in bed** (2 marks): If the bed settles unevenly, fluid bypasses the packing, giving anomalously LOW $f_p$ (as seen in the 96% deviation above). Repacking and tapping the column is essential.
> 6. **Reporting $Re_p$ for ALL points** (1 mark): KTU expects a complete table, not just one row.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🚀 High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- [ ] **Ergun equation** combines **Darcy (viscous, $u_s^1$)** and **Burke–Plummer (inertial, $u_s^2$)** contributions.
- [ ] **Constants:** $150$ (viscous) and $1.75$ (inertial) — remember them in this order; do NOT swap.
- [ ] **$d_p$** must be in **meters**; the $1/d_p^2$ dependence makes this the most unit-sensitive parameter.
- [ ] **$\varepsilon$ appears as $\varepsilon^3$** in the denominator — small porosity errors cause large $\Delta P$ errors.
- [ ] **Modified friction factor** $f_p = \dfrac{150}{Re_p} + 1.75$ — the **single master equation** for verification.
- [ ] **Plot $f_p$ vs $Re_p$** on **log-log paper**; expect slope $-1$ at low $Re_p$ and flat asymptote at $1.75$ at high $Re_p$.
- [ ] **Manometer equation:** $\Delta P = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$ — always subtract working-fluid density.
- [ ] **Superficial velocity** $u_s = Q / A_c$ uses **empty column area**, not the void area.
- [ ] **Void fraction** for random spheres: $\varepsilon \approx 0.38$–$0.42$.
- [ ] **Typical lab error** tolerance: **±5% to ±15%**; >20% indicates experimental error.
- [ ] **Channeling** (fluid bypassing packing) is the #1 cause of anomalously low $\Delta P$ measurements.
- [ ] **Air bubble in manometer** is the #1 cause of anomalous readings; always bleed the manometer.
- [ ] **Ergun is valid** for $Re_p$ from **0.001 to 2500**; outside this range, use other correlations.
- [ ] **Real-world uses:** catalytic reactors, water filters, scrubbers, distillation columns.
- [ ] **Industrial design impact:** Underestimating $\Delta P$ leads to undersized pumps/compressors and reactor failure.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Experiment on fluidization techniques and determination of minimum fluidization velocity and pressure drop profile
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL308 - FLUID & PARTICLE MECHANICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Experiment on fluidization techniques and determination of minimum fluidization velocity and pressure drop profile

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Fluidization** is the unit operation in which a bed of finely divided solid particles is suspended in a flowing fluid (gas or liquid) such that the solid phase behaves as a fluid. The state begins when the upward drag force exerted by the fluid on the particles equals the effective weight (weight minus buoyancy) of the particles in the bed.

**Minimum Fluidization Velocity ($U_{mf}$):** It is the superficial fluid velocity at which the pressure drop across the particle bed becomes equal to the weight of the bed per unit cross-sectional area, marking the exact transition point from a **fixed bed** to a **fluidized bed**.

**Bed Porosity ($\varepsilon$):** The fraction of the bed volume occupied by the fluid (voidage) and is given by:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{V_{void}}{V_{bed}} = 1 - \frac{V_{solid}}{V_{bed}}$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine a transparent jar full of dry sand. When you gently blow air from the bottom (like blowing through a straw into a sugar pile), at low flow, the sand is packed — this is a **fixed bed**. As you blow harder, the sand grains suddenly start dancing, swirling, and floating — this is a **fluidized bed**. The exact moment the grains start moving is the **minimum fluidization point**.

Think of fluidization like the **"liquid-boiling" of solids**:
- Below $U_{mf}$ → particles **locked** (fixed bed).
- At $U_{mf}$ → particles **just suspended** (incipient fluidization).
- Above $U_{mf}$ → bed **expands** like a boiling liquid (particulate/pulsating fluidization).
- Very high velocity → particles **entrained** out (pneumatic transport / fast fluidization).

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \ m/s^2$
- **Density of air (NTP):** $\rho_f = 1.2 \ kg/m^3$
- **Viscosity of air (NTP):** $\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5} \ Pa \cdot s$
- **Density of water:** $\rho_f = 1000 \ kg/m^3$
- **Viscosity of water (25°C):** $\mu = 8.9 \times 10^{-4} \ Pa \cdot s$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In the lab PCCHL308, the fluidization experiment is typically performed using **air as the fluidizing medium** with solid particles like sand, glass beads, or rice. The standard column diameter is **50–80 mm** with a **sintered/perforated distributor plate** to ensure uniform flow.

### 1.4 Terminology Checklist for KTU Viva

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|------|--------|------------|
| Superficial velocity | $U$ | Volumetric flow rate / Column cross-sectional area |
| Bed height | $H$ | Height of solid particles at any instant |
| Bed expansion ratio | $R$ | $H / H_0$ |
| Sphericity | $\phi_s$ | Surface area of sphere of equal volume / Actual surface area |
| Archimedes number | $Ar$ | Gravity / Viscous force ratio (dimensionless) |
| Reynolds number | $Re$ | Inertia / Viscous force ratio |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Fluidization Process — Stage-by-Stage Logic

A packed bed of solid particles undergoes a sequence of regimes as the fluid velocity is gradually increased:

**Step 1 — Fixed Bed Region ($U < U_{mf}$):**
- Particles remain stationary, supported by inter-particle contact.
- Pressure drop ($\Delta P$) **increases** with velocity (approximately linearly at low $Re$, then with a $v^2$ term at high $Re$).
- Described by the **Ergun equation** (valid for $Re_p \leq 1000$).

**Step 2 — Incipient Fluidization ($U = U_{mf}$):**
- Drag force = Net weight of bed.
- $\Delta P$ equals bed weight per unit area.
- Bed may exhibit small localized motion (channeling may appear).

**Step 3 — Particulate Fluidization ($U_{mf} < U < U_{t}$):**
- Smooth, homogeneous expansion.
- Bed height $H$ increases steadily with $U$.
- $\Delta P$ remains **nearly constant** (equal to bed weight per unit area).

**Step 4 — Bubbling Fluidization (gas-solid systems, Geldart B & D powders):**
- Excess gas forms bubbles that rise through the dense phase.
- $\Delta P$ fluctuates around a constant mean value.

**Step 5 — Slugging / Channeling:**
- In narrow columns, bubbles can grow to column diameter → slugs.
- Large pressure fluctuations occur.

**Step 6 — Entrainment / Pneumatic Transport ($U \geq U_t$):**
- Particles carried out of the column by the fluid.
- $U_t$ = **terminal settling velocity** of a single particle.

### 2.2 Why Does the Pressure Drop Stay Constant After $U_{mf}$?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Once the bed is fully fluidized, any additional fluid must pass through the suspended particles. The particles simply move further apart, increasing porosity $\varepsilon$, so that the total drag available **always matches** the bed weight. Since the bed weight is constant (no particles leave), $\Delta P$ remains constant — this is the **fundamental signature of fluidization**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $U = \dfrac{Q}{A_c}$ | Superficial velocity (Q = flow rate, $A_c$ = column area) | m/s |
| 2 | $\Delta P = (1-\varepsilon)(\rho_s - \rho_f)gH$ | Pressure drop across fluidized/fixed bed | Pa |
| 3 | $\Delta P = H_0 (1 - \varepsilon_0)(\rho_s - \rho_f)g$ | Pressure drop at incipient fluidization | Pa |
| 4 | $\varepsilon_0 = 1 - \dfrac{m_s}{\rho_s A_c H_0}$ | Initial bed porosity (from mass $m_s$) | — |
| 5 | $\dfrac{\Delta P}{H}\cdot\dfrac{\phi_s^2 d_p^2 \varepsilon^3}{150 \mu (1-\varepsilon)^2} U + \dfrac{\phi_s d_p \varepsilon^3}{1.75 \rho_f (1-\varepsilon)} U^2$ | Ergun equation | — |
| 6 | $Re_{mf} = \dfrac{d_p U_{mf} \rho_f}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number at minimum fluidization | — |
| 7 | $Ar = \dfrac{d_p^3 \rho_f (\rho_s - \rho_f) g}{\mu^2}$ | Archimedes number | — |
| 8 | $Re_{mf} = 33.7^2 + 0.0408 \cdot Ar - 33.7$ | Wen & Yu correlation (most common) | — |
| 9 | $Re_{mf} = \sqrt{27.2^2 + 0.0408 \cdot Ar} - 27.2$ | Alternative form for laminar regime | — |
| 10 | $H = H_0 \cdot \dfrac{(1 - \varepsilon_0)}{(1 - \varepsilon)}$ | Bed expansion relationship | m |
| 11 | $U_t = \dfrac{d_p^2 (\rho_s - \rho_f) g}{18 \mu}$ | Stokes terminal velocity ($Re < 0.1$) | m/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorization Priority for KTU 2024:** Formulas **2, 3, 4, 6, 7, 8, 9** are the **highest-yield** for the lab exam. The Wen & Yu equation (8) is the most tested correlation.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of Fluidization

| Industry | Application |
|----------|-------------|
| Petroleum | **Fluid Catalytic Cracking (FCC)** — biggest industrial use of fluidization |
| Chemical | Roasting of sulfide ores, calcination of limestone |
| Pharmaceutical | Fluidized bed dryers/coaters for granules |
| Energy | Coal combustion in circulating fluidized bed (CFB) boilers |
| Pollution Control | Fluidized bed scrubbers for SOx/NOx removal |
| Food | Instant coffee/tea agglomeration, drying of grains |

### 2.5 Geldart Powder Classification (Background for Viva)

- **Geldart A (Aeratable):** Small, low-density particles → smooth fluidization (typical FCC catalyst).
- **Geldart B (Sand-like):** Most common in lab experiments → bubbling fluidization.
- **Geldart C (Cohesive):** Fine, sticky particles → channeling observed.
- **Geldart D (Spoutable):** Large/dense particles → require spouting assistance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Pressure Drop at Minimum Fluidization

**Forces acting on a particle in the bed:**

Consider a single spherical particle of diameter $d_p$ and density $\rho_s$ immersed in a fluid of density $\rho_f$:

- **Weight (downward):** $F_g = \dfrac{\pi d_p^3}{6} \rho_s g$
- **Buoyancy (upward):** $F_b = \dfrac{\pi d_p^3}{6} \rho_f g$
- **Net gravitational force (effective weight):** $F_e = F_g - F_b = \dfrac{\pi d_p^3}{6} (\rho_s - \rho_f) g$
- **Drag force (upward):** $F_d = C_D \cdot \dfrac{\pi d_p^2}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_f U^2}{2}$

**For a bed of $N$ particles per unit volume:**

Total effective weight per unit cross-section of bed (per unit area):

$$W = N \cdot A_c \cdot H \cdot \dfrac{\pi d_p^3}{6}(\rho_s - \rho_f)g$$

Dividing by $A_c$, and noting that the solid volume fraction is $(1 - \varepsilon)$:

$$W = H(1 - \varepsilon)(\rho_s - \rho_f)g$$

At minimum fluidization, drag force exactly balances this weight, so:

$$\Delta P_{mf} = H_{mf}(1 - \varepsilon_{mf})(\rho_s - \rho_f)g$$

Since particles are conserved: $H_0(1 - \varepsilon_0) = H_{mf}(1 - \varepsilon_{mf})$ (at incipient fluidization the porosity is just the loose-packed value $\varepsilon_0$):

$$\boxed{\Delta P_{mf} = H_0(1 - \varepsilon_0)(\rho_s - \rho_f)g}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Wen & Yu Correlation for $U_{mf}$

The Ergun equation combined with the incipient fluidization condition yields a relation between $Re_{mf}$ and $Ar$.

The Ergun equation in dimensionless form:

$$\frac{\Delta P}{H} \cdot \frac{\phi_s^2 d_p^3 \rho_f}{150 \mu^2 (1-\varepsilon)^2} Re + \frac{\phi_s d_p^3 \rho_f}{1.75 \mu (1-\varepsilon)} Re^2 = Ar$$

Setting $\Delta P/H = (1-\varepsilon)(\rho_s - \rho_f)g$ and dividing by $Ar$:

$$\frac{150(1-\varepsilon)}{\phi_s^2 \varepsilon^3} \cdot \frac{Re}{Ar} + \frac{1.75}{\phi_s \varepsilon^3} \cdot \frac{Re^2}{Ar} = 1$$

For typical spherical particles at incipient fluidization, $\varepsilon_{mf} \approx 0.4$ and $\phi_s \approx 1$, the constants reduce to give:

$$Re_{mf}^2 \cdot \frac{1.75}{1} \cdot \frac{1}{(0.4)^3} = constant$$

Wen & Yu (1966) empirically fit this across many systems to obtain the cleanest form:

$$Re_{mf} = 33.7 \left[ \sqrt{1 + \frac{0.0408 \cdot Ar}{33.7^2}} - 1 \right]$$

Expanding the square root via the identity $\sqrt{1+x} \approx 1 + x/2 - x^2/8$ gives the commonly written form:

$$\boxed{Re_{mf} = 33.7^2 + 0.0408 \cdot Ar - 33.7}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Caveat:** Many textbooks use $Re_{mf} = \sqrt{33.7^2 + 0.0408 \cdot Ar} - 33.7$. Both forms are mathematically equivalent — confirm which form your KTU textbook uses before writing in the exam.

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Lab Question Style)

**Given:**
- Particle: Sand, $d_p = 0.5 \ mm = 5 \times 10^{-4} \ m$
- $\rho_s = 2500 \ kg/m^3$
- Fluid: Air at 25°C, $\rho_f = 1.2 \ kg/m^3$, $\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5} \ Pa \cdot s$
- Bed mass: $m_s = 1.5 \ kg$
- Column diameter: $D = 75 \ mm$, Area $A_c = \pi (0.075)^2 / 4 = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \ m^2$
- Initial bed height: $H_0 = 0.20 \ m$

**Step 1: Compute initial porosity $\varepsilon_0$:**

$$\varepsilon_0 = 1 - \frac{m_s}{\rho_s A_c H_0}$$

$$\varepsilon_0 = 1 - \frac{1.5}{2500 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.20}$$

$$\varepsilon_0 = 1 - \frac{1.5}{2.209}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_0 = 0.321}$$

**Step 2: Compute pressure drop at incipient fluidization $\Delta P_{mf}$:**

$$\Delta P_{mf} = H_0 (1 - \varepsilon_0)(\rho_s - \rho_f)g$$

$$\Delta P_{mf} = 0.20 \times (1 - 0.321) \times (2500 - 1.2) \times 9.81$$

$$\Delta P_{mf} = 0.20 \times 0.679 \times 2498.8 \times 9.81$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P_{mf} \approx 3328 \ Pa \approx 33.28 \ cm \ of \ water}$$

**Step 3: Compute Archimedes number $Ar$:**

$$Ar = \frac{d_p^3 \rho_f (\rho_s - \rho_f) g}{\mu^2}$$

$$Ar = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-4})^3 \times 1.2 \times 2498.8 \times 9.81}{(1.81 \times 10^{-5})^2}$$

$$Ar = \frac{1.25 \times 10^{-10} \times 1.2 \times 2498.8 \times 9.81}{3.276 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$Ar = \frac{3.675 \times 10^{-6}}{3.276 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$\boxed{Ar \approx 11{,}220}$$

**Step 4: Compute $Re_{mf}$ using Wen & Yu:**

$$Re_{mf} = \sqrt{33.7^2 + 0.0408 \times 11220} - 33.7$$

$$Re_{mf} = \sqrt{1135.69 + 457.78} - 33.7$$

$$Re_{mf} = \sqrt{1593.47} - 33.7$$

$$Re_{mf} = 39.92 - 33.7$$

$$\boxed{Re_{mf} \approx 6.22}$$

**Step 5: Compute $U_{mf}$:**

$$U_{mf} = \frac{Re_{mf} \cdot \mu}{d_p \cdot \rho_f} = \frac{6.22 \times 1.81 \times 10^{-5}}{5 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.2}$$

$$\boxed{U_{mf} \approx 0.188 \ m/s}$$

**Step 6: Verification using measured data:**
Compare with experimental $U_{mf}$ obtained from the intersection of the fixed-bed line and the constant $\Delta P$ plateau on the $\Delta P$ vs $U$ plot.

### 3.4 Plot Interpretation — Reading the Graph

| Region | Slope | Behavior |
|--------|-------|----------|
| AB (Fixed bed) | Steep, $\Delta P \propto U$ initially | Particles stationary |
| B (Incipient point) | Peak / break | $U = U_{mf}$ |
| BC (Fluidized) | Nearly flat, $\Delta P$ constant | Bed expands |
| CD (Entrainment) | $\Delta P$ drops sharply | Particles leave bed |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Many students mistakenly report the $U$ at point **C** (end of plateau) instead of **B** (start of plateau). The minimum fluidization velocity is at the **first** point where $\Delta P$ becomes constant, not the last.

### 3.5 Summary of $U_{mf}$ Determination Methods

1. **Experimental graphical method:** From $\Delta P$ vs $U$ plot.
2. **Wen & Yu equation:** Using $Ar$ and the empirical correlation.
3. **Ergun equation approach:** Set $\Delta P$ from Ergun equal to bed weight; solve iteratively for $U_{mf}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Fluidization Experimental Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Blower Air Supply Unit] --> A2[Calibrated Rotameter Flow Control Valve]
    A2 --> A3[Plenum Chamber Below Distributor]
    A3 --> A4[Perforated Sintered Distributor Plate]
    A4 --> A5[Fluidization Column Acrylic Transparent]
    A5 --> A6[Solid Particle Bed Sand or Glass Beads]
    A6 --> A7[Freeboard Region Above Bed]
    A7 --> A8[Top Outlet to Atmosphere or Filter]
    A6 -.U tube Manometer.-> A9[Differential Manometer Bank]
    A5 -. Bed Height H.-> A10[Scale on Column Side]
    A1 --> A11[Regulator Pressure Stabilizer]
    A11 --> A1
```

**Reading the block diagram:**
- The air enters from the **blower** at the bottom.
- The **rotameter** measures the volumetric flow rate $Q$.
- The **distributor plate** ensures uniform flow across the cross-section.
- The **bed** is the section containing the particles.
- The **manometer** bank measures the pressure drop across various sections.
- The **scale** on the column allows reading of bed height $H$.

### 4.2 Mermaid Process Flowchart: Experimental Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start Experiment] --> P2[Weigh Known Mass of Particles ms]
    P2 --> P3[Measure Initial Bed Height H0]
    P3 --> P4[Switch On Blower and Set Flow to Minimum]
    P4 --> P5[Record Flow Rate Q and Pressure Drop DeltaP]
    P5 --> P6{Increase Flow Incrementally}
    P6 --> P7[Record Q and DeltaP at Each Step]
    P7 --> P8{DeltaP Levels Off}
    P8 -- No --> P6
    P8 -- Yes --> P9[Note Bed Expansion and Height H]
    P9 --> P10[Continue Increasing Flow Until Slugging or Entrainment]
    P10 --> P11[Switch Off Blower and Repeat for Two More Trials]
    P11 --> P12[Plot DeltaP vs Superficial Velocity U]
    P12 --> P13[Identify Fixed Bed Fluidized and Entrainment Regions]
    P13 --> P14[Determine Umf from Intersection of Regions]
    P14 --> P15[Compare Experimental Umf with Wen Yu Prediction]
    P15 --> P16[End Experiment]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Graph: Expected $\Delta P$ vs $U$ Curve (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Pressure Drop vs Superficial Velocity"
    x-axis "U (m/s)" [0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.15, 0.2, 0.25, 0.3, 0.35, 0.4]
    y-axis "Delta P (Pa)" 0 --> 4000
    line [0, 1200, 2400, 3200, 3350, 3360, 3350, 3300, 2800]
```

### 4.4 Component-Level Block Diagram of Fluidization Column Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BottomSection
        S1[Inlet Pipe]
        S2[Plenum Chamber]
        S3[Distributor Plate]
    end
    subgraph MiddleSection
        S4[Particle Bed Region]
        S5[Manometer Taps T1 T2 T3 T4]
    end
    subgraph TopSection
        S6[Freeboard]
        S7[Outlet Filter]
        S8[Exhaust]
    end
    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8
    S4 -.Taps.-> S5
```

> [!NOTE]
> The taps $T_1$–$T_4$ are pressure-sensing points connected to the manometer bank to measure the static pressure at multiple heights in the bed.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define **minimum fluidization velocity** and list any **two** factors on which it depends.

**Model Answer:**

Minimum fluidization velocity ($U_{mf}$) is the superficial fluid velocity at which the pressure drop across a fixed bed of solid particles first becomes equal to the weight of the bed per unit cross-sectional area. At this point, the drag force on the particles just balances their net submerged weight, and the bed is on the verge of becoming fluidized.

**Two factors affecting $U_{mf}$:**
1. **Particle diameter ($d_p$):** $U_{mf} \propto d_p^{1.82}$ for fine particles (Wen & Yu, large $Ar$); generally increases with particle size.
2. **Density difference ($\rho_s - \rho_f$):** Heavier particles (greater $\rho_s - \rho_f$) require higher $U_{mf}$.

(Other valid factors: fluid viscosity $\mu$, sphericity $\phi_s$, bed voidage $\varepsilon_{mf}$.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Differentiate between a **fixed bed** and a **fluidized bed** in three points.

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Fixed Bed | Fluidized Bed |
|----------|-----------|---------------|
| Particle motion | Stationary, in contact | Suspended, free to move |
| Pressure drop | Increases with velocity ($U < U_{mf}$) | Nearly constant ($U \geq U_{mf}$) |
| Bed height | Constant, equals initial $H_0$ | Expands with $U$ |
| Mixing | Plug flow of fluid, no particle mixing | Excellent solid and fluid mixing |
| Heat transfer | Poor (contact conduction only) | High (due to particle motion) |
| Surface | Defined, planar | Fluctuating, "boiling-like" |

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Pitfall 1:** Confusing $U_{mf}$ with the velocity at the end of the flat region — it is at the **start** (break-point).
> - **Pitfall 2:** Forgetting the buoyancy correction — students often write $(\rho_s)g$ instead of $(\rho_s - \rho_f)g$, losing 1–2 marks.
> - **Pitfall 3:** Using $H_0$ in place of $H$ in the bed-expansion relation. Always check which height is being used.
> - **Pitfall 4:** Not converting mm to m when computing $d_p$ and $Ar$ — a $10^9$ error will arise.
> - **Pitfall 5:** Writing the Ergun equation but failing to state **the regime of validity** ($Re_p < 1000$).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Fluidization Theory + Calculation**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Question for ESE]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat $\Delta P$ vs $U$ diagram, describe the different **regimes of fluidization** as the gas velocity is increased gradually. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a fluidization experiment, a bed of sand particles (diameter $d_p = 1.0 \ mm$, density $\rho_s = 2600 \ kg/m^3$, $m_s = 2.0 \ kg$) is fluidized by air ($\rho_f = 1.2 \ kg/m^3$, $\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5} \ Pa \cdot s$). The column diameter is $0.10 \ m$ and the initial bed height $H_0 = 0.25 \ m$. Calculate: (i) the initial porosity $\varepsilon_0$, (ii) the Archimedes number $Ar$, and (iii) the minimum fluidization velocity $U_{mf}$ using the **Wen & Yu correlation**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Regimes of fluidization** (graph description — 7 marks):

As gas velocity is increased:

1. **Fixed bed (O–A):** Particles are stationary; $\Delta P$ increases with $U$ following the Ergun equation.
2. **Incipient fluidization (A):** $\Delta P$ reaches a maximum equal to the bed weight per unit area; $U = U_{mf}$.
3. **Fluidized bed (A–B):** $\Delta P$ remains **constant**; bed height increases smoothly with $U$.
4. **Bubbling/slugging (B–C):** Bubbles form, pressure fluctuates; slugs in narrow columns.
5. **Entrainment (C onwards):** $U$ exceeds terminal velocity $U_t$; particles are carried out; $\Delta P$ drops sharply.

**Note:** A clearly labelled sketch must accompany this answer in the exam — typically a curve with axes labelled $\Delta P$ (y) and $U$ (x), with points A, B, C marked.

**(b) Numerical solution** (7 marks):

**(i) Initial porosity $\varepsilon_0$:**

$$A_c = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.10)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \ m^2$$

$$\varepsilon_0 = 1 - \frac{m_s}{\rho_s A_c H_0} = 1 - \frac{2.0}{2600 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.25}$$

$$\varepsilon_0 = 1 - \frac{2.0}{5.105} = 1 - 0.392$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_0 = 0.608}$$

**(ii) Archimedes number $Ar$:**

$$Ar = \frac{d_p^3 \rho_f (\rho_s - \rho_f)g}{\mu^2}$$

$$Ar = \frac{(1.0 \times 10^{-3})^3 \times 1.2 \times (2600 - 1.2) \times 9.81}{(1.81 \times 10^{-5})^2}$$

$$Ar = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.2 \times 2598.8 \times 9.81}{3.276 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$Ar = \frac{3.058 \times 10^{-5}}{3.276 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$\boxed{Ar \approx 93{,}360}$$

**(iii) Minimum fluidization velocity $U_{mf}$:**

Using Wen & Yu: $Re_{mf} = \sqrt{33.7^2 + 0.0408 \cdot Ar} - 33.7$

$$Re_{mf} = \sqrt{1135.69 + 3809.1} - 33.7 = \sqrt{4944.79} - 33.7$$

$$Re_{mf} = 70.32 - 33.7 = 36.62$$

$$U_{mf} = \frac{Re_{mf} \cdot \mu}{d_p \cdot \rho_f} = \frac{36.62 \times 1.81 \times 10^{-5}}{1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.2}$$

$$\boxed{U_{mf} \approx 0.552 \ m/s}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Question for ESE]**

**(a)** State the **Ergun equation** and explain the physical significance of each term. Derive the relationship for the **pressure drop at incipient fluidization**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fluidization column is packed with 3.0 kg of glass beads of density $2450 \ kg/m^3$ and mean diameter $0.6 \ mm$. The initial packed height is $0.20 \ m$ in a column of $0.085 \ m$ diameter. Fluidizing air has $\rho_f = 1.184 \ kg/m^3$ and $\mu = 1.85 \times 10^{-5} \ Pa \cdot s$. Calculate: (i) the pressure drop expected at incipient fluidization, (ii) the experimental $Re_{mf}$, and (iii) the operating $U_{mf}$ in $m/s$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Ergun equation + significance + derivation (7 marks):**

**Statement of Ergun equation:**

$$\frac{\Delta P}{H} = \frac{150 \mu (1-\varepsilon)^2}{\phi_s^2 d_p^2 \varepsilon^3} U + \frac{1.75 \rho_f (1-\varepsilon)}{\phi_s d_p \varepsilon^3} U^2$$

**Physical significance:**
- The **first term** ($\propto U$) is the **viscous (laminar) loss** — dominant at low Reynolds numbers (Carman-Kozeny type).
- The **second term** ($\propto U^2$) is the **inertial (turbulent) loss** — dominant at high Reynolds numbers (Burke-Plummer type).
- $\phi_s$ is the sphericity (shape factor); $\varepsilon$ is bed porosity; $d_p$ is particle diameter.
- The equation combines the viscous and inertial contributions to pressure drop in a packed bed.

**Derivation of $\Delta P_{mf}$:**

At minimum fluidization, drag = submerged weight. For a bed of $N$ particles per unit volume:

$$\text{Drag} = \Delta P \cdot A_c, \quad \text{Weight (effective)} = N \cdot A_c H \cdot \frac{\pi d_p^3}{6}(\rho_s - \rho_f)g$$

Equating and noting $N \cdot \frac{\pi d_p^3}{6} = (1-\varepsilon)$:

$$\Delta P_{mf} = H_0 (1 - \varepsilon_0)(\rho_s - \rho_f) g$$

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Pressure drop at incipient fluidization:**

$$A_c = \frac{\pi (0.085)^2}{4} = 5.674 \times 10^{-3} \ m^2$$

$$\varepsilon_0 = 1 - \frac{3.0}{2450 \times 5.674 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.20} = 1 - \frac{3.0}{2.780} = 1 - 1.079$$

**Note:** $\varepsilon_0 < 0$ is non-physical. This means the bed is **closely packed** and the given mass is excessive for the stated $H_0$. The standard KTU approach: assume $\varepsilon_0 \approx 0.40$ (typical for glass beads) and recompute $H_0$, or use the given $H_0$ with calculated $\varepsilon_0 = 0.40$ directly.

Using $\varepsilon_0 = 0.40$ (loose packing):

$$\Delta P_{mf} = 0.20 \times (1 - 0.40) \times (2450 - 1.184) \times 9.81$$

$$\Delta P_{mf} = 0.20 \times 0.60 \times 2448.82 \times 9.81$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P_{mf} \approx 2883 \ Pa \approx 29.4 \ cm \ of \ water}$$

**(ii) Archimedes number:**

$$Ar = \frac{(6 \times 10^{-4})^3 \times 1.184 \times 2448.82 \times 9.81}{(1.85 \times 10^{-5})^2} = \frac{2.16 \times 10^{-10} \times 28{,}481}{3.42 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$Ar \approx \frac{6.152 \times 10^{-6}}{3.42 \times 10^{-10}} \approx 17{,}990$$

**Reynolds number at $U_{mf}$:**

$$Re_{mf} = \sqrt{33.7^2 + 0.0408 \times 17990} - 33.7 = \sqrt{1135.69 + 733.99} - 33.7$$

$$Re_{mf} = \sqrt{1869.68} - 33.7 = 43.24 - 33.7$$

$$\boxed{Re_{mf} \approx 9.54}$$

**(iii) Operating $U_{mf}$:**

$$U_{mf} = \frac{Re_{mf} \cdot \mu}{d_p \cdot \rho_f} = \frac{9.54 \times 1.85 \times 10^{-5}}{6 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.184}$$

$$\boxed{U_{mf} \approx 0.249 \ m/s}$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Preparation):**

- ⭐ **Definition:** $U_{mf}$ = superficial fluid velocity when $\Delta P$ first equals bed weight per unit area.
- ⭐ **Pressure drop at $U_{mf}$:** $\Delta P_{mf} = H_0(1 - \varepsilon_0)(\rho_s - \rho_f)g$ — **always include buoyancy**.
- ⭐ **Porosity formula:** $\varepsilon_0 = 1 - \dfrac{m_s}{\rho_s A_c H_0}$ — convert all units to SI.
- ⭐ **Archimedes number:** $Ar = \dfrac{d_p^3 \rho_f (\rho_s - \rho_f)g}{\mu^2}$ — dimensionless, often very large ($10^3$–$10^7$).
- ⭐ **Wen & Yu correlation:** $Re_{mf} = \sqrt{33.7^2 + 0.0408 \cdot Ar} - 33.7$ — most-tested in KTU.
- ⭐ **Ergun equation:** combines viscous + inertial losses; valid for $Re_p < 1000$.
- ⭐ **Regimes in order:** Fixed bed → Incipient → Particulate fluidization → Bubbling/slugging → Entrainment.
- ⭐ **Constant $\Delta P$ plateau** is the signature of fluidization — used to identify $U_{mf}$ graphically.
- ⭐ **Bigger particles → larger $U_{mf}$**; **denser particles → larger $U_{mf}$**; **more viscous fluid → larger $U_{mf}$**.
- ⭐ **Sphericity $\phi_s$:** sphere = 1; irregular shapes < 1; appears in Ergun equation only (cancels in Wen–Yu at $\phi_s = 1$).
- ⭐ **Industrial use:** FCC in petroleum refining is the largest single application of fluidization in the world.
- ⭐ **Common lab errors:** Channeling (small column, cohesive particles), static electricity (dry powders), manometer fluctuation (bubbling regime) — these are **expected**, not failures.
- ⭐ **Unit conversions:** $1 \ cm \ H_2O = 98.1 \ Pa$; $1 \ mm = 10^{-3} \ m$; $1 \ cP = 10^{-3} \ Pa \cdot s$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** When writing the answer, **always draw the $\Delta P$ vs $U$ curve with all regions labelled** (fixed, incipient, fluidized, entrainment) and **mark the point of $U_{mf}$** clearly. Examiners reward well-labelled sketches generously in the lab exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of efficiency of a centrifugal pump or reciprocating pump
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL308 - FLUID & PARTICLE MECHANICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of efficiency of a centrifugal pump or reciprocating pump

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pump Efficiency** is defined as the ratio of useful hydraulic power (water power) delivered by the pump to the mechanical power supplied to the pump shaft. It quantifies how effectively a pump converts input mechanical energy into useful fluid energy.

**Formally:**

$$\eta_{overall} = \frac{\text{Water Power (Output)}}{\text{Shaft Power (Input)}}$$

For a **centrifugal pump**, the overall efficiency is the product of three sub-efficiencies:

$$\eta_{overall} = \eta_{manometric} \times \eta_{mechanical} \times \eta_{volumetric}$$

For a **reciprocating pump**, the overall efficiency is typically expressed as:

$$\eta_{overall} = \eta_{mechanical} \times \eta_{volumetric} \times \eta_{hydraulic}$$

### Terminology Chart

| Term | Definition | Typical Value |
|------|------------|---------------|
| **Water Power (P_w)** | Useful power delivered to water (W) | Function of Q × H |
| **Shaft Power (P_sh)** | Power supplied to pump shaft by motor | Function of torque |
| **Manometric Head (H_m)** | Head developed by pump measured using pressure gauges | metres of water |
| **Suction Head (H_s)** | Vertical distance between sump and pump centreline | metres |
| **Delivery Head (H_d)** | Vertical distance between pump and delivery point | metres |
| **Slip (%)** | Difference between theoretical and actual discharge | 2–5% (well-maintained) |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy – The Water Carrier Scenario**

Imagine you are a worker (the **motor**) lifting buckets of water from a well to a rooftop tank. You are paid for the energy you expend (your **salary = shaft power**), but the homeowner only cares about the water that actually reaches the tank (**water power**).

- Some of your energy is wasted because you spill water while climbing the stairs → **volumetric loss**.
- Some energy is lost as heat from your muscles and friction in the rope over the pulley → **mechanical loss**.
- Some energy is lost because the water itself churns and forms eddies inside the bucket before it settles → **hydraulic loss**.

The ratio of the *useful water that reaches the tank* to the *total food/energy you consumed* is the **overall efficiency** of the water-carrying operation. A pump behaves exactly the same way – the input is electrical/mechanical power, and the output is the pressurized, flowing water.

> **Key Insight:** No real pump is 100% efficient. A well-designed centrifugal pump typically has an efficiency of **60–85 %**, while a well-maintained reciprocating pump can reach **70–90 %**.

---

## 1.3 Why Efficiency Determination is a KTU Lab Priority

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **Fluid & Particle Mechanics Lab (PCCHL308)**, this experiment directly maps to:
> - **CO2**: Apply the principles of fluid mechanics to determine performance characteristics of hydraulic machines.
> - **CO3**: Analyse experimental data to compute efficiencies and validate theoretical models.
> - **Bloom's Level**: *Apply (Level 3)* + *Analyse (Level 4)*

The lab teaches you to:
1. Measure discharge, head, and power.
2. Calculate water power, shaft power, and overall efficiency.
3. Plot characteristic curves (Head vs. Discharge, Efficiency vs. Discharge).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown (Centrifugal Pump)

A centrifugal pump uses a rotating **impeller** to impart kinetic energy to the water, which is then converted to pressure energy in the **volute casing**.

**Why efficiency exists at all:**
- Water leaks back from the delivery side to the suction side through the clearances → volumetric loss.
- Bearings, shaft seals, and disc friction on the back of the impeller consume power → mechanical loss.
- Turbulence, eddy formation, and friction inside the casing dissipate energy as heat → hydraulic/manometric loss.

**How we measure it experimentally:**

1. We measure the **actual discharge (Q)** by collecting water in a measuring tank over a known time.
2. We measure the **suction head (H_s)** and **delivery head (H_d)** using piezometers or Bourdon gauges.
3. We measure the **shaft power (P_sh)** using a rope brake dynamometer or by an electrical input method (Wattmeter + motor efficiency).
4. We compute the **manometric head (H_m)** from gauge readings.
5. We compute the **water power (P_w)** using $P_w = \rho g Q H_m$.
6. We compute efficiency as $\eta = P_w / P_sh$.

---

## 2.2 Operational Concept Breakdown (Reciprocating Pump)

A reciprocating pump uses a **piston/plunger** moving back and forth inside a cylinder to displace water. It is a positive-displacement device.

**Critical concept – SLIP:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Slip** is the difference between the **theoretical discharge (Q_th)** and the **actual discharge (Q_act)**. It is caused by:
> - Leakage of water past the piston rings, valves, and gland packing.
> - Delayed opening/closing of suction and delivery valves.

$$\text{Slip} = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$$

**Coefficient of discharge (C_d):**

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = 1 - \frac{\text{Slip}}{Q_{th}}$$

A well-designed reciprocating pump should have $C_d$ between **0.85 and 0.99**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table – it covers 95% of the marks in Part A and Part B of Module Experiment 2.**

### Section A – Centrifugal Pump Formulas

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Theoretical Discharge | $Q = A \times v = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 v$ | m³/s |
| 2 | Manometric Head | $H_m = H_d + H_s + \frac{V_d^2 - V_s^2}{2g}$ | m |
| 3 | Water Power | $P_w = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H_m$ | W |
| 4 | Water Power (kW) | $P_w = \frac{Q \cdot H_m}{75}$ (metric HP) or $\frac{\rho g Q H_m}{1000}$ | kW |
| 5 | Shaft Power (Rope Brake) | $P_{sh} = \frac{2\pi N T}{60} = \frac{2\pi N (W - S) R}{60}$ | W |
| 6 | Shaft Power (Brake) | $P_{sh} = \frac{(W - S) \cdot \pi \cdot N \cdot R}{60}$ | W |
| 7 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_{overall} = \frac{P_w}{P_{sh}}$ | decimal/% |
| 8 | Manometric Efficiency | $\eta_{man} = \frac{g \cdot H_m}{V_{w2} \cdot u_2}$ | decimal/% |
| 9 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = \frac{P_w / \eta_{man}}{P_{sh}}$ | decimal/% |
| 10 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_{vol} = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | decimal/% |

### Section B – Reciprocating Pump Formulas

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Theoretical Discharge | $Q_{th} = \frac{A \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$ (single-acting, single-cylinder) | m³/s |
| 2 | Theoretical Discharge (Double-acting) | $Q_{th} = \frac{(2A - a) \cdot L \cdot N}{60}$ | m³/s |
| 3 | Slip | $\text{Slip} = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$ | m³/s |
| 4 | Coefficient of Discharge | $C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | – |
| 5 | % Slip | $\%\text{Slip} = \frac{Q_{th} - Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} \times 100$ | % |
| 6 | Water Power | $P_w = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q_{act} \cdot H$ | W |
| 7 | Shaft Power (from motor input) | $P_{sh} = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi \cdot \eta_{motor}$ | W |
| 8 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_{overall} = \frac{\rho g Q_{act} H}{P_{sh}}$ | – |

> **Note on units:** In KTU exams, $Q$ is usually in **LPS (litres per second)** and you must convert: $1\ \text{LPS} = 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Centrifugal pumps** dominate in water supply schemes, HVAC, irrigation, fire-fighting, and boiler feed. Efficiency testing helps in selecting the Best Efficiency Point (BEP).
> - **Reciprocating pumps** are used for high-pressure, low-flow applications – oil well drilling, hydraulics, chemical dosing. Slip measurement predicts wear and maintenance needs.
> - BIS Standard **IS 1710** and **ISO 9906** govern pump performance testing in India.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Manometric Head for a Centrifugal Pump

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Starting point – Energy equation between suction and delivery gauge tapping points (per unit weight of water):**

$$\frac{P_s}{\rho g} + \frac{V_s^2}{2g} + H_s = \frac{P_d}{\rho g} + \frac{V_d^2}{2g} + H_d + \text{losses}$$

When we measure $P_s$ and $P_d$ directly using vacuum and pressure gauges, the **manometric head** absorbed by the pump itself is:

$$H_m = \left(H_d - H_s\right) + \frac{P_d - P_s}{\rho g} + \frac{V_d^2 - V_s^2}{2g}$$

**Substitution of gauge pressures:**

Let $P_d$ be read by a Bourdon pressure gauge (gauge pressure, not absolute), and $P_s$ be read by a vacuum gauge. The vacuum gauge reading $h_v$ corresponds to:

$$P_s = P_{atm} - \rho g h_v$$

So $\frac{P_d - P_s}{\rho g} = \frac{P_d - P_{atm}}{\rho g} + h_v$. The atmospheric terms cancel if we use a compound gauge, giving:

$$\boxed{H_m = (H_d + H_s) + h_v + h_d \cdot \frac{g}{g} \pm \frac{V_d^2 - V_s^2}{2g}}$$

For laboratory-scale pumps with small-diameter taps, the velocity-head terms are often negligible:

$$H_m \approx (H_d + H_s) + h_v + h_d$$

where $h_v$ = vacuum gauge reading (m of water), $h_d$ = pressure gauge reading (m of water).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Water Power and Shaft Power

### Water Power

By definition, power = force × velocity = (pressure × area) × velocity:

$$P_w = \Delta p \cdot Q = \rho g H_m \cdot Q \quad [\text{Watts}]$$

In **kilowatts** (using $Q$ in m³/s):

$$P_w = \frac{\rho g Q H_m}{1000} = \frac{9.81 \cdot Q \cdot H_m}{1000} \quad [\text{kW}]$$

In **metric HP** (1 HP = 75 kgf·m/s):

$$P_w = \frac{\rho g Q H_m}{75} = \frac{Q H_m}{75} \quad [\text{HP}]$$

### Shaft Power (Rope-Brake Dynamometer Method)

The rope brake applies a frictional tangential force $(W - S)$ on a brake-drum of radius $R$ rotating at $N$ rpm.

**Tangential braking force:** $F = (W - S)\ g$ Newtons (where $W, S$ are in kgf).

**Braking torque:** $T = F \cdot R = (W - S)\ g \cdot R$ (N·m)

**Angular velocity:** $\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$ rad/s

**Shaft power:**

$$P_{sh} = T \cdot \omega = \frac{2\pi N (W - S) g R}{60} \quad [\text{Watts}]$$

**Compact working formula (KTU standard):**

$$\boxed{P_{sh} = \frac{\pi N (W - S) R}{60} \times 9.81 \quad [\text{W}] \quad \text{or} \quad P_{sh} = \frac{2\pi N (W - S) R}{60} \text{ if g is included}}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Worked-Out Numerical Example (Centrifugal Pump)

> [!NOTE]
> **This is a typical KTU Part-B style question. Solve it step by step as shown.**

**Given data (from lab observation table):**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Diameter of delivery pipe, $D$ | 0.05 m |
| Vacuum gauge reading, $h_v$ | 4 m of water |
| Pressure gauge reading, $h_d$ | 16 m of water |
| Suction head, $H_s$ | 2 m |
| Delivery head, $H_d$ | 6 m |
| Discharge, $Q$ | 12 LPS = 0.012 m³/s |
| Speed, $N$ | 1440 rpm |
| Brake-drum radius, $R$ | 0.20 m |
| Spring balance readings, $W$ and $S$ | $W = 20$ kg, $S = 4$ kg |

**Find:** (i) Manometric head $H_m$  (ii) Water Power $P_w$  (iii) Shaft Power $P_{sh}$  (iv) Overall efficiency.

---

### Step 1: Compute the Manometric Head

$$H_m = (H_s + H_d) + h_v + h_d \quad \text{(neglecting velocity-head terms)}$$

$$H_m = (2 + 6) + 4 + 16$$

$$H_m = 28\ \text{m of water}$$

---

### Step 2: Compute the Water Power

$$P_w = \rho g Q H_m = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 \times 28$$

$$P_w = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.336 = 3296.16\ \text{W} \approx 3.296\ \text{kW}$$

---

### Step 3: Compute the Shaft Power (Rope-Brake Method)

$$P_{sh} = \frac{2\pi N (W - S) g R}{60}$$

$$P_{sh} = \frac{2 \times \pi \times 1440 \times (20 - 4) \times 9.81 \times 0.20}{60}$$

**Substituting values:**

$$P_{sh} = \frac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 1440 \times 16 \times 9.81 \times 0.20}{60}$$

**Numerator computation:**

- $2 \times 3.1416 = 6.2832$
- $6.2832 \times 1440 = 9047.81$
- $9047.81 \times 16 = 144764.96$
- $144764.96 \times 9.81 = 1420344.66$
- $1420344.66 \times 0.20 = 284068.93$

**Final division:**

$$P_{sh} = \frac{284068.93}{60} = 4734.48\ \text{W} \approx 4.734\ \text{kW}$$

---

### Step 4: Compute the Overall Efficiency

$$\eta_{overall} = \frac{P_w}{P_{sh}} = \frac{3.296}{4.734}$$

$$\eta_{overall} = 0.6962 = 69.62\ \text{\%}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Worked-Out Numerical Example (Reciprocating Pump)

**Given data:**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Bore diameter, $D$ | 100 mm = 0.10 m |
| Stroke length, $L$ | 150 mm = 0.15 m |
| Crank speed, $N$ | 60 rpm |
| Total head, $H$ | 20 m |
| Actual discharge, $Q_{act}$ | 4.2 LPS |
| Single-acting, single-cylinder | – |

**Find:** (i) Theoretical discharge  (ii) Slip  (iii) % Slip  (iv) Coefficient of discharge  (v) Overall efficiency (assume shaft power = 1.5 kW).

---

### Step 1: Theoretical Discharge

Area of piston: $A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.10)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2$

$$Q_{th} = \frac{A L N}{60} = \frac{7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.15 \times 60}{60}$$

$$Q_{th} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.15 = 1.178 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 1.178\ \text{LPS}$$

---

### Step 2: Slip

$$\text{Slip} = Q_{th} - Q_{act} = 1.178 - 4.2$$

Wait – this is negative, which is impossible! The "negative slip" is a real phenomenon called **negative slip** caused by water inertia at high speeds (the water continues to flow even as the piston reverses).

**Corrected interpretation:** At low speeds $Q_{th} < Q_{act}$ (positive slip); at high speeds $Q_{th} > Q_{act}$ becomes $Q_{act} > Q_{th}$ (negative slip). Here, $Q_{act} > Q_{th}$, so:

$$\text{Slip} = Q_{th} - Q_{act} = 1.178 - 4.2 = -3.022\ \text{LPS (negative slip)}$$

**% Slip** = $-3.022 / 1.178 \times 100 = -256.5\ \text{\%}$ (negative, indicating inertial dominance)

**Coefficient of discharge:**

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{4.2}{1.178} = 3.566\ (>1)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU exam tip:** If $C_d > 1$, write "*Negative slip due to high operating speed – inertial effect dominates*" as a justified explanation to secure full marks.

---

### Step 3: Water Power

$$P_w = \rho g Q_{act} H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 4.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 20 = 824.04\ \text{W}$$

---

### Step 4: Overall Efficiency

$$\eta_{overall} = \frac{P_w}{P_{sh}} = \frac{824.04}{1500} = 0.549 = 54.9\ \text{\%}$$

> A value of 54.9% is low because we neglected mechanical and electrical losses in $P_{sh}$. In practice, a reciprocating pump with $Q_{act} > Q_{th}$ indicates a real loss in efficiency compared to its design point.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Centrifugal Pump Test Rig (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reservoir Sump] -->|Suction pipe| B[Centrifugal Pump]
    B -->|Delivery pipe| C[Control Valve]
    C --> D[Measuring Tank]
    D -->|Overflow back| A
    B -.Shaft.-> E[Coupling]
    E --> F[Variable Speed Motor]
    F --> G[Energy Meter / Rope Brake]
    B --> H[Vacuum Gauge at suction]
    B --> I[Pressure Gauge at delivery]
    G --> J[Tachometer for N]
    D --> K[Stopwatch + Scale for Q]
    L[Manometer Connection] -.Pressure tappings.-> B
```

### Node Function Map

| Node | Function |
|------|----------|
| A | Source reservoir — water at atmospheric pressure |
| B | Centrifugal pump — impeller + casing |
| C | Throttle valve — used to vary operating point and obtain multiple readings |
| D | Volumetric measuring tank — for direct $Q$ measurement |
| F | Prime mover — induction motor, typically 1–3 kW in lab rigs |
| G | Rope-brake dynamometer — to apply known load on shaft |
| H, I | Pressure and vacuum gauges — at pump suction and delivery tappings |
| J | Digital tachometer — to record N in rpm |
| K | Stopwatch + calibrated tank — for time-collected volume |

---

## 4.2 Reciprocating Pump Test Rig (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Sump Tank] -->|Suction pipe with strainer| B1[Suction Valve SV]
    B1 --> C1[Reciprocating Pump Cylinder]
    C1 --> D1[Delivery Valve DV]
    D1 --> E1[Delivery Tank Overhead]
    C1 -->|Crank mechanism| F1[Crank-Connecting Rod Linkage]
    F1 --> G1[Drive Motor with Starter]
    C1 -->|Pressure tappings| H1[Pressure Gauge Delivery Side]
    C1 -->|Vacuum tap| I1[Vacuum Gauge Suction Side]
    D1 --> J1[Measuring Tank for Actual Discharge]
    K1[Energy Meter] -.Electrical Input.-> G1
    L1[Stroboscope or Tachometer] -.Speed N.-> F1
```

---

## 4.3 Functional Flow of Efficiency Calculation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Collect Raw Data] --> S2[Volume V collected in time t → Q = V/t]
    S2 --> S3[Read gauges → compute H_m]
    S3 --> S4[Read N, W, S → compute P_sh]
    S4 --> S5[Compute P_w = ρgQH_m]
    S5 --> S6[Compute η = P_w / P_sh × 100 %]
    S6 --> S7{Vary Q by throttle valve}
    S7 -->|Yes - 6 to 8 readings| S2
    S7 -->|No| S8[Plot H vs Q and η vs Q]
    S8 --> S9[Identify BEP Best Efficiency Point]
    S9 --> S10[End: Report η_max]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q1]**
>
> **Q1.** Define **manometric efficiency** of a centrifugal pump and write its formula.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Manometric efficiency is the ratio of the manometric head to the head imparted by the impeller to the water at its outer periphery.

$$\eta_{manometric} = \frac{\text{Manometric head }(H_m)}{\text{Head imparted by impeller to water} \left(\frac{V_{w2} \cdot u_2}{g}\right)}$$

It measures the effectiveness of conversion of kinetic energy into pressure energy inside the volute casing.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q2]**
>
> **Q2.** What is **slip** in a reciprocating pump? Why can slip be **negative**?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Slip is the difference between the theoretical discharge and the actual discharge of a reciprocating pump: $\text{Slip} = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$.

Negative slip occurs at high operating speeds when the water, due to its inertia, continues to flow into the delivery pipe even after the piston has begun its return stroke. This causes the actual discharge to exceed the theoretical discharge, resulting in $C_d > 1$.

---

## 5.2 Part B – Full 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert – Common Mark Deductions:**
> - Forgetting to **convert Q from LPS to m³/s** (lose 2 marks).
> - Mixing up $W - S$ vs $S - W$ in rope brake formula (lose 2 marks).
> - Forgetting the factor of **2π** in shaft power calculation (lose 2 marks).
> - Not specifying **g = 9.81 m/s²** explicitly (lose 1 mark).
> - Writing "negative slip" without explanation (lose 2 marks).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) – Centrifugal Pump Focus** [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

A centrifugal pump test rig delivers water at the rate of 15 LPS against a total head of 25 m. The pump speed is 1500 rpm. A rope-brake dynamometer on the pump shaft has a brake-drum diameter of 30 cm, a spring balance reading of 25 kgf, and a dead weight of 5 kgf. Compute:
**(a)** the shaft power and overall efficiency of the pump.  **(7 marks)**
**(b)** If the pump is to be tested at 4 different deliveries, outline the **experimental procedure** to determine the characteristic curves (H–Q, η–Q, P–Q).  **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) – 7 marks**

**Given:**
- $Q = 15\ \text{LPS} = 0.015\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$
- $H_m = 25\ \text{m}$
- $N = 1500\ \text{rpm}$
- Brake-drum diameter = 30 cm → $R = 0.15\ \text{m}$
- $W = 25\ \text{kgf}$, $S = 5\ \text{kgf}$

**Step 1: Water Power**

$$P_w = \rho g Q H_m = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.015 \times 25$$

$$P_w = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.375 = 3678.75\ \text{W} = 3.679\ \text{kW}$$

**Step 2: Shaft Power (Rope Brake)**

$$P_{sh} = \frac{2\pi N (W - S) g R}{60}$$

$$P_{sh} = \frac{2 \times \pi \times 1500 \times (25 - 5) \times 9.81 \times 0.15}{60}$$

**Numerator step-by-step:**

- $2\pi \times 1500 = 9424.78$
- $9424.78 \times 20 = 188495.56$
- $188495.56 \times 9.81 = 1849141.45$
- $1849141.45 \times 0.15 = 277371.22$

**Final:**

$$P_{sh} = \frac{277371.22}{60} = 4622.85\ \text{W} = 4.623\ \text{kW}$$

**Step 3: Overall Efficiency**

$$\eta_{overall} = \frac{P_w}{P_{sh}} = \frac{3.679}{4.623} = 0.7958 = \mathbf{79.58\ \%}$$

---

**Part (b) – 7 marks – Experimental Procedure**

1. **Priming:** Fill the pump casing and suction pipe with water to expel air. A centrifugal pump cannot self-prime unless designed as a self-priming unit.
2. **Initial Checks:** Verify all valve positions (delivery valve closed, suction valve open). Switch on the motor and gradually open the delivery valve.
3. **First Reading (Full Delivery):** With the delivery valve fully open, record:
   - Rise in water level in measuring tank over a known time → $Q$
   - Vacuum gauge and pressure gauge readings → $H_m$
   - Spring balance readings $W$ and $S$ → $P_{sh}$
   - Speed $N$ via tachometer
4. **Throttling for Varying Q:** Partially close the delivery valve to obtain 6–8 different flow rates. Repeat all measurements at each setting.
5. **Computation:** Calculate $H_m$, $P_w$, $P_{sh}$, and $\eta$ for each reading.
6. **Plotting:** Plot three characteristic curves on the same graph:
   - $H$ vs $Q$ (Head–Discharge curve)
   - $\eta$ vs $Q$ (Efficiency curve, with peak = BEP)
   - $P$ vs $Q$ (Power curve)
7. **Best Efficiency Point (BEP):** Mark the point on the $Q$–$\eta$ curve where $\eta$ is maximum. This is the recommended operating point for field installation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) – Reciprocating Pump Focus** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

A single-acting, single-cylinder reciprocating pump has a bore of 150 mm and a stroke of 200 mm. It runs at 70 rpm and discharges water to a height of 18 m. The actual discharge measured is 6 LPS.
**(a)** Calculate the **theoretical discharge, slip, % slip**, and **coefficient of discharge**. Comment on the result.  **(7 marks)**
**(b)** If the input shaft power to the pump is 2.5 kW, calculate the **overall efficiency** and explain how you would determine the **mechanical and volumetric efficiencies** separately using an indicator diagram.  **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) – 7 marks**

**Given:** $D = 0.15\ \text{m}$, $L = 0.20\ \text{m}$, $N = 70\ \text{rpm}$, $H = 18\ \text{m}$, $Q_{act} = 6\ \text{LPS} = 0.006\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$.

**Step 1: Piston Area**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.15)^2 = 1.767 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2: Theoretical Discharge**

$$Q_{th} = \frac{A L N}{60} = \frac{1.767 \times 10^{-2} \times 0.20 \times 70}{60}$$

$$Q_{th} = \frac{0.24738}{60} = 4.123 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 4.123\ \text{LPS}$$

**Step 3: Slip**

$$\text{Slip} = Q_{th} - Q_{act} = 4.123 - 6.0 = -1.877\ \text{LPS}$$

**Negative slip** is observed. The operating speed of 70 rpm is high enough that inertial effects dominate, causing water to continue flowing into the delivery pipe even during the suction stroke.

**Step 4: % Slip**

$$\%\ \text{Slip} = \frac{-1.877}{4.123} \times 100 = -45.52\ \text{\%}$$

**Step 5: Coefficient of Discharge**

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{6.0}{4.123} = 1.455$$

Since $C_d > 1$, the pump is operating in the **negative slip regime**.

---

**Part (b) – 7 marks**

**Step 1: Water Power**

$$P_w = \rho g Q_{act} H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.006 \times 18 = 1059.48\ \text{W} \approx 1.06\ \text{kW}$$

**Step 2: Overall Efficiency**

$$\eta_{overall} = \frac{P_w}{P_{sh}} = \frac{1.06}{2.5} = 0.424 = 42.4\ \text{\%}$$

**Step 3: Indicator Diagram Method (for separate efficiencies)**

1. Fix a **Crosby indicator** (or a modern pressure transducer) at the cylinder head to obtain the **indicator diagram** — a plot of pressure inside the cylinder vs. piston displacement.
2. From the indicator diagram, measure the area $A_i$ enclosed by the actual work loop and convert to **indicated power**:

$$P_{indicated} = \frac{A_i \times L \times N \times n}{60}$$

where $n$ = number of working strokes per minute.

3. **Mechanical Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{mechanical} = \frac{P_{indicated}}{P_{shaft}}$$

4. **Volumetric Efficiency (from indicator diagram):** Measured as the ratio of the actual length of the diagram (where pressure is on the delivery side) to the theoretical stroke length. This accounts for valve lag and piston leakage.

$$\eta_{volumetric} = \frac{L_{actual}}{L_{theoretical}}$$

5. **Hydraulic Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{hydraulic} = \frac{\rho g Q_{act} H}{P_{indicated}}$$

6. The product $\eta_{mech} \times \eta_{vol} \times \eta_{hyd}$ should approximately equal $\eta_{overall}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap – Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin this in your lab record / notebook before the exam.**

### Centrifugal Pump Quick-Reference

- ✅ Manometric head: $H_m = (H_d + H_s) + h_v + h_d$  *(neglect velocity heads for small pipes)*
- ✅ Water power: $P_w = \rho g Q H_m$  *(use $Q$ in m³/s)*
- ✅ Shaft power (rope brake): $P_{sh} = \frac{2\pi N (W - S) g R}{60}$
- ✅ Overall efficiency: $\eta = P_w / P_{sh}$
- ✅ Three characteristic curves: $H$–$Q$, $\eta$–$Q$, $P$–$Q$
- ✅ BEP = Best Efficiency Point (peak of $\eta$–$Q$ curve)
- ✅ Specific speed, NPSH, priming – be ready for viva questions

### Reciprocating Pump Quick-Reference

- ✅ Theoretical discharge: $Q_{th} = ALN/60$ (single-acting); $Q_{th} = (2A - a)LN/60$ (double-acting)
- ✅ Slip = $Q_{th} - Q_{act}$  *(positive or negative)*
- ✅ Coefficient of discharge: $C_d = Q_{act} / Q_{th}$
- ✅ % Slip: $\left(\frac{Q_{th} - Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}\right) \times 100$
- ✅ Indicator diagram gives indicated power → mechanical efficiency
- ✅ Negative slip explanation: water inertia at high N

### Common Conversion Factors (Memorize!)

| From | To | Multiply by |
|------|-----|-------------|
| LPS (litres/s) | m³/s | $10^{-3}$ |
| LPM (litres/min) | LPS | $1/60$ |
| kgf·m/s | Watts | 9.81 |
| Metric HP | Watts | 735.5 |
| Bar | m of water | 10.197 |
| psi | m of water | 0.703 |

> **Final Exam Tip:** Always quote the answer with **3 significant figures** and **units**. KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of the friction factor for pipes of different sizes and materials in different ranges of Reynolds number
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL308 - FLUID & PARTICLE MECHANICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of the friction factor for pipes of different sizes and materials in different ranges of Reynolds number

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Friction Factor (f):** A dimensionless quantity that characterizes the resistance to fluid flow offered by the internal surface of a pipe. It encapsulates the combined effect of viscous shear and wall roughness on the pressure drop per unit length of the pipe.

In the **Darcy-Weisbach formulation** (the standard adopted by KTU), the friction factor $f_D$ relates the frictional head loss $h_f$ to the dynamic pressure of the flow:

$$h_f = \frac{f_D \, L \, v^2}{2 \, g \, D}$$

where $L$ is the pipe length, $D$ is the internal diameter, $v$ is the average (superficial) fluid velocity, and $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reynolds Number (Re):** The fundamental dimensionless grouping that predicts whether a flow regime is **laminar (Re < 2000)**, **transitional (2000 < Re < 4000)**, or **turbulent (Re > 4000)**. The friction factor is a *strong* function of Re, and additionally a function of relative roughness $\varepsilon/D$ in the turbulent regime.

$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho \, v \, D}{\mu} = \frac{4 \, Q}{\pi \, D \, \mu}$$

where $\rho$ is the fluid density (**kg/m³**), $\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity (**Pa·s** or **N·s/m²**), and $Q$ is the volumetric flow rate (**m³/s**).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Slippery vs. Rusted Pipe" Intuition

Imagine you are drinking a thick milkshake (high viscosity, like laminar flow) through two straws:

- **Straw A** is a wide, smooth glass tube.
- **Straw B** is a narrow, slightly rusted steel tube.

Even with the same milkshake, **Straw B requires much more sucking effort** because:
1. The small diameter increases the wall-to-fluid interaction.
2. The rust (roughness) creates tiny turbulent eddies that resist motion.

The **friction factor** is essentially a numerical "resistance index" that quantifies exactly *how much* harder it is to push fluid through pipe B compared to pipe A. By running water at different flow rates, we sweep through different **Reynolds number regimes** and watch how this resistance index behaves — for smooth pipes, it follows a clean mathematical curve; for rough pipes, the curve deviates at higher Re.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Lab Insight:** This experiment does **NOT** measure a single number. It produces a *curve* — a plot of $\log(f)$ vs. $\log(\text{Re})$ — which is then compared against the **Moody Chart**, a universal engineering reference for pipe-flow design.

## 1.3 Why Pipe Material & Size Both Matter

| Variable | Physical Meaning | Engineering Impact |
|---|---|---|
| **Pipe Diameter ($D$)** | Sets the velocity $v$ for a given $Q$ | Smaller $D$ → higher $v$ → higher Re → different regime |
| **Pipe Material (Roughness $\varepsilon$)** | Surface micro-irregularity height | Cast iron ($\varepsilon \approx 0.26$ mm) vs. drawn copper ($\varepsilon \approx 0.0015$ mm) |
| **Relative Roughness ($\varepsilon/D$)** | Ratio of roughness to diameter | Determines where the friction curve "levels off" at high Re |

Standard roughness values for KTU-lab materials (typical):

- **Galvanized Iron (GI)**: $\varepsilon \approx 0.15$ mm
- **Commercial Steel**: $\varepsilon \approx 0.045$ mm
- **Drawn Copper / Brass**: $\varepsilon \approx 0.0015$ mm
- **PVC / Plastic**: $\varepsilon \approx 0.0$ mm (hydraulically smooth)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Step-by-Step Logic

The experiment rests on **two coupled governing equations** plus **one dimensionless ratio**:

1. **Continuity Equation (mass conservation)** — gives the velocity from measured flow rate.
2. **Darcy-Weisbach Equation** — gives the friction factor from measured pressure drop.
3. **Reynolds Number** — classifies the flow regime.

### Step 1: Measure Volumetric Flow Rate ($Q$)
The collecting tank + stopwatch method (or a rotameter) gives $Q$ in m³/s. Velocity follows from continuity:

$$v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{4 \, Q}{\pi \, D^2}$$

### Step 2: Measure Pressure Drop ($\Delta P$ or $\Delta h$)
Using a **U-tube manometer** connected across two tappings separated by length $L$:

$$\Delta P = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \, g \, h_m$$

where $\rho_m$ is manometric fluid density (mercury = **13,600 kg/m³** for high-pressure drops, or carbon tetrachloride/water for low drops), $\rho_f$ is the working fluid density, and $h_m$ is the manometer deflection.

### Step 3: Compute the Friction Factor
Rearranging the Darcy-Weisbach equation for $f_D$:

$$f_D = \frac{2 \, g \, D \, \Delta h}{L \, v^2}$$

or in differential manometric form (eliminating the $2g$ factor):

$$f_D = \frac{2 \, D \, (\rho_m - \rho_f) \, h_m}{L \, \rho_f \, v^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention Pitfall (examiner-trap):** $h_f$ is the *loss*, so $h_f > 0$ means $\Delta P$ must be the **upstream-minus-downstream** static pressure difference. Many students inadvertently invert this and get a negative $f$, losing full marks.

### Step 4: Compute Reynolds Number
$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho_f \, v \, D}{\mu_f}$$

For water at **27 °C (300 K)**: $\rho_f \approx 996$ kg/m³, $\mu_f \approx 8.5 \times 10^{-4}$ Pa·s. The lab manual usually supplies temperature-corrected values to avoid viscosity-lookup errors.

### Step 5: Classify the Regime and Verify the Theory
- If $\text{Re} < 2000$ (laminar) → plot must satisfy $f_D = 64/\text{Re}$ exactly.
- If $\text{Re} > 4000$ (turbulent) → plot must approach the **smooth-pipe curve** (Blasius: $f = 0.316 \, \text{Re}^{-0.25}$) for plastic/copper pipes, or the **fully-rough asymptote** for GI pipes at very high Re.

## 2.2 The Moody Chart — The Engineer's "Refrigerator Magnet"

The **Moody Chart** plots $f_D$ vs. $\text{Re}$ on log-log axes, with a family of parametric curves for different relative roughness $\varepsilon/D$. It is the single most important reference for any pipe-flow design problem. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to be able to:

1. Read $f$ from the chart given Re and $\varepsilon/D$.
2. Solve the inverse problem: given $Q$, $L$, $D$, $\varepsilon$, and allowable $\Delta P$, find the required pump head.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Table** — Memorize the boxed expressions and their valid regimes. KTU valuations award full credit only when the *correct regime* is explicitly stated in the answer.

| # | Formula | Application / Regime | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ | Average velocity from flow rate | m/s |
| 2 | $\text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Regime classification | dimensionless |
| 3 | $h_f = \dfrac{f_D \, L \, v^2}{2 g D}$ | Darcy-Weisbach head loss | m |
| 4 | $f_D = \dfrac{2 g D \, h_f}{L \, v^2}$ | Experimental $f_D$ from measurements | dimensionless |
| 5 | $f_D = \dfrac{64}{\text{Re}}$ | Laminar flow theoretical check | $\text{Re} < 2000$ |
| 6 | $f_D = 0.316 \, \text{Re}^{-0.25}$ | Blasius equation (smooth turbulent) | $4000 < \text{Re} < 10^5$ |
| 7 | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f_D}} = -2.0 \log\!\left( \dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{2.51}{\text{Re} \, \sqrt{f_D}} \right)$ | Colebrook-White (implicit, all regimes) | Universal |
| 8 | $\Delta P = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \, g \, h_m$ | Manometer equation | Pa |
| 9 | $\varepsilon / D$ | Relative roughness | dimensionless |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Water Distribution Networks (Kerala PWD / KWA):** Every pump station design begins with a Moody-chart lookup. The choice between ductile iron and HDPE pipe hinges entirely on which gives a lower $f$ at the operating Re.
- **Chemical Plant Piping:** Corrosion increases $\varepsilon$ with time. Engineers design with a *future* roughness value (e.g., 1–2 mm) to ensure adequate capacity over the 30-year plant life.
- **HVAC & Refrigeration:** Small-bore copper tubes operate at high Re. Even a 0.001 mm roughness drift in $f$ translates to a measurable kW loss in compressor power across an entire building.
- **Oil & Gas Trunk Lines:** Long-distance pipelines operate in the **fully-rough regime** where $f$ becomes *independent of Re*. Designers use the explicit **Nikuradse** or **Moody** approximation:

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2.0 \log\!\left( \frac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} \right)$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Friction Factor from Manometer + Rotameter Data

### Setup
Consider a horizontal test pipe of internal diameter $D$ and length $L$ between two manometer tappings. A differential manometer (mercury for water flows) reads a deflection $h_m$. The volumetric flow rate $Q$ is logged from a rotameter or measuring cylinder.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Apply the Mechanical Energy Balance (Bernoulli + losses) between tappings 1 and 2.**

For a horizontal pipe, the elevation term cancels, and assuming no pump or turbine between tappings:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho_f g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho_f g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + h_f$$

**Step 2: Recognize constant diameter, hence constant velocity.**

Since $D_1 = D_2$, continuity demands $v_1 = v_2 = v$. The kinetic-energy terms cancel:

$$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho_f g} = h_f$$

**Step 3: Express the pressure drop from the manometer reading.**

For a U-tube manometer with manometric fluid denser than the working fluid:

$$P_1 - P_2 = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \, g \, h_m$$

Substituting:

$$h_f = \frac{(\rho_m - \rho_f) \, g \, h_m}{\rho_f \, g} = \frac{(\rho_m - \rho_f) \, h_m}{\rho_f}$$

**Step 4: Equate to the Darcy-Weisbach definition.**

$$h_f = \frac{f_D \, L \, v^2}{2 \, g \, D}$$

Setting the two expressions for $h_f$ equal:

$$\frac{f_D \, L \, v^2}{2 \, g \, D} = \frac{(\rho_m - \rho_f) \, h_m}{\rho_f}$$

**Step 5: Solve explicitly for $f_D$.**

$$f_D = \frac{2 \, g \, D \, (\rho_m - \rho_f) \, h_m}{L \, \rho_f \, v^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Note for Exam:** In the differential-manometer form, the factor of $2$ in the numerator (as opposed to the head-form expression $2gDh_f/Lv^2$) is **NOT** a typo — it arises because the manometer equation already encodes the $g$ of the working fluid. Examiners specifically test whether students keep the $(\rho_m - \rho_f)$ vs. $\rho_f$ ratio straight.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Theoretical Laminar $f$ from First Principles (Hagen-Poiseuille)

**Step 1: Force balance on a cylindrical fluid element of radius $r$ and length $L$.**

The driving pressure force $\Delta P \, \pi r^2$ must equal the viscous shear force $\tau \, (2 \pi r L)$:

$$\Delta P \, \pi r^2 = - \tau \, (2 \pi r L)$$

(The negative sign reflects that $\tau$ acts opposite to flow direction.)

**Step 2: Use Newton's law of viscosity.**

$$\tau = \mu \left( -\frac{dv}{dr} \right)$$

Substituting:

$$\Delta P \, \pi r^2 = 2 \pi r L \, \mu \, \frac{dv}{dr}$$

**Step 3: Separate variables and integrate.**

$$dv = \frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} \, r \, dr$$

Integrating from $r$ to the wall ($r = R$, $v = 0$):

$$\int_v^0 dv = \frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} \int_r^R r \, dr$$

$$-v = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} (R^2 - r^2)$$

**Step 4: Recover the parabolic velocity profile.**

$$v(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} (R^2 - r^2)$$

**Step 5: Find the average velocity.**

$$v_{avg} = \frac{1}{\pi R^2} \int_0^R v(r) \, 2 \pi r \, dr = \frac{\Delta P}{8 \mu L}$$

This is the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation**.

**Step 6: Convert to friction factor.**

Equating $v_{avg} = v$ in the Darcy-Weisbach form $h_f = \Delta P / (\rho g) = f_D L v^2 / (2 g D)$ and using $D = 2R$:

$$f_D = \frac{64 \, \mu}{\rho \, v \, D} = \frac{64}{\text{Re}}$$

This **exact result** is the gold-standard check for any laminar-flow experimental data point in the lab.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

> **Given:** A horizontal commercial-steel pipe of $D = 25$ mm, $L = 2.0$ m is tested with water at 30 °C ($\rho = 995.7$ kg/m³, $\mu = 7.98 \times 10^{-4}$ Pa·s). Mercury manometer reads $h_m = 0.085$ m. Rotameter shows $Q = 1.2 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s. Take $\varepsilon = 0.045$ mm for commercial steel.

### Solution

**Step 1: Compute velocity.**

$$v = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2} = \frac{4 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-4}}{\pi \times (0.025)^2} = \frac{4.8 \times 10^{-4}}{1.963 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.2445 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute Reynolds number.**

$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{995.7 \times 0.2445 \times 0.025}{7.98 \times 10^{-4}} = 7628$$

Since $\text{Re} > 4000$, the flow is **turbulent**.

**Step 3: Compute pressure drop from manometer.**

$$\Delta P = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \, g \, h_m = (13600 - 995.7) \times 9.81 \times 0.085 = 10\,511 \; \text{Pa}$$

**Step 4: Compute experimental friction factor.**

$$f_{D,\text{exp}} = \frac{2 \, D \, \Delta P}{L \, \rho_f \, v^2} = \frac{2 \times 0.025 \times 10\,511}{2.0 \times 995.7 \times (0.2445)^2}$$

$$f_{D,\text{exp}} = \frac{525.55}{119.04} = 4.415 \times 10^{-2} = 0.0442$$

**Step 5: Verify with Colebrook equation.**

$$\varepsilon/D = 0.045/25 = 0.0018$$

Colebrook:

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2.0 \log\!\left( \frac{0.0018}{3.7} + \frac{2.51}{7628 \sqrt{0.0442}} \right)$$

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{0.0442}} = \frac{1}{0.2102} = 4.757$$

$$-2.0 \log(0.000486 + 0.00560) = -2.0 \log(0.00608) = -2.0 \times (-2.216) = 4.433$$

$$f = \left( \frac{1}{4.433} \right)^2 = (0.2256)^2 = 0.0509$$

The experimental value (0.0442) and theoretical value (0.0509) agree to within ~13%, which is acceptable for a student-grade apparatus; the small discrepancy is due to entrance/exit losses, tappings location, and instrument tolerance.

**Step 6: Cross-check with Blasius (smooth-pipe approximation).**

$$f_{\text{Blasius}} = 0.316 \times (7628)^{-0.25} = 0.316 \times 0.1898 = 0.0600$$

Higher than the actual because the steel is rough; the Blasius curve under-predicts $f$ for a rough pipe, as expected.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Experimental Setup — Block Functional Architecture

The physical apparatus consists of a sump tank, pump, control valve, rotameter, test pipe section with manometer tappings, differential manometer, and a collection/measuring tank. The diagram below maps the functional flow of fluid and signals.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SW1: Sump Tank] -->|Suction line| B[SW2: Centrifugal Pump]
    B -->|Delivery line| C[SW3: Bypass Valve]
    C -->|Flow control| D[SW4: Globe Valve]
    D -->|Test flow path| E[SW5: Rotameter]
    E -->|Calibrated Q| F[SW6: Inlet Tank with Stilling Section]
    F -->|Inlet length 50D| G[SW7: Test Pipe Section]
    G -->|Length L between tappings| H[SW8: Outlet Tank]
    H -->|Discharge| I[SW9: Measuring Cylinder and Stopwatch]
    J[SW10: Differential U-tube Manometer] -.->|Pressure tap 1| G
    J -.->|Pressure tap 2| G
    K[SW11: Thermometer at Inlet Tank] --> F
    L[SW12: Pipe Sample Set 1 GI 25mm] -.-> G
    M[SW13: Pipe Sample Set 2 Copper 15mm] -.-> G
    N[SW14: Pipe Sample Set 3 PVC 20mm] -.-> G
```

**Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows (`-->`) represent the *fluid flow path*; dashed arrows (`-.->`) represent *instrumentation signal lines* (pressure taps, temperature sensing, or interchangeable test sections).

## 4.2 Procedure Flowchart — Step-by-Step Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Power ON pump, fill sump] --> S1[Prime test pipe to remove air pockets]
    S1 --> S2[Set globe valve to maximum flow position]
    S2 --> S3[Wait 2 minutes for steady state]
    S3 --> S4[Record Q from rotameter, hm from manometer, T from thermometer]
    S4 --> S5[Partially close globe valve to reduce flow]
    S5 --> S6[Wait 1 minute, record new set of Q hm T]
    S6 --> S7{More flow settings?}
    S7 -->|Yes| S5
    S7 -->|No| S8[Repeat for second test pipe: change section, reprime]
    S8 --> S9[Repeat for third test pipe]
    S9 --> S10[Compute v, Re, fD for every reading]
    S10 --> S11[Plot log fD vs log Re on log-log graph]
    S11 --> S12[Overlay Moody chart curves for the corresponding epsilon over D]
    S12 --> S13[Compare with f equals 64 over Re line in laminar zone]
    S13 --> S14[Calculate percentage deviation, tabulate, end]
```
