
## 4.3 Data-Interpretation Matrix

| Observation in Lab | Physical Cause | Expected Trend on Plot |
|---|---|---|
| $f_D$ falls steeply as Re increases at low Re | Viscous-dominated laminar flow | $f = 64/\text{Re}$ straight line on log-log with slope $-1$ |
| Slope of $f$ vs. Re plot flattens at moderate Re | Transition to turbulent; inertia dominates | Slope $\approx -0.25$ (Blasius) |
| Curves for different materials separate at high Re | Wall roughness begins to dominate viscous effects | Plateaux at $\varepsilon/D$-dependent values |
| Curve for GI lies above curve for copper at high Re | Higher roughness of GI | Higher $f$ for same Re |
| Curve for PVC stays close to smooth-pipe Blasius | PVC is hydraulically smooth | Follows $0.316 \, \text{Re}^{-0.25}$ |
| Manometer reading becomes erratic at very high Q | Pump pulsations + secondary flows | Scatter band widens |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Reading:** The fact that all curves *converge* at low Re is the single most important qualitative observation in this experiment. It proves that at low Reynolds numbers, the flow "sees" only the fluid viscosity and not the wall — which is precisely why viscometers use laminar flow in narrow capillaries.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define the Darcy friction factor. How is it related to the Fanning friction factor?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Darcy friction factor ($f_D$)** is a dimensionless quantity appearing in the Darcy-Weisbach equation for head loss due to pipe friction:

$$h_f = \frac{f_D \, L \, v^2}{2 \, g \, D}$$

> The **Fanning friction factor ($f_F$)** is an alternative (older) definition, four times smaller:

$$h_f = \frac{4 \, f_F \, L \, v^2}{2 \, g \, D}$$

> Hence the relation is: $\boxed{f_D = 4 \, f_F}$ or equivalently $f_F = f_D / 4$. Both are dimensionless. The Darcy form is preferred in modern chemical and civil engineering; the Fanning form persists in some petroleum and food-engineering texts. **[3 marks]**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State the Hagen-Poiseuille equation. Under what conditions is it strictly valid?**

**Model Answer:**

> The Hagen-Poiseuille equation gives the pressure drop for steady, fully-developed, laminar flow of an incompressible Newtonian fluid in a circular pipe:

$$\Delta P = \frac{32 \, \mu \, L \, v}{D^2} = \frac{128 \, \mu \, L \, Q}{\pi \, D^4}$$

> Validity conditions:
> 1. **Laminar regime** ($\text{Re} < 2000$).
> 2. **Steady state** (time-invariant).
> 3. **Newtonian fluid** (constant $\mu$).
> 4. **Incompressible flow** (constant $\rho$).
> 5. **Fully developed** (no entrance effects, taken as $L > 0.05 \, D \cdot \text{Re}$).
> 6. **Rigid, smooth, circular cross-section** of constant diameter.
> 7. **No-slip at the wall**.

> It follows that the laminar friction factor is $f_D = 64/\text{Re}$. **[3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** A horizontal test pipe of internal diameter $D = 0.020$ m and length $L = 1.5$ m is connected to a water-mercury differential manometer. For a flow rate of $Q = 4.0 \times 10^{-5}$ m³/s, the manometer deflection is $h_m = 0.18$ m. Water properties at the operating temperature are $\rho = 998$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s. Mercury density = 13,600 kg/m³. Determine:
   (i) The average velocity and the Reynolds number.
   (ii) The experimental Darcy friction factor $f_D$.
   (iii) The theoretical friction factor assuming the flow is fully turbulent and the pipe is smooth. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** Explain the construction and use of the **Moody Chart**. Discuss how the friction factor varies with Reynolds number and relative roughness in:
   (i) the laminar regime,
   (ii) the smooth-turbulent regime,
   (iii) the fully-rough turbulent regime. **\[7 marks\]**

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** With the aid of a labelled schematic, describe the experimental procedure to determine the friction factor for pipes of different materials (GI, copper, PVC) across different Reynolds number ranges. List the apparatus, the observations, and the calculations. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** For a fully-rough commercial steel pipe ($\varepsilon = 0.045$ mm) of diameter $D = 50$ mm carrying water at a velocity of 2.0 m/s, calculate:
   (i) The Reynolds number and the flow regime.
   (ii) The Darcy friction factor using the Colebrook equation.
   (iii) The pressure drop per metre of pipe length. **\[7 marks\]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing up Darcy and Fanning factors.** If the question asks for $f_D$ and you give $f_F$, you lose **all** derivation marks. Always state the definition you are using in the first line of the answer.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\rho$ and $g$ in the manometer equation.** Many students write $\Delta P = \rho_m h_m$ — this is **dimensionally wrong** (units of kg/m²) and the examiner deducts **1 mark per occurrence**.
> 3. **Not classifying the regime before applying $f = 64/\text{Re}$ or Blasius.** A common KTU trap: a question gives a flow that *appears* laminar (e.g., a small pipe and small $Q$), but the actual Re is 3500 — transitional. The correct answer is *neither* laminar nor Blasius, but the **Colebrook** result. Always compute Re **first**.
> 4. **Plotting on linear graph paper instead of log-log.** The entire physics of the Moody chart (the linear $64/\text{Re}$ region) is visible *only* on log-log axes. KTU rubric explicitly demands the log-log plot for full marks on graph-based questions.
> 5. **Omitting the entrance-length correction.** Real lab data shows $f$ slightly higher than theory at high Re due to incomplete development. Mentioning this in the discussion earns bonus marks.

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions to Question A

### Solution to Q-A (a)

**(i) Velocity and Reynolds Number**

$$v = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2} = \frac{4 \times 4.0 \times 10^{-5}}{\pi \times (0.020)^2} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-4}}{1.2566 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1273 \; \text{m/s}$$

$$\text{Re} = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{998 \times 0.1273 \times 0.020}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 2542$$

**Interpretation:** $\text{Re} = 2542$ lies in the **transitional regime** ($2000 < \text{Re} < 4000$). The Colebrook equation (or a Moody chart reading) is the most appropriate theoretical reference here. **\[3 marks\]**

**(ii) Experimental Friction Factor**

First, the pressure drop from the manometer:

$$\Delta P = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \, g \, h_m = (13600 - 998) \times 9.81 \times 0.18 = 22\,252 \; \text{Pa}$$

Then the friction factor:

$$f_{D,\text{exp}} = \frac{2 \, D \, \Delta P}{L \, \rho_f \, v^2} = \frac{2 \times 0.020 \times 22\,252}{1.5 \times 998 \times (0.1273)^2}$$

$$f_{D,\text{exp}} = \frac{890.1}{24.27} = 0.0367$$

**\[2 marks\]**

**(iii) Theoretical Smooth-Pipe Friction Factor (Blasius)**

$$f_{\text{Blasius}} = 0.316 \times \text{Re}^{-0.25} = 0.316 \times (2542)^{-0.25} = 0.316 \times 0.1408 = 0.0445$$

> **Note for the student:** Blasius is technically only valid for $\text{Re} < 10^5$, so applying it at the transitional boundary is a *rough approximation*. The more rigorous answer would solve the Colebrook equation, but Blasius is acceptable here because the problem specifies "fully turbulent and smooth". Since the actual regime is transitional, the Blasius value **over-predicts** the friction factor compared to the experimental value — a useful diagnostic. **\[2 marks\]**

---

### Solution to Q-B (b) — Friction Factor for Fully-Rough Steel Pipe

**Given:** $\varepsilon = 0.045$ mm $= 4.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m, $D = 50$ mm $= 0.050$ m, $v = 2.0$ m/s, water at ~25 °C ($\rho \approx 997$ kg/m³, $\mu \approx 8.9 \times 10^{-4}$ Pa·s).

**(i) Reynolds Number and Regime**

$$\text{Re} = \frac{997 \times 2.0 \times 0.050}{8.9 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{99.7}{8.9 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.12 \times 10^5$$

Since $\text{Re} > 4000$, flow is **fully turbulent**. **\[2 marks\]**

**(ii) Colebrook Iteration**

Relative roughness:

$$\frac{\varepsilon}{D} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^{-5}}{0.050} = 9.0 \times 10^{-4}$$

Initial guess: assume fully-rough asymptote (no Re term):

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f_0}} = -2.0 \log\!\left( \frac{9.0 \times 10^{-4}}{3.7} \right) = -2.0 \log(2.432 \times 10^{-4}) = -2.0 \times (-3.614) = 7.228$$

$$f_0 = (1/7.228)^2 = 0.01913$$

Refine with Colebrook (including the Re term):

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f_1}} = -2.0 \log\!\left( \frac{9.0 \times 10^{-4}}{3.7} + \frac{2.51}{1.12 \times 10^5 \times \sqrt{0.01913}} \right)$$

$$= -2.0 \log(2.432 \times 10^{-4} + 1.624 \times 10^{-4}) = -2.0 \log(4.056 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$= -2.0 \times (-3.392) = 6.784$$

$$f_1 = (1/6.784)^2 = 0.02173$$

Second iteration:

$$\frac{2.51}{1.12 \times 10^5 \times \sqrt{0.02173}} = 1.518 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f_2}} = -2.0 \log(2.432 \times 10^{-4} + 1.518 \times 10^{-4}) = -2.0 \log(3.95 \times 10^{-4}) = 6.807$$

$$f_2 = (1/6.807)^2 = 0.02158$$

The solution has converged:

$$\boxed{f_D \approx 0.0216}$$ **\[3 marks\]**

**(iii) Pressure Drop per Metre**

$$\frac{\Delta P}{L} = \frac{f_D \, \rho \, v^2}{2 D} = \frac{0.0216 \times 997 \times (2.0)^2}{2 \times 0.050} = \frac{86.14}{0.100} = 861.4 \; \text{Pa/m}$$

**\[2 marks\]**

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Viva + ESE**

- [ ] **Reynolds Number formula** is $\text{Re} = \rho v D / \mu = 4Q/(\pi D \mu)$. The second form is preferred when only flow rate is measured.
- [ ] **Regime boundaries:** Laminar $\text{Re} < 2000$, Transitional $2000 \le \text{Re} \le 4000$, Turbulent $\text{Re} > 4000$.
- [ ] **Darcy vs Fanning:** $f_D = 4 f_F$. Always verify which one the question (and the textbook) is using before computing.
- [ ] **Laminar check:** $f_D = 64/\text{Re}$. If your experimental $f_D$ deviates by more than ~15% from this line at low Re, suspect instrument error or air entrainment.
- [ ] **Blasius check (smooth-pipe, turbulent):** $f_D = 0.316 \, \text{Re}^{-0.25}$, valid for $4000 < \text{Re} < 10^5$.
- [ ] **Colebrook equation** is the *universal* predictor for $f_D$, but it is **implicit** — must be solved iteratively. Start with the fully-rough asymptote as the first guess.
- [ ] **Manometer equation** must include the $(\rho_m - \rho_f)$ ratio and a $g$ — never omit the $g$ or write $\Delta P = \rho_m h_m$ without $g$.
- [ ] **Log-log plot** of $f_D$ vs. Re is **mandatory** in the lab record. Label axes with units, mark the data points, draw the best-fit line, and overlay the theoretical $64/\text{Re}$ line for comparison.
- [ ] **Relative roughness** $\varepsilon / D$ is *dimensionless* and is the **key parameter** in the turbulent-plateau region. Memorize at least three values: GI $\approx 0.15$ mm, commercial steel $\approx 0.045$ mm, drawn copper $\approx 0.0015$ mm.
- [ ] **Entrance length** is approximately $L_e = 0.06 \, D \cdot \text{Re}$ (laminar) or $L_e = 4.4 \, D \cdot \text{Re}^{1/6}$ (turbulent). The test section must be longer than $L_e$ to ensure fully-developed flow.
- [ ] **Moody Chart** must be drawn / overlaid correctly: $f_D$ on the $y$-axis, Re on the $x$-axis, parametric curves for $\varepsilon/D = 0$, $0.0001$, $0.001$, $0.01$, $0.05$, $0.1$, $0.2$, $0.5$.
- [ ] **Units consistency:** all lengths in **metres**, all pressures in **Pa**, all flow rates in **m³/s** when using SI. Mixing CGS and SI is a guaranteed mark-loss.
- [ ] **Lab safety:** vent the manometer before disassembly; mercury spills require a specific clean-up protocol (sulfite powder + specialized vacuum pickup).
- [ ] **Typical KTU viva questions:** "What is the physical significance of $f$ being constant in the fully-rough regime?" (Answer: viscous sublayer is *thinner* than the roughness elements, so wall texture alone dictates drag.) "Why does the $f$-Re curve not depend on pipe material at low Re?" (Answer: the viscous sublayer is much thicker than the roughness, so the wall is 'invisible' to the bulk flow.)
- [ ] **Plot interpretation tip:** If your experimental $f$-Re curve for the GI pipe sits *above* the copper curve at the same Re, you have correctly demonstrated the **roughness effect** — a guaranteed 2-mark bonus on viva.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calibration of a Rotameter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL308 - FLUID & PARTICLE MECHANICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Calibration of a Rotameter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rotameter (Variable Area Flowmeter):** A Rotameter is a **variable area flow measuring instrument** consisting of a vertically mounted, gradually tapered transparent tube (conical bore) and a free-floating "float" (also called a *plumb-bob* or *piston*) whose vertical position is a function of the volumetric flow rate of the fluid passing upward through the tube.

**Calibration of a Rotameter** is the experimental procedure of establishing the relationship between the **indicated flow rate** (or float height `h`) of the rotameter and the **actual (true) flow rate** of the fluid (typically water) as determined by an independent primary standard — usually a **collecting tank and stopwatch method** (gravimetric / volumetric timing).

The end product of the calibration is a **Calibration Curve** (also called the *characteristic curve* or *flow curve*): a plot of *Actual Volumetric Flow Rate (Q)* on the Y-axis versus *Rotameter Scale Reading (h)* on the X-axis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is calibration necessary?**
> - Manufacturer-supplied rotameter scales are calibrated for **air at STP (Standard Temperature and Pressure: 20 °C, 1.013 bar)** or **water at 20 °C**.
> - For any other fluid (density, viscosity, temperature different), the float position for a given flow rate will differ.
> - Hence, a **lab-scale, in-situ calibration with the working fluid (water)** is mandatory before the rotameter is used for process measurements.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **marble dropped inside a transparent, slightly flared drinking straw** held vertically, with water flowing *upwards* through the straw.

- When **no water** flows → the marble simply **sits at the bottom**, resting on a stop.
- When a **small stream** of water is pushed up → the marble is **lifted slightly** because the upward drag force just balances the net downward force (weight − buoyancy).
- When you **increase the water flow** → the marble rises **higher** up the flared tube. Because the tube is wider at the top, a larger **annular gap** opens up between the marble and the tube wall, allowing more water to escape → equilibrium is reached at a *higher* position.
- The **height of the marble** is therefore a direct, visual indicator of the **flow rate**.

| Component in Analogy | Actual Rotameter Part | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Drinking straw (flared) | Conical borosilicate glass tube | Variable area channel |
| Marble | Stainless-steel / lead float | Flow-sensing element |
| Tap water flow | Process fluid | Measurable variable |
| Height of marble in straw | Scale reading `h` (mm) | Indicated flow output |
| Stopwatch + measuring jar | Collecting tank + stopwatch | Primary standard (reference) |

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> Standard reference values used during rotameter calibration with water:
> - **Density of water at 20 °C (ρ_w): 998.2 kg/m³** (often approximated as **1000 kg/m³**)
> - **Acceleration due to gravity (g): 9.81 m/s²**
> - **Stainless-steel float density (ρ_f): ≈ 7800 – 8000 kg/m³** (depends on grade)
> - **Cross-sectional area of float (A_f):** provided by manufacturer (typically 1–5 cm²)
> - **Volume of float (V_f):** provided by manufacturer
> - **Taper angle of the tube (θ):** typically 1:250 to 1:50 (very shallow)

---

## 2.1 Working Principle — Force Balance on the Float

When the fluid flows upward through the rotameter, the float reaches a stable position where the **net downward force** is balanced by the **upward fluid drag force**.

**Forces acting on the float (in the vertical direction):**

1. **Weight of the float (downward):** $W = V_f \times \rho_f \times g$

2. **Buoyancy force (upward):** $F_B = V_f \times \rho_m \times g$

3. **Pressure (drag) force exerted by the fluid (upward):** $F_D = \Delta P \times A_f = (P_1 - P_2) \times A_f$

Where:
- $P_1$ = pressure of fluid below the float
- $P_2$ = pressure of fluid above the float
- $\Delta P$ = pressure drop across the float = $P_1 - P_2$
- $\rho_m$ = density of the flowing fluid (manometric fluid = water here)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equilibrium Condition:** At steady-state, the float is stationary. Applying Newton's first law (ΣF = 0):
>
> $$\text{Downward forces} = \text{Upward forces}$$
>
> $$V_f \, \rho_f \, g = V_f \, \rho_m \, g + \Delta P \, A_f$$
>
> $$\boxed{\Delta P = \frac{V_f \, (\rho_f - \rho_m) \, g}{A_f}}$$

This shows that the **pressure drop across the float is constant** for a given float and fluid, regardless of flow rate.

## 2.2 Volumetric Flow Rate Equation

Applying **Bernoulli's equation** between the bottom (point 1) and top (point 2) of the float, with the float in mechanical equilibrium, the volumetric flow rate is given by:

$$\boxed{Q = C_d \, A_a \, \sqrt{\frac{2 \, \Delta P}{\rho_m}} = C_d \, A_a \, \sqrt{\frac{2 \, V_f \, (\rho_f - \rho_m) \, g}{A_f \, \rho_m}}}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/s)
- $C_d$ = **coefficient of discharge** (dimensionless, typically **0.60 – 0.98**)
- $A_a$ = **annular area** between the float and the tube wall at the float's equilibrium height (m²)
- $A_f$ = maximum cross-sectional area of the float (m²)
- $V_f$ = volume of the float (m³)
- $\rho_f$ = density of the float material (kg/m³)
- $\rho_m$ = density of the metered fluid (kg/m³)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (9.81 m/s²)

The **annular area** $A_a$ depends linearly on the float height $h$ because the tube is conical:

$$A_a = A_f \, \left(1 + K \, h\right)$$

Where $K$ is a geometric constant of the tube (depends on taper angle). Therefore:

$$\boxed{Q \;\propto\; \sqrt{A_a} \;\propto\; \sqrt{h}}$$

This is why a **square-root scale** is often printed on commercial rotameters.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table — Calibration of a Rotameter**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{V_{\text{collected}}}{t}$ | Actual flow rate from collecting tank & stopwatch | m³/s or L/s |
| 2 | $Q_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{\text{Rise in water level} \times A_{\text{tank}}}{t}$ | Alternative using tank cross-section | m³/s |
| 3 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{V_f \, (\rho_f - \rho_m) \, g}{A_f}$ | Pressure drop across the float (constant) | Pa (N/m²) |
| 4 | $Q = C_d \, A_a \, \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, \Delta P}{\rho_m}}$ | Theoretical flow rate through the rotameter | m³/s |
| 5 | $A_a = A_f \, (1 + K h)$ | Annular area as a function of float height | m² |
| 6 | $Q \propto \sqrt{h}$ | Linear relationship after squaring | — |
| 7 | $\text{Reynolds No.} = \dfrac{\rho_m \, v \, D_h}{\mu}$ | To check flow regime (laminar/turbulent) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $D_h = \dfrac{4 A_a}{\text{wetted perimeter}}$ | Hydraulic diameter of annular region | m |
| 9 | $\% \text{ Error} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{indicated}} - Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\text{actual}}} \times 100$ | Accuracy / deviation calculation | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Careful with the pipe character!** In all exam answer sheets, use `mod`, `vert`, or write out "magnitude of" to avoid markdown parsing issues when copying tables.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Rotameter Calibration

| Industry | Application |
|---|---|
| **Chemical Process Plants** | Monitoring acid/alkali flow in pipelines |
| **Water Treatment Plants** | Dosing chlorine, alum, coagulants |
| **Pharmaceutical Industry** | Precise liquid flow in batch reactors |
| **Food & Beverage** | Syrup, milk, juice flow measurement |
| **Oil & Gas** | Low-pressure gas flow measurement |
| **HVAC** | Air-conditioning chilled water flow |
| **Laboratories** | Carrier gas flow in gas chromatography, pilot plant reactors |

> [!TIP]
> A **calibrated rotameter** offers ±1–2% accuracy in the upper 80% of its scale; accuracy degrades below 10% of full scale. Always operate in the **upper-middle** of the scale for best results.

---

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Float Equilibrium Pressure Drop

We consider the float as a rigid body in **steady-state equilibrium** inside a vertical tapered tube with fluid flowing **upward**.

### Step 1: Identify the Forces

**Downward forces:**
- Weight of the float: $W = m_f \, g = V_f \, \rho_f \, g$

**Upward forces:**
- Buoyancy of the displaced fluid: $F_B = V_f \, \rho_m \, g$
- Net pressure force from the fluid: $F_{\Delta P} = (P_{\text{below}} - P_{\text{above}}) \times A_f = \Delta P \cdot A_f$

### Step 2: Apply Newton's Equilibrium Condition

For the float to remain stationary (not accelerating):

$$F_{\Delta P} + F_B = W$$

$$\Delta P \cdot A_f + V_f \, \rho_m \, g = V_f \, \rho_f \, g$$

### Step 3: Solve for the Pressure Drop ΔP

Rearranging the equation to isolate $\Delta P$:

$$\Delta P \cdot A_f = V_f \, \rho_f \, g - V_f \, \rho_m \, g$$

$$\Delta P \cdot A_f = V_f \, g \, (\rho_f - \rho_m)$$

Dividing both sides by $A_f$:

$$\boxed{\Delta P = \frac{V_f \, (\rho_f - \rho_m) \, g}{A_f}}$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** Since $V_f$, $A_f$, $\rho_f$, $\rho_m$, and $g$ are all constants for a given rotameter-fluid combination, $\Delta P$ is **independent of flow rate**. The float always experiences the **same pressure drop** regardless of where it sits in the tube.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Volumetric Flow Rate Equation

### Step 1: Apply Bernoulli's Equation

Apply Bernoulli's equation between the **bottom (1)** and **top (2)** of the float, assuming the flow velocity above the float is much larger than below (because of the annular restriction):

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho_m g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho_m g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

The elevation difference is $z_2 - z_1 = h$ (float height). The velocity $v_1$ is small (large cross-section below the float), so $v_1^2/(2g) \approx 0$.

Rearranging:

$$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho_m g} = \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + h$$

Ignoring the minor $h$ term (because float is small and fluid density effects dominate), and noting $P_1 - P_2 = \Delta P$:

$$\frac{\Delta P}{\rho_m g} = \frac{v_2^2}{2g}$$

### Step 2: Solve for the Throat Velocity

$$v_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \, \Delta P}{\rho_m}}$$

### Step 3: Substitute ΔP from the Equilibrium Condition

$$v_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \, V_f \, (\rho_f - \rho_m) \, g}{A_f \, \rho_m}}$$

### Step 4: Compute the Volumetric Flow Rate

The actual flow passes through the **annular area** $A_a$ between the float and the tube. Introducing a discharge coefficient $C_d$ to account for viscous and contraction losses:

$$Q = C_d \, A_a \, v_2$$

$$\boxed{Q = C_d \, A_a \, \sqrt{\frac{2 \, V_f \, (\rho_f - \rho_m) \, g}{A_f \, \rho_m}}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Q ∝ √h Relationship

### Step 1: Geometry of the Tapered Tube

The tube has a half-cone angle $\alpha$. If the float has a maximum diameter $D_f$, then at height $h$ above the bottom stop, the tube's inner diameter is:

$$D(h) = D_f + 2 h \tan \alpha$$

### Step 2: Annular Area Calculation

The annular area is the difference between the tube cross-section and the float cross-section:

$$A_a = \frac{\pi}{4} \left[ D(h)^2 - D_f^2 \right] = \frac{\pi}{4} \left[(D_f + 2h \tan \alpha)^2 - D_f^2 \right]$$

Expanding the square:

$$A_a = \frac{\pi}{4} \left[D_f^2 + 4 D_f h \tan \alpha + 4 h^2 \tan^2 \alpha - D_f^2 \right]$$

$$A_a = \frac{\pi}{4} \left[4 D_f h \tan \alpha + 4 h^2 \tan^2 \alpha \right]$$

$$A_a = \pi D_f h \tan \alpha + \pi h^2 \tan^2 \alpha$$

Since $\tan \alpha$ is very small for a typical rotameter (shallow taper), the $h^2$ term is negligible. Thus:

$$A_a \approx \pi D_f \tan \alpha \cdot h = K' h$$

Where $K' = \pi D_f \tan \alpha$ is a geometric constant.

### Step 3: Substitute Back into the Flow Equation

$$Q = C_d \, K' h \sqrt{\frac{2 \, V_f \, (\rho_f - \rho_m) \, g}{A_f \, \rho_m}}$$

$$Q = C_d \, K'' \cdot h$$

Wait — that would be **linear in h**, not square root! The square-root dependence comes from the **empirical discharge coefficient**, which itself depends on Reynolds number (and hence on flow rate). In the regime where $C_d$ varies approximately as $h^{-1/2}$, the product $C_d \cdot h$ becomes approximately $\sqrt{h}$, recovering the empirically observed relationship.

A more rigorous treatment shows:

$$Q = C_d(h) \cdot A_a(h) \cdot v_2(h) \;\Longrightarrow\; Q \propto h^{n}, \quad 0.4 \leq n \leq 0.6$$

Experimentally, $n \approx 0.5$ is observed for most practical rotameters, hence:

$$\boxed{Q \propto \sqrt{h}}$$

## 3.4 Sample Numerical Calculation (Model Solution)

**Given:**
- Float volume $V_f = 5 \text{ cm}^3 = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$
- Float cross-sectional area $A_f = 1 \text{ cm}^2 = 1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$
- Float density $\rho_f = 7900 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (stainless steel)
- Water density $\rho_m = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- $C_d = 0.95$ (assumed)
- Taper constant: $K' = 8 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}$ (per unit height in meters)
- Float height reading: $h = 25 \text{ cm} = 0.25 \text{ m}$

**Step 1 — Compute ΔP:**

$$\Delta P = \frac{5 \times 10^{-6} \times (7900 - 1000) \times 9.81}{1 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\Delta P = \frac{5 \times 10^{-6} \times 6900 \times 9.81}{1 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{0.3386}{1 \times 10^{-4}} = 3386 \, \text{Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Annular Area $A_a$:**

$$A_a = K' \cdot h = 8 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.25 = 2 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^2 = 2 \, \text{cm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute Theoretical Flow Rate:**

$$Q = C_d \, A_a \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_m}} = 0.95 \times 2 \times 10^{-5} \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 3386}{1000}}$$

$$Q = 0.95 \times 2 \times 10^{-5} \times \sqrt{6.772} = 0.95 \times 2 \times 10^{-5} \times 2.6022$$

$$Q = 0.95 \times 5.2044 \times 10^{-5} = 4.944 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 49.44 \, \text{cm}^3/\text{s} \approx 2.97 \, \text{L/min}}$$

**Step 4 — Actual Flow Rate (Volumetric Method):**

If in 30 seconds, the collected volume is 1.5 L = 0.0015 m³:

$$Q_{\text{actual}} = \frac{0.0015}{30} = 5 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 50 \, \text{cm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Percentage Error:**

$$\% \text{ Error} = \frac{Q_{\text{rotameter}} - Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\text{actual}}} \times 100 = \frac{49.44 - 50}{50} \times 100 = -1.12\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> An error within ±2% is considered **acceptable** for a properly calibrated rotameter.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

### Q1. Define a Rotameter. Why is it called a "Variable Area" flowmeter?
**Model Answer:**
A rotameter is a **vertically mounted, tapered-tube flow measuring device** in which a free-floating element (float) rises to a height that depends on the volumetric flow rate. It is called a "variable area" flowmeter because the **annular flow area between the float and the tube wall changes** with flow rate (the float moves to a position where annular area = required area for that flow). In contrast, orifice meters and venturi meters have a *fixed* cross-sectional area and a *variable* pressure drop.

### Q2. State the working principle of a Rotameter.
**Model Answer:**
The working principle is based on **force balance on the float** in upward fluid flow. The float is held in a stable position where the **net downward force (weight − buoyancy)** equals the **upward pressure-drag force** exerted by the fluid. As flow rate increases, the drag force increases, lifting the float higher until a new annular area (and hence lower velocity) is reached that restores equilibrium. The float height `h` is therefore an indicator of flow rate.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

> **Note (KTU 2024 Pattern):** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks, with internal choice between two questions (Q9 OR Q10 style).

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Full Theory + Numerical**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a rotameter. Derive the expression for the volumetric flow rate through a rotameter. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A rotameter consists of:
1. A **vertically mounted, gradually tapered (conical) transparent tube** made of borosilicate glass.
2. A **float** (plumb-bob) made of stainless steel, tantalum, or lead, whose shape is designed to provide stable, central positioning in the flow.
3. **Scale markings** etched on the tube corresponding to flow rates (for water or air at standard conditions).
4. **Inlet and outlet fittings** at the bottom and top for connection to the pipeline.
5. A **bottom stop** to prevent the float from falling out when flow is zero.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
As described in Section 1.2 and 2.1, the float stabilizes at a height where the net downward force equals the upward drag force, and the height is read on the scale.

**Derivation (2 marks):** As in Section 3.1 and 3.2 — the float equilibrium yields $\Delta P$ constant, and applying Bernoulli's equation gives:

$$Q = C_d \, A_a \sqrt{\frac{2 V_f (\rho_f - \rho_m) g}{A_f \rho_m}}$$

---

**(b)** During calibration of a rotameter with water at 25 °C, the following observations were recorded. Calculate the actual flow rate, plot the calibration curve (Q vs h), and determine the discharge coefficient C_d for each reading, given: $V_f = 4.5 \text{ cm}^3$, $A_f = 0.9 \text{ cm}^2$, $\rho_f = 7800 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_w = 997 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. The annular area relation is $A_a = 7.5 \times 10^{-5} \cdot h$ (with h in meters, A_a in m²). **(7 marks)**

| S. No. | Rotameter Reading h (cm) | Collected Volume V (mL) | Time t (s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5.0 | 250 | 30.0 |
| 2 | 10.0 | 480 | 30.0 |
| 3 | 15.0 | 690 | 30.0 |
| 4 | 20.0 | 880 | 30.0 |
| 5 | 25.0 | 1050 | 30.0 |
| 6 | 30.0 | 1200 | 30.0 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Actual Flow Rate $Q_{\text{actual}} = V / t$:**

For reading 1:
$$Q_1 = \frac{250 \text{ mL}}{30 \text{ s}} = 8.33 \text{ mL/s} = 8.33 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

For all readings:

| h (cm) | V (mL) | t (s) | Q_actual (mL/s) | Q_actual × 10⁻⁶ (m³/s) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 5.0 | 250 | 30 | 8.33 | 8.33 |
| 10.0 | 480 | 30 | 16.00 | 16.00 |
| 15.0 | 690 | 30 | 23.00 | 23.00 |
| 20.0 | 880 | 30 | 29.33 | 29.33 |
| 25.0 | 1050 | 30 | 35.00 | 35.00 |
| 30.0 | 1200 | 30 | 40.00 | 40.00 |

**Step 2 — Compute ΔP (constant for all readings):**

$$\Delta P = \frac{V_f (\rho_f - \rho_m) g}{A_f} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^{-6} \times (7800 - 997) \times 9.81}{0.9 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\Delta P = \frac{4.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 6803 \times 9.81}{0.9 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{0.3003}{0.9 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 3336.7 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3 — For each h, compute $A_a$, then theoretical $Q_{\text{rotameter}}$, then $C_d$:**

For h = 5 cm = 0.05 m:
$$A_a = 7.5 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.05 = 3.75 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$$

$$Q_{\text{rotameter,ideal}} = A_a \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_m}} = 3.75 \times 10^{-6} \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 3336.7}{997}}$$

$$= 3.75 \times 10^{-6} \times \sqrt{6.694} = 3.75 \times 10^{-6} \times 2.587 = 9.70 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$C_d = \frac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\text{rotameter,ideal}}} = \frac{8.33 \times 10^{-6}}{9.70 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.859$$

Repeating for all readings (similar procedure):

| h (cm) | A_a × 10⁻⁶ (m²) | Q_ideal × 10⁻⁶ (m³/s) | C_d |
|---|---|---|---|
| 5 | 3.75 | 9.70 | 0.859 |
| 10 | 7.50 | 19.40 | 0.825 |
| 15 | 11.25 | 29.10 | 0.790 |
| 20 | 15.00 | 38.81 | 0.756 |
| 25 | 18.75 | 48.51 | 0.722 |
| 30 | 22.50 | 58.21 | 0.687 |

**Step 4 — Average C_d:**

$$\bar{C_d} = \frac{0.859 + 0.825 + 0.790 + 0.756 + 0.722 + 0.687}{6} \approx 0.773$$

**Step 5 — Calibration Curve:** Plot Q_actual (Y-axis) vs h (X-axis). The curve should be smooth, monotonically increasing, and roughly square-root shaped. Use MS Excel or graph paper.

**Conclusion:** The average discharge coefficient is **C_d ≈ 0.77**, which is within the typical range (0.6 – 0.98) for commercial rotameters.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Concept-Focused + Lab Procedure**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Pattern]**

**(a)** Differentiate between a rotameter and an orifice meter. Why is a rotameter preferred for low flow rates of corrosive liquids? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Rotameter | Orifice Meter |
|---|---|---|
| Type | Variable area | Variable pressure drop (head) |
| Rangeability | 10:1 (good) | 3:1 to 4:1 (limited) |
| Accuracy | ±1–2% in upper 80% | ±1–2% generally |
| Pressure drop | Constant (≈ constant ΔP) | Varies with flow |
| Pressure recovery | Excellent (good for low-pressure systems) | Poor (high permanent loss) |
| Cost | Moderate | Low (simple plate) |
| Reading | Direct visual scale | Requires manometer + calculation |
| Orientation | Must be **vertical** (upward flow) | Any orientation |
| Suitable for slurries | Not ideal (float can jam) | Yes (if designed properly) |
| Corrosive fluids | Yes — float & tube made of chemical-resistant materials (e.g., PTFE, Hastelloy) | Yes — but differential pressure transmitter can corrode |
| Suitability for low flow | **Excellent** (visual, no need for dp cell) | Limited (low dp hard to measure) |

**Why rotameter is preferred for low flow rates of corrosive liquids (3 marks):**
1. **Low flow rates give very small pressure drops** in orifice meters, making them hard to measure accurately. A rotameter has a **constant and predictable pressure drop**, and its reading is **directly visible** on the scale.
2. The **float and tube can be fabricated from corrosion-resistant materials** (glass for tube; PTFE, PVDF, Hastelloy, or tantalum for float), making it suitable for aggressive chemicals (HCl, H₂SO₄, etc.).
3. **No moving mechanical linkages** exposed to the fluid (only the float touches the fluid).
4. **Simple to install, read, and clean** — ideal for laboratory and pilot plant use.
5. **No need for differential pressure transmitter** or mercury manometer, which themselves are vulnerable to corrosion.

---

**(b)** Describe the experimental procedure to calibrate a rotameter using the collecting tank method. What precautions must be taken to ensure accurate results? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Apparatus Required (1 mark):**
- Rotameter (test specimen)
- Centrifugal pump with overhead reservoir
- Inlet valve, control valve (needle valve), drain valve
- Collecting tank (graduated) of known cross-sectional area
- Stopwatch (accuracy ± 0.1 s)
- Measuring cylinder (1 L, graduated)
- Thermometer (to record water temperature)
- Connecting pipes and fittings

**Experimental Procedure (4 marks):**

1. **Setup Inspection:** Assemble the apparatus as per the flow diagram (Section 4.1). Check for leakages in pipes, valves, and the rotameter fittings. Ensure the rotameter is **perfectly vertical** (use a plumb line).

2. **Priming:** Fill the overhead tank and start the pump. Open the inlet valve slowly to fill the rotameter with water, ensuring no air bubbles are trapped. Open the control valve fully and let water flow until the system is **air-free**.

3. **Zero Adjustment:** With the control valve closed, the float should rest on the bottom stop. Note the zero reading of the rotameter.

4. **Trial Run:** Partially open the control valve. Let the flow stabilize. Observe the float position. Note the **rotameter reading h** (in mm or cm).

5. **Collection & Timing:** Simultaneously start the stopwatch and direct the outlet water into the **collecting tank**. After a fixed time (typically 30–60 s, chosen to collect at least 0.5 L), stop the watch. Note the **collected volume V** and **time t**.

6. **Repeat:** Repeat steps 4–5 for **6 to 8 different flow rates** (by varying the control valve position from minimum to maximum). For each, record (h, V, t).

7. **Calculations:** Compute $Q_{\text{actual}} = V / t$ for each reading.

8. **Calibration Curve:** Plot $Q_{\text{actual}}$ (Y-axis) vs $h$ (X-axis) on a graph sheet or using MS Excel. Fit a best-fit curve (often a 2nd-degree polynomial or power law of the form $Q = a h^b$).

9. **Determine C_d** using the rotameter equation (if float parameters are known).

**Precautions for Accurate Results (2 marks):**
- ✅ Rotameter must be **perfectly vertical**.
- ✅ System must be **completely free of air bubbles** (bleed thoroughly).
- ✅ Collect sufficient volume to **minimize timing error** (at least 0.5 L).
- ✅ Maintain **constant water temperature** (note it; if temperature changes by >2 °C, redo).
- ✅ For each reading, wait for **steady state** (float must be steady, not oscillating).
- ✅ Repeat each reading **at least 2 times** and take the average.
- ✅ Ensure the **collecting tank is empty at the start** of each reading.
- ✅ Do not let water splash out of the collecting tank (use a wide-mouth container).
- ✅ Clean the rotameter float periodically to avoid deposits affecting its weight.
- ✅ **Avoid vibration** from the pump — mount rotameter on a vibration-isolated stand if needed.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** (cm³ → m³, minutes → seconds). This single error can lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Using ρ_water = 1000 kg/m³ blindly** at temperatures other than 4 °C. At 25 °C, ρ = 997 kg/m³; at 30 °C, ρ = 996 kg/m³.
> 3. **Confusing indicated flow with actual flow.** A rotameter scale marked "L/min" assumes a particular fluid density. Always use the **volumetric method** to obtain actual flow.
> 4. **Not stating the working principle clearly** — examiners expect the phrase *"force balance on the float"* and *"variable annular area"*.
> 5. **Forgetting to include a labeled diagram.** A diagram without labels is worth half the marks.
> 6. **Not mentioning ΔP is constant** — this is the key conceptual insight of a rotameter.
> 7. **Plotting the wrong axis** — flow rate on Y, height on X (independently variable goes on X-axis).
> 8. **Not reporting units** in the calibration curve and in the final answer.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **📋 Last-Minute Revision Bullet Points — Calibration of a Rotameter**

- [x] A **rotameter** is a **variable area flowmeter** (NOT variable pressure drop like orifice/venturi).
- [x] It consists of a **vertical, tapered transparent tube** and a **free-floating float (plumb-bob)**.
- [x] Working principle: **Force balance** — Weight − Buoyancy = Pressure Drag Force.
- [x] The **pressure drop across the float (ΔP) is constant** for a given float and fluid, regardless of flow rate.
- [x] The **float height (h) is proportional to the square root of flow rate (Q ∝ √h)**.
- [x] **Calibration** = comparing rotameter reading (h) with actual flow (V/t) measured by collecting tank.
- [x] **Calibration curve** = plot of Q_actual (Y-axis) vs h (X-axis).
- [x] Standard conditions assumed by manufacturer: water at 20 °C, ρ = 998.2 kg/m³, or air at STP.
- [x] **Discharge coefficient C_d** for a rotameter typically lies in the range **0.6 – 0.98**.
- [x] **Annular area A_a** between float and tube wall is the effective flow cross-section.
- [x] **Key formula:** $Q = C_d \, A_a \, \sqrt{\dfrac{2 V_f (\rho_f - \rho_m) g}{A_f \rho_m}}$
- [x] **Float equilibrium formula:** $\Delta P = \dfrac{V_f (\rho_f - \rho_m) g}{A_f}$
- [x] **Float must be vertical** during installation, else the reading will be erroneous.
- [x] **Air bubbles** must be bled out before taking readings.
- [x] **Higher float density (ρ_f) → higher ΔP → better stability** but more pressure loss.
- [x] **Taper angle (α) of tube is very small** (typically 1°–4°) — larger taper gives more sensitive response.
- [x] **Float shape affects C_d** — sharp-edged floats give different C_d than rounded floats.
- [x] **Rangeability** = ratio of max to min measurable flow ≈ 10:1 for rotameters.
- [x] **Accuracy** of a well-calibrated rotameter = **±1% to ±2%** of full scale.
- [x] **Rotameter is best for:** low-to-medium flow rates, clean liquids/gases, corrosive fluids, transparent flow visualization.
- [x] **Rotameter is NOT suitable for:** slurries (float jams), very high temperatures (glass breaks), opaque fluids (can't see float), very high pressures (tube may burst).
- [x] **End connections** of a rotameter are standardized (e.g., ½", 1" BSP/NPT) for easy installation.
- [x] **KTU viva questions to expect:**
  - *"Why is the rotameter installed vertically?"* — To ensure the drag force is purely vertical and the float sits concentrically.
  - *"What happens if the fluid viscosity changes?"* — C_d changes, calibration becomes invalid; need to re-calibrate.
  - *"Can a rotameter measure gas flow?"* — Yes, with appropriate float material and calibration.
  - *"What is the difference between float types?"* — Sharp-edged, ball, plumb-bob, and vane floats each have different stability and C_d characteristics.
- [x] **Lab report must include:** Objective, Apparatus, Diagram, Theory (with formulas), Procedure, Observations table, Sample calculation, Calibration graph, Result (C_d, % error), Precautions.
- [x] **Minimum 6 readings** must be taken to draw a smooth, reliable calibration curve.
- [x] **Time for each reading** should be **30–60 s** to balance accuracy and experiment duration.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of viscosity of Newtonian and non-Newtonian fluid by falling sphere method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHL308 - FLUID & PARTICLE MECHANICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Determination of viscosity of Newtonian and non-Newtonian fluid by falling sphere method # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Aim of the Experiment (KTU 2024 Statement) To determine the **viscosity** of a given **Newtonian fluid** (e.g., castor oil, glycerine) and a **non-Newtonian fluid** (e.g., CMC solution, glycerol-water mixture) using the **falling sphere (falling ball) viscometer** by measuring the terminal settling velocity of a spherical ball under gravity. ## 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU Syllabus-Aligned) > [!NOTE] Core Definition — Viscosity > **Viscosity (μ or η)** is the internal resistance of a fluid to flow. It is defined as the ratio of the shear stress (τ, in Pa) to the shear rate (γ̇, in s⁻¹) at a point in the fluid. > $$\mu = \frac{\tau}{\dot{\gamma}}$$ > The SI unit is **Pa·s (Pascal-second)** or **N·s/m²**; the CGS unit is **poise (P)**, where 1 Pa·s = 10 P. > [!NOTE] Core Definition — Newton's Law of Viscosity > For a **Newtonian fluid**, the shear stress is directly proportional to the velocity gradient (shear rate). The proportionality constant is the dynamic viscosity μ, which is **independent of the shear rate** at a given temperature. > $$\tau = \mu \frac{dv}{dy}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] Newtonian vs. Non-Newtonian Fluids (High-Yield for KTU) > - **Newtonian fluid**: Viscosity is constant (e.g., water, air, glycerine, castor oil, light oils). > - **Non-Newtonian fluid**: Viscosity varies with shear rate — called **apparent viscosity (μₐ)**. > - **Pseudoplastic (shear-thinning)**: Viscosity decreases with shear rate (e.g., CMC solutions, blood, paints, ketchup). > - **Dilatant (shear-thickening)**: Viscosity increases with shear rate (e.g., starch suspensions, wet sand). > - **Bingham plastic**: Behaves like a solid until yield stress is exceeded (e.g., toothpaste, drilling mud). > - **Thixotropic / Rheopectic**: Time-dependent behaviour. > [!NOTE] Falling Sphere Method > The **falling sphere (falling ball) viscometer** determines viscosity by measuring the **terminal (steady) velocity** of a small, dense sphere falling under gravity through the fluid. At terminal velocity, the **net gravitational force** is balanced by the **drag force** (buoyancy-corrected weight = viscous drag). It is governed by **Stokes' Law** for laminar flow. ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🍎 Imagine dropping a **marble into a jar of honey** versus into a glass of **water**. - The marble falls **very slowly** in honey (high viscosity → high resistance). - The marble falls **very fast** in water (low viscosity → low resistance). The **terminal velocity** is the constant speed the marble eventually reaches, where the gravitational pull is balanced by viscous drag. By **measuring this terminal speed** and knowing the marble's size and density, we can mathematically extract the **viscosity** of the fluid — just like reverse-engineering a fluid's "stickiness" by observing a falling ball. For **non-Newtonian fluids** (like ketchup), the apparent viscosity changes with how fast you push — here, the apparent viscosity is tied to the **shear rate experienced by the sphere's surface**, so the falling ball method still works but gives an **apparent viscosity** rather than a true constant viscosity. ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | Acceleration due to gravity | g | **9.81 m/s²** | | Density of steel ball | ρₛ | ~**7850 kg/m³** | | Density of glass ball | ρₛ | ~**2500 kg/m³** | | Density of water (reference) | ρ_f | **1000 kg/m³** | | Dynamic viscosity of water (20 °C) | μ | **1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s** | # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Working Principle — The Physics of a Falling Sphere When a sphere is released into a stagnant fluid column: 1. Initially, the sphere **accelerates** under gravity. 2. As velocity increases, the **viscous drag** (resistance) increases. 3. Eventually, drag + buoyancy = gravitational pull, and the sphere reaches a **constant terminal velocity (vₜ)**. 4. From vₜ and known sphere/fluid properties, viscosity is calculated. ## 2.2 Force Balance on the Falling Sphere Three forces act on a sphere of radius *r* falling at velocity *v* in a fluid of viscosity *μ*: | Force | Formula | Direction | |---|---|---| | Weight (W) | $W = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3} \rho_{s} g$ | Downward | | Buoyancy (F_b) | $F_{b} = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3} \rho_{f} g$ | Upward | | Viscous drag (F_d) | $F_{d} = 6 \pi \mu r v$ (Stokes' Law) | Upward (opposing motion) | At **terminal velocity vₜ**, the net force is zero: $$W = F_{b} + F_{d}$$ ## 2.3 Stokes' Law (1851) — The Heart of the Experiment **George Gabriel Stokes** established that for a sphere moving slowly through a viscous fluid (laminar flow, very low Reynolds number), the drag force is: $$F_{d} = 6 \pi \mu r v_{t}$$ **Validity Conditions (must be tested):** - Steady, laminar flow → **Reynolds number Re < 1** (often < 0.1 for strict Stokes regime). - Sphere moves in an **infinite, stagnant fluid** (no wall effects). - Sphere is **rigid and smooth**. - Fluid is **homogeneous** and shows **no-slip** at the sphere surface. ## 2.4 Derivation Logic of Terminal Velocity Equating forces at terminal velocity: $$\frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}(\rho_{s} - \rho_{f}) g = 6 \pi \mu r v_{t}$$ Rearranging for **vₜ**: $$v_{t} = \frac{2 r^{2} g (\rho_{s} - \rho_{f})}{9 \mu}$$ Rearranging for **μ (Newtonian fluid)**: $$\mu = \frac{2 r^{2} g (\rho_{s} - \rho_{f})}{9 v_{t}} = \frac{d^{2} g (\rho_{s} - \rho_{f})}{18 v_{t}}$$ where *d = 2r* is the **diameter of the sphere**. ## 2.5 Non-Newtonian Extension — Apparent Viscosity For a **non-Newtonian fluid** (Power-law / Ostwald-de Waele model): $$\tau = K \dot{\gamma}^{n}$$ where **K** is the consistency index and **n** is the flow behaviour index. The falling-ball viscometer does not directly give K and n. Instead, it provides an **apparent viscosity μₐ** at a representative shear rate. **Representative shear rate at sphere surface (Pei / Bruss / Famularo approximation):** $$\dot{\gamma}_{eff} = \frac{v_{t}}{d}$$ **Apparent viscosity:** $$\mu_{a} = \frac{d^{2} g (\rho_{s} - \rho_{f})}{18 v_{t}}$$ The same formula holds, but the result is reported as **μₐ (apparent)** rather than true μ. ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU question on this experiment. | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $v_{t} = \dfrac{L}{t}$ | Terminal velocity = fall distance / time | m/s | | 2 | $\mu = \dfrac{2 r^{2} g (\rho_{s} - \rho_{f})}{9 v_{t}}$ | Viscosity of Newtonian fluid | Pa·s | | 3 | $\mu = \dfrac{d^{2} g (\rho_{s} - \rho_{f})}{18 v_{t}}$ | Same, in terms of diameter d | Pa·s | | 4 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho_{f} v_{t} d}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number (validity check, Re < 1) | dimensionless | | 5 | $\mu_{a} = \dfrac{d^{2} g (\rho_{s} - \rho_{f})}{18 v_{t}}$ | Apparent viscosity (non-Newtonian) | Pa·s | | 6 | $\dot{\gamma} = \dfrac{v_{t}}{d}$ | Effective shear rate for non-Newtonian | s⁻¹ | | 7 | $\mu_{c} = \mu \cdot C_{w}$ | Wall-corrected viscosity, where $C_{w} = \dfrac{1}{1 - 2.104 \frac{d}{D} + 2.09 \left(\frac{d}{D}\right)^{3} - 0.95 \left(\frac{d}{D}\right)^{5}}$ (Faxén correction) | Pa·s | | 8 | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho_{f}}$ | Kinematic viscosity (sometimes asked) | m²/s | | 9 | $F_{d} = 6 \pi \mu r v$ | Stokes' drag force | N | | 10 | $W - F_{b} = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^{3}(\rho_{s} - \rho_{f})g$ | Net downward force (weight − buoyancy) | N | > [!NOTE] Wall Correction (Faxén, 1924) > Since the fluid in the glass tube is not infinite, the **container walls** increase drag. The **Faxén correction factor C_w** multiplies the calculated viscosity. *D* = tube inner diameter, *d* = sphere diameter. ## 2.7 Why is this Important in Real Engineering? - **Pharmaceutical & biotech industry**: Quality control of polymer solutions, drug formulations. - **Food industry**: Ketchup, sauces, dairy products are non-Newtonian. - **Cosmetics**: Shampoos, creams, lotions. - **Petroleum industry**: Crude oil, drilling muds (Bingham plastics). - **Coatings & paints**: Shear-thinning behaviour is critical for brushing/spraying. - **Polymer processing**: Molten polymers (PET, HDPE) are shear-thinning. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Stokes' Law via Force Balance ### Step 1 — Identify all forces on the sphere A sphere of radius **r** and density **ρₛ** falls under gravity through a fluid of density **ρ_f** and dynamic viscosity **μ**. Three forces act: **Weight (gravitational force, downward):** $$W = m \cdot g = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3} \rho_{s} \cdot g$$ **Buoyancy (upward, Archimedes' principle):** $$F_{b} = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3} \rho_{f} \cdot g$$ **Viscous drag (upward, opposing motion):** $$F_{d} = 6 \pi \mu r v$$ ### Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law At any instant: $$W - F_{b} - F_{d} = m \frac{dv}{dt}$$ ### Step 3 — Apply the Terminal Velocity Condition At terminal velocity **vₜ**, acceleration = 0, so: $$W - F_{b} - F_{d} = 0$$ ### Step 4 — Substitute the forces $$\frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3} \rho_{s} g - \frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3} \rho_{f} g - 6 \pi \mu r v_{t} = 0$$ Factor out common terms ($\frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3} g$): $$\frac{4}{3}\pi r^{3} g (\rho_{s} - \rho_{f}) = 6 \pi \mu r v_{t}$$ ### Step 5 — Solve for terminal velocity vₜ Cancel one r and rearrange: $$v_{t} = \frac{4 \pi r^{3} g (\rho_{s} - \rho_{f})}{3 \cdot 6 \pi \mu r}$$ $$v_{t} = \frac{4 r^{2} g (\rho_{s} - \rho_{f})}{18 \mu} = \frac{2 r^{2} g (\rho_{s} - \rho_{f})}{9 \mu}$$ This is the **fundamental equation** for the falling sphere experiment. ### Step 6 — Solve for viscosity μ (the experimental goal) $$\mu = \frac{2 r^{2} g (\rho_{s} - \rho_{f})}{9 v_{t}}$$ Substituting *d = 2r* (so $r^{2} = d^{2}/4$): $$\mu = \frac{2 \cdot \frac{d^{2}}{4} \cdot g (\rho_{s} - \rho_{f})}{9 v_{t}} = \frac{d^{2} g (\rho_{s} - \rho_{f})}{18 v_{t}}$$ --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style) ### Problem (Model Question Pattern) A steel ball of diameter **d = 3 mm** and density **ρₛ = 7850 kg/m³** falls through castor oil of density **ρ_f = 960 kg/m³** in a glass tube. The ball traverses a distance of **L = 200 mm = 0.2 m** between two marked points in **t = 4.5 s**. Calculate the viscosity of the oil. Given g = 9.81 m/s². Check Stokes' law validity. ### Step 1 — Compute terminal velocity $$v_{t} = \frac{L}{t} = \frac{0.2 \text{ m}}{4.5 \text{ s}} = 0.04444 \text{ m/s}$$ ### Step 2 — Substitute into viscosity formula $$\mu = \frac{d^{2} g (\rho_{s} - \rho_{f})}{18 v_{t}}$$ $$\mu = \frac{(0.003)^{2} \cdot 9.81 \cdot (7850 - 960)}{18 \cdot 0.04444}$$ ### Step 3 — Evaluate numerator $$(0.003)^{2} = 9 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$(7850 - 960) = 6890 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$$ $$\text{Numerator} = 9 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 9.81 \cdot 6890 = 0.60836 \text{ (SI units)}$$ ### Step 4 — Evaluate denominator $$18 \cdot 0.04444 = 0.7999 \approx 0.8$$ ### Step 5 — Compute viscosity $$\mu = \frac{0.60836}{0.8} = 0.7604 \text{ Pa·s}$$ $$\boxed{\mu \approx 0.760 \text{ Pa·s}}$$ (Castor oil's literature value is ~0.985 Pa·s at 20 °C — close agreement confirms the method.) ### Step 6 — Check Reynolds number validity $$Re = \frac{\rho_{f} v_{t} d}{\mu} = \frac{960 \cdot 0.04444 \cdot 0.003}{0.7604}$$ $$Re = \frac{0.1280}{0.7604} = 0.1683$$ Since **Re ≈ 0.17 < 1**, Stokes' law is **valid**. ✅ --- ## 3.3 Wall-Corrected Worked Example If the tube inner diameter **D = 30 mm**, apply Faxén correction: $$\frac{d}{D} = \frac{3}{30} = 0.1$$ $$C_{w} = \frac{1}{1 - 2.104(0.1) + 2.09(0.1)^{3} - 0.95(0.1)^{5}}$$ $$C_{w} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.2104 + 0.00209 - 0.00000095} = \frac{1}{0.79169} = 1.2631$$ $$\mu_{corrected} = \mu \cdot C_{w} = 0.7604 \cdot 1.2631 = 0.9604 \text{ Pa·s}$$ This is **much closer to the literature value (0.985 Pa·s)**. ✅ --- ## 3.4 Non-Newtonian (CMC Solution) Worked Example A glass ball (ρₛ = 2500 kg/m³, d = 4 mm) falls through a 0.5% CMC solution (ρ_f = 1000 kg/m³) in a tube of inner diameter D = 25 mm. The fall time over L = 250 mm is t = 12 s. Find apparent viscosity and effective shear rate. **Step 1 — Terminal velocity:** $$v_{t} = \frac{0.250}{12} = 0.02083 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Apparent viscosity:** $$\mu_{a} = \frac{(0.004)^{2} \cdot 9.81 \cdot (2500 - 1000)}{18 \cdot 0.02083}$$ $$\mu_{a} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 9.81 \cdot 1500}{0.375}$$ $$\mu_{a} = \frac{0.23544}{0.375} = 0.6278 \text{ Pa·s}$$ **Step 3 — Effective shear rate:** $$\dot{\gamma}_{eff} = \frac{v_{t}}{d} = \frac{0.02083}{0.004} = 5.21 \text{ s}^{-1}$$ $$\boxed{\mu_{a} = 0.628 \text{ Pa·s} \text{ at } \dot{\gamma} = 5.21 \text{ s}^{-1}}$$ --- ## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Experimental Choices | Observation Type | Newtonian Fluid | Non-Newtonian Fluid | |---|---|---| | Choice of sphere | Small dense ball (steel/glass) | Glass/plastic ball (avoid sedimentation issues) | | Tube diameter | D ≥ 10d (minimize wall effects) | Same | | Result reported as | **μ (true viscosity)** | **μₐ (apparent viscosity)** at a stated shear rate | | Viscosity is | Constant at given T | Varies with shear rate | | Validity check | Re < 1 | Re < 1; mention shear-dependent | | Correction | Faxén wall correction (if D small) | Same, plus declare shear rate | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Experimental Setup — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Clean graduated glass tube] --> B[Fill tube with test fluid up to mark] B --> C[Allow fluid to stand and reach thermal equilibrium] C --> D[Measure fluid density rho_f using pycnometer] D --> E[Select sphere and measure diameter d with micrometer] E --> F[Measure sphere density rho_s] F --> G[Mark two reference lines A and B on tube distance L apart] G --> H[Release sphere gently along central axis using tweezers] H --> I[Start stopwatch when sphere crosses line A] I --> J[Stop stopwatch when sphere crosses line B] J --> K[Record fall time t] K --> L{Is sphere moving in straight line at center?} L -->|No| H L -->|Yes| M[Compute terminal velocity v_t = L divided by t] M --> N[Calculate viscosity mu from Stokes formula] N --> O[Check Reynolds number Re less than 1] O --> P[Apply Faxen wall correction if needed] P --> Q[Report result with units and uncertainty] Q --> R[End] ``` ## 4.2 Force-Balance & Calculation Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Input: sphere diameter d] --> B[Compute sphere volume V] B --> C[Compute net downward force W minus Fb] C --> D[Input: fall time t and distance L] D --> E[Compute terminal velocity vt] E --> F[Apply Stokes Law for mu] F --> G[Newtonian: report mu] F --> H[Non-Newtonian: report mu_a at gamma dot] G --> I[Check Re for validity] H --> I I --> J[Apply Faxen wall correction] J --> K[Final viscosity report] ``` ## 4.3 Fluid Classification Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Fluid Sample] --> B{Viscosity constant
at all shear rates?} B -->|Yes| C[Newtonian Fluid] B -->|No| D[Non-Newtonian Fluid] C --> E[Report mu] D --> F{Apparent viscosity
decreases with shear?} F -->|Yes| G[Pseudoplastic or shear-thinning] F -->|No| H{Apparent viscosity
increases with shear?} H -->|Yes| I[Dilatant or shear-thickening] H -->|No| J{Yield stress
present?} J -->|Yes| K[Bingham Plastic] J -->|No| L[Time-dependent non-Newtonian] G --> M[Report mu_a at gamma dot] I --> M K --> M L --> M ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **State Stokes' Law and write its formula. What are the assumptions made in deriving it?** **Model Answer:** Stokes' Law states that the **viscous drag force** experienced by a small sphere moving slowly through a viscous fluid is directly proportional to the **radius of the sphere, its velocity, and the dynamic viscosity of the fluid**. $$F_{d} = 6 \pi \mu r v$$ **Assumptions:** 1. The sphere is **smooth and rigid**. 2. The fluid is **homogeneous, isotropic, and incompressible**. 3. Flow around the sphere is **laminar** (Re < 1). 4. The fluid is **infinite in extent** (no wall effects). 5. There is **no-slip** at the sphere surface. 6. Sphere moves at **constant velocity** (terminal velocity, no acceleration). --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Differentiate between Newtonian and non-Newtonian fluids with two examples each.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Newtonian Fluid | Non-Newtonian Fluid | |---|---|---| | Definition | Shear stress ∝ shear rate (linear) | Shear stress vs. shear rate is non-linear | | Viscosity | **Constant** (independent of shear rate) | **Variable** — apparent viscosity depends on shear rate | | Examples | Water, air, glycerine, castor oil, light oils | Blood, paint, ketchup, CMC solution, toothpaste, drilling mud | | Rheogram (τ vs. γ̇) | Straight line through origin | Curve (shear-thinning) or curve (shear-thickening) or yield-stress line | --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice > [!IMPORTANT] KTU ESE Pattern (14 Marks) > Each Part B question carries **7 + 7 = 14 marks** with internal choice. You must attempt **one full question** out of the two choices offered. ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Sample Pattern - Module 2]** **(a)** Derive the equation for the **terminal velocity of a sphere falling through a viscous fluid** under Stokes' regime. (7 marks) **(b)** A steel ball of diameter **4 mm** and density **7800 kg/m³** falls through a Newtonian liquid of density **1200 kg/m³** in a glass tube of inner diameter **40 mm**. The ball falls a distance of **300 mm** in **6.2 seconds**. Calculate: (i) The terminal velocity of the ball (ii) The viscosity of the liquid (iii) Verify whether Stokes' law is valid. Apply Faxén wall correction if necessary. (7 marks) --- ### Model Solution for Question A #### Part (a) — Derivation of Terminal Velocity Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes for the complete derivation. **Key points to write in exam (for full 7 marks):** - Free-body diagram with Weight (W), Buoyancy (F_b), Drag (F_d) — **(1 mark)** - State Stokes' Law: F_d = 6πμrv — **(1 mark)** - Archimedes' buoyancy expression: F_b = (4/3)πr³ρ_f·g — **(1 mark)** - Weight expression: W = (4/3)πr³ρ_s·g — **(1 mark)** - Force balance: W − F_b = F_d at terminal velocity — **(1 mark)** - Final equation: vₜ = (2r²g(ρₛ − ρ_f)) / (9μ) — **(2 marks)** #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution **Given:** - d = 4 mm = 0.004 m - ρₛ = 7800 kg/m³ - ρ_f = 1200 kg/m³ - D = 40 mm = 0.04 m - L = 300 mm = 0.3 m - t = 6.2 s - g = 9.81 m/s² **(i) Terminal velocity:** $$v_{t} = \frac{L}{t} = \frac{0.3}{6.2} = 0.04839 \text{ m/s}$$ **(ii) Viscosity (uncorrected):** $$\mu = \frac{d^{2} g (\rho_{s} - \rho_{f})}{18 v_{t}}$$ $$\mu = \frac{(0.004)^{2} \cdot 9.81 \cdot (7800 - 1200)}{18 \cdot 0.04839}$$ $$\mu = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 9.81 \cdot 6600}{0.8710}$$ $$\mu = \frac{1.0359}{0.8710} = 1.189 \text{ Pa·s}$$ **(iii) Validity check (Reynolds number):** $$Re = \frac{\rho_{f} v_{t} d}{\mu} = \frac{1200 \cdot 0.04839 \cdot 0.004}{1.189}$$ $$Re = \frac{0.2323}{1.189} = 0.195$$ Since **Re = 0.195 < 1**, Stokes' law is **valid**. ✅ **Faxén wall correction** (d/D = 0.004/0.04 = 0.1): $$C_{w} = \frac{1}{1 - 2.104(0.1) + 2.09(0.1)^{3} - 0.95(0.1)^{5}} = 1.2631$$ $$\mu_{corrected} = 1.189 \times 1.2631 = 1.502 \text{ Pa·s}$$ **Final Answer:** - vₜ = **0.0484 m/s** - μ (uncorrected) = **1.189 Pa·s** - Re = 0.195 → Stokes' law valid ✅ - μ (wall-corrected) = **1.502 Pa·s** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam - Sample Pattern - Module 2]** **(a)** Explain the **falling sphere viscometer method** in detail. What are the **sources of error** and **precautions** to be taken during the experiment? (7 marks) **(b)** A glass ball of diameter **5 mm** and density **2500 kg/m³** is dropped through a **0.8% CMC solution** (treated as non-Newtonian, ρ_f = 1000 kg/m³). The ball falls a distance of **200 mm** in **8.4 s**. Calculate: (i) The terminal velocity (ii) The **apparent viscosity** of the fluid (iii) The **effective shear rate** at the sphere surface (iv) Comment on whether the fluid shows **shear-thinning or shear-thickening** behaviour, given that the literature viscosity at 10 s⁻¹ is 0.4 Pa·s. (7 marks) --- ### Model Solution for Question B #### Part (a) — Falling Sphere Viscometer — Explanation **Principle:** A sphere falling under gravity through a viscous fluid attains a **terminal velocity** when the net gravitational force (weight − buoyancy) is balanced by **Stokes' viscous drag**. The viscosity is computed from this terminal velocity. **Apparatus (Block diagram — refer Section 4.1):** - A **long, transparent, graduated glass tube** (~30–50 cm length, 25–40 mm diameter) - A **calibrated measuring scale** on the tube - **Spherical balls** of known diameter (steel, glass, or brass) - **Micrometer / Vernier caliper** for ball diameter - **Stopwatch** (±0.01 s) - **Pycnometer / specific gravity bottle** for fluid density - **Thermometer** for temperature control - **Tweezers** for releasing the ball **Procedure (Stepwise):** 1. Clean the glass tube thoroughly and dry it. 2. Fill the tube with the test fluid, allowing it to settle (no air bubbles). 3. Maintain the tube at the **desired temperature** (viscosity is highly T-dependent). 4. Measure the **fluid density ρ_f** using a pycnometer. 5. Measure the **ball diameter d** with a micrometer (average of 3 readings). 6. Note the **ball density ρₛ** from the manufacturer or measure by Archimedes. 7. Mark **two reference points A and B** on the tube, distance **L** apart (typically 100–250 mm), located well away from top and bottom ends to avoid entry/exit effects. 8. Gently release the ball along the **central axis** using tweezers (avoid splash and rotation). 9. Start the stopwatch when the ball **crosses line A**; stop when it **crosses line B**. 10. Repeat **3–5 times** and take the **average time t**. 11. Calculate **vₜ = L/t** and substitute into the viscosity equation. **Sources of Error & Precautions:** | Source of Error | Precaution | |---|---| | Ball not moving along the axis | Release gently along the central axis using tweezers | | Wall effects | Use tube with **D ≥ 10d**; apply **Faxén correction** | | Air bubbles in fluid | Allow fluid to stand and settle; use degassed fluid | | Temperature variation | Maintain constant T; report viscosity with T | | Ball not smooth | Use calibrated, smooth spheres | | End effects | Mark lines A and B **away** from top and bottom (give ~5 cm clearance) | | Rotation of ball | Use small, dense, symmetric spheres; release carefully | | Timing parallax | Use **electronic timer / photogate** if available | | Turbulent flow (Re > 1) | Choose a **small ball** or **viscous fluid** to keep Re < 1 | #### Part (b) — Non-Newtonian Numerical **Given:** - d = 5 mm = 0.005 m - ρₛ = 2500 kg/m³ - ρ_f = 1000 kg/m³ - L = 200 mm = 0.2 m - t = 8.4 s **(i) Terminal velocity:** $$v_{t} = \frac{0.2}{8.4} = 0.02381 \text{ m/s}$$ **(ii) Apparent viscosity:** $$\mu_{a} = \frac{d^{2} g (\rho_{s} - \rho_{f})}{18 v_{t}}$$ $$\mu_{a} = \frac{(0.005)^{2} \cdot 9.81 \cdot (2500 - 1000)}{18 \cdot 0.02381}$$ $$\mu_{a} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 9.81 \cdot 1500}{0.42857}$$ $$\mu_{a} = \frac{0.3679}{0.42857} = 0.8584 \text{ Pa·s}$$ **(iii) Effective shear rate:** $$\dot{\gamma}_{eff} = \frac{v_{t}}{d} = \frac{0.02381}{0.005} = 4.762 \text{ s}^{-1}$$ **(iv) Comment on fluid behaviour:** - At γ̇ ≈ 4.76 s⁻¹, the measured **μₐ = 0.858 Pa·s**. - At γ̇ = 10 s⁻¹, the literature value is **0.4 Pa·s**. - Since viscosity **decreases** as the shear rate **increases** (0.858 Pa·s at 4.76 s⁻¹ → 0.4 Pa·s at 10 s⁻¹), the fluid exhibits **shear-thinning (pseudoplastic)** behaviour. ✅ > [!WARNING] KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks > 1. **Forgetting to convert units**: d in mm, L in cm, t in s — always convert to **SI (m, s)** before substituting. > 2. **Mixing up ρₛ and ρ_f**: The **denser** material (sphere) is ρₛ; the **fluid** is ρ_f. Swapping them gives a **negative viscosity** (zero marks!). > 3. **Ignoring Reynolds number check**: Examiners explicitly ask "verify Stokes' law validity." Skipping the Re calculation loses **1–2 marks**. > 4. **Forgetting wall correction**: When d/D > 0.05, applying Faxén correction is expected. > 5. **Reporting non-Newtonian result as μ (true)**: It must be called **apparent viscosity μₐ** with the **shear rate** stated. > 6. **Forgetting buoyancy**: Some students drop the (ρₛ − ρ_f) term and write (ρₛ) only — this is wrong by definition. > 7. **Sign convention error**: Drag acts **upward**, weight **downward**; clear free-body diagram earns full marks. > 8. **No replication**: Always report **average of multiple readings** with a standard deviation. > 9. **Not justifying fluid classification**: For non-Newtonian, you must **state the shear-thinning/thickening** nature explicitly. > 10. **Skipping units in the final answer**: Always state "**Pa·s**" or "**poise**" with the numerical value. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🧠 > [!IMPORTANT] Rapid Revision Checklist (Save for Last-Minute Review) > - **Aim**: Find viscosity from the terminal velocity of a falling sphere. > - **Core equation (Newtonian)**: $\mu = \dfrac{d^{2} g (\rho_{s} - \rho_{f})}{18 v_{t}}$ > - **Core equation (Non-Newtonian)**: $\mu_{a} = \dfrac{d^{2} g (\rho_{s} - \rho_{f})}{18 v_{t}}$ — but reported as **apparent viscosity** at shear rate $\dot{\gamma} = v_{t}/d$. > - **Stokes' Law**: $F_{d} = 6 \pi \mu r v$ (valid only when **Re < 1**). > - **Reynolds number**: $Re = \dfrac{\rho_{f} v_{t} d}{\mu}$ > - **Wall correction (Faxén)**: Multiply μ by $C_{w} = \dfrac{1}{1 - 2.104(d/D) + 2.09(d/D)^{3} - 0.95(d/D)^{5}}$ > - **Newtonian fluids**: Water, air, glycerine, castor oil, light oils — viscosity **constant** with shear rate. > - **Non-Newtonian fluids**: Blood, paint, ketchup, CMC, toothpaste — viscosity **varies** with shear rate. > - **Shear-thinning (pseudoplastic)**: Viscosity ↓ as shear rate ↑ (e.g., CMC, blood, paints). > - **Shear-thickening (dilatant)**: Viscosity ↑ as shear rate ↑ (e.g., starch suspension, wet sand). > - **Bingham plastic**: Requires yield stress to start flowing (e.g., toothpaste, drilling mud). > - **Force balance**: Weight (↓) = Buoyancy (↑) + Drag (↑) at terminal velocity. > - **Precautions**: Central release, avoid air bubbles, mark lines away from tube ends, D ≥ 10d, maintain constant T, use smooth spheres. > - **End effects**: Always ignore — start timing well below the fluid surface and stop well above the tube bottom. > - **Sources of error**: Wall effects, temperature drift, air bubbles, parallax in timing, off-axis motion, ball rotation, turbulent flow (Re > 1). > - **Common units**: Pa·s (SI), poise (CGS) — 1 Pa·s = 10 poise. > - **KTU classic 14-marker**: Derivation (7 marks) + numerical with validity check and wall correction (7 marks).

topics.title: Verification of Bernoulli's theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCHL308 - FLUID & PARTICLE MECHANICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Verification of Bernoulli's theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bernoulli's Theorem (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> It is a statement of the *conservation of mechanical energy* applied to a *steady, incompressible, inviscid (frictionless)* fluid flow along a *streamline*. Mathematically, the sum of the **pressure head**, the **velocity head (kinetic head)**, and the **elevation head (potential/datam head)** remains constant between any two points on the same streamline.

For a flowing fluid, the total head is given by:

$$\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{v^2}{2g} + z = H = \text{constant}$$

where:
- $P/\rho g$ = Pressure head (m)
- $v^2/2g$ = Velocity head (m)  
- $z$ = Elevation head (m)
- $H$ = Total hydraulic head (m)
- $\rho$ = Density of the fluid (kg/m³) = **1000 kg/m³** for water
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Rolling Ball on a Hill":**
> Imagine a smooth ball rolling down a frictionless hill. As it descends (elevation head decreases), it speeds up (velocity head increases), and if you squeeze it through a narrow passage, the pressure on the walls increases. The *total mechanical energy* (height energy + motion energy) is conserved. A fluid particle behaves exactly like this ball — its energy is constantly being redistributed among three forms, but the **sum never changes** (in the ideal case).

Another vivid analogy: think of a river flowing down a mountain. Where the riverbed narrows (constriction), the water moves faster, the surface level drops slightly, and the pressure on the riverbed changes — yet the *total energy budget* of every water particle is preserved.

### 1.3 Governing Assumptions (Mandatory for KTU Board Exam)

The theorem is strictly valid only when **ALL** the following conditions are satisfied:

1. **Steady flow** — flow parameters at any point do not change with time.
2. **Incompressible flow** — fluid density $\rho$ is constant.
3. **Inviscid (frictionless) flow** — viscosity is assumed to be **zero**.
4. **Flow along a streamline** — the equation applies between two points on the *same* streamline.
5. **No energy addition or extraction** — no pump, turbine, or work device between the two points.
6. **No heat transfer** — the process is isothermal and adiabatic (irreversible losses ignored).

> [!WARNING]
> **Real fluids ALWAYS have viscosity**, so the *experimentally observed total head* will be slightly *less* than the theoretical value. This difference is precisely the **head loss due to friction**, which is the key outcome students are expected to *quantify* during verification.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Breakdown of the Three Energy Heads

| Head Type | Symbol | Formula | Physical Meaning | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pressure Head** | $h_p$ | $P / (\rho g)$ | Energy due to fluid pressure per unit weight | Measured using **piezometer tubes** or **U-tube manometers** |
| **Velocity Head (Kinetic Head)** | $h_v$ | $v^2 / (2g)$ | Energy due to fluid motion per unit weight | Calculated from measured volumetric flow rate and cross-sectional area |
| **Elevation Head (Datam/Potential Head)** | $h_z$ | $z$ | Energy due to height above a datum per unit weight | Measured from a **fixed reference plane** using a pointer gauge |
| **Total Hydraulic Head** | $H$ | $h_p + h_v + h_z$ | Sum of all three energy heads | Should remain **constant** along a streamline (ideal case) |

### 2.2 Why Each Step Matters — The "How" Behind the Equation

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "per unit weight"?** — In fluid mechanics, we often express energy on a *per-unit-weight* basis (units: metres of fluid). This converts all three forms of mechanical energy into a **common length unit**, making addition and comparison straightforward. This is what makes the verification experiment work — we literally *measure heights of water columns*!

### 2.3 Velocity Calculation at Any Section (Continuity Equation)

The velocity of water at any cross-section is obtained from the volumetric flow rate $Q$ and the cross-sectional area $A$ of that section:

$$v = \frac{Q}{A}$$

For a circular section of diameter $d$:

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v = \frac{4Q}{\pi d^2}$$

The volumetric flow rate is measured using a **collecting tank** and a **stopwatch**:

$$Q = \frac{\text{Volume collected (m}^3\text{)}}{\text{Time taken (s)}} = \frac{R \times 10^{-3}}{t}$$

where $R$ is the rise in water level in the collecting tank (in litres or millilitres) and $t$ is the time in seconds.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H = P/(\rho g) + v^2/(2g) + z$ | Bernoulli's equation (total head) | m | Verification of theorem |
| 2 | $v = Q/A$ | Average velocity from continuity | m/s | Velocity at any section |
| 3 | $Q = R \times 10^{-3} / t$ | Volumetric flow rate (collecting tank method) | m³/s | Flow measurement |
| 4 | $A = (\pi/4) d^2$ | Area of circular section | m² | Cross-section of test pipe |
| 5 | $h_f = H_1 - H_2$ | Head loss between two sections | m | Quantifying friction loss |
| 6 | $\% \text{ error} = \frac{H_{\text{max}} - H_{\text{min}}}{H_{\text{mean}}} \times 100$ | Percentage deviation | % | Verifying constancy of total head |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In the table above, vertical bars are NOT used for absolute values to maintain KTU-premium markdown table integrity. All expressions use full bracket notation.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Chemical Engineering

Bernoulli's theorem is the **backbone** of fluid mechanics and finds direct application in:

- **Venturi meters, orifice meters, and rotameters** — flow measurement devices.
- **Pitot tubes** — measurement of fluid velocity in pipes and open channels.
- **Centrifugal pumps** — calculation of suction lift and developed head.
- **Design of chemical reactors** — predicting pressure drop and flow distribution.
- **Aerodynamics and process piping** — calculating pressure variations at contractions, expansions, and bends.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Bernoulli's Equation from Euler's Equation of Motion

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is **highly weighted** in KTU theory papers and viva-voce examinations. Memorise every step.

**Step 1 — Starting Point: Newton's Second Law for a Fluid Element**

Consider a small cylindrical fluid element of cross-sectional area $dA$ and length $ds$ moving along a streamline. The net force on this element in the direction of motion equals mass × acceleration (Euler's equation):

$$-dP \cdot dA - \rho g \cdot dA \cdot ds \cdot \frac{dz}{ds} = \rho \cdot dA \cdot ds \cdot a$$

where the negative signs arise because pressure and gravity components oppose motion along the streamline.

**Step 2 — Simplify by Dividing by $\rho \cdot dA \cdot ds$**

$$\frac{-dP}{\rho \cdot ds} - g \frac{dz}{ds} = a$$

**Step 3 — Express Acceleration as $v \cdot \frac{dv}{ds}$**

Since the fluid element moves along the streamline with velocity $v$ and covers a small distance $ds$:

$$a = v \frac{dv}{ds}$$

Substituting back:

$$-\frac{dP}{\rho \, ds} - g \frac{dz}{ds} = v \frac{dv}{ds}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply throughout by $ds$**

$$-\frac{dP}{\rho} - g \, dz = v \, dv$$

**Step 5 — Integrate Between Sections 1 and 2**

Integrating from point 1 (upstream) to point 2 (downstream):

$$\int_{P_1}^{P_2} -\frac{dP}{\rho} - \int_{z_1}^{z_2} g \, dz = \int_{v_1}^{v_2} v \, dv$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate Each Integral**

$$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho} - g(z_2 - z_1) = \frac{v_2^2 - v_1^2}{2}$$

**Step 7 — Rearrange to Group Point 1 Terms on the Left and Point 2 Terms on the Right**

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho} + g z_1 + \frac{v_1^2}{2} = \frac{P_2}{\rho} + g z_2 + \frac{v_2^2}{2}$$

**Step 8 — Final Form: Divide Through by $g$ to Express as Heads (metres of fluid)**

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + z_1 + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + z_2 + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} = H \text{ (constant)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Marker:** This derivation in the form of "Pressure head + Elevation head + Velocity head = Constant" is the **single most important result** in fluid mechanics. Write it in your lab record with proper units and clear section labels.

### 3.2 Sample Numerical Calculation (Model Lab Record Format)

**Given Data (Typical KTU Lab Setup):**
- Test section: a converging-diverging pipe with **3 measuring sections**.
- Section 1 (inlet, large diameter): $d_1 = 25$ mm = $0.025$ m.
- Section 2 (throat, small diameter): $d_2 = 15$ mm = $0.015$ m.
- Section 3 (outlet, large diameter): $d_3 = 25$ mm = $0.025$ m.
- Volume of water collected: $V = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m³ in $t = 10$ s.
- Density of water: $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³.
- $g = 9.81$ m/s².
- Manometer readings (pressure heads) and datum heights: tabulated below.

**Step 1 — Volumetric Flow Rate**

$$Q = \frac{V}{t} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{10} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Cross-Sectional Areas**

$$A_1 = A_3 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.025)^2 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.015)^2 = 1.767 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Velocities at Each Section**

$$v_1 = \frac{Q}{A_1} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.019 \ \text{m/s}$$

$$v_2 = \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{1.767 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.830 \ \text{m/s}$$

$$v_3 = v_1 = 1.019 \ \text{m/s} \ (\text{same diameter as Section 1})$$

**Step 4 — Velocity Heads**

$$\frac{v_1^2}{2g} = \frac{(1.019)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 0.0529 \ \text{m}$$

$$\frac{v_2^2}{2g} = \frac{(2.830)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 0.4081 \ \text{m}$$

**Step 5 — Total Head at Each Section**

Assume typical measured values (from manometer and datum gauge):
