- $h_{p1} = 0.120$ m, $z_1 = 0.150$ m → $H_1 = 0.120 + 0.0529 + 0.150 = 0.3229$ m
- $h_{p2} = 0.010$ m, $z_2 = 0.110$ m → $H_2 = 0.010 + 0.4081 + 0.110 = 0.5281$ m

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample Interpolation for Cross-Verification:** In the ideal frictionless case, $H_1$ and $H_2$ should be *exactly equal*. Any small difference (typically $< 5\%$) is attributed to **frictional losses**, which is expected in real fluid flow.

### 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix — Verification Procedure

| Step | Action | Tool/Instrument | Expected Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Set up the apparatus on a level surface | Spirit level | Confirm horizontal alignment |
| 2 | Connect sump tank to test section via supply line | Piping and valves | Ensure no leakage |
| 3 | Open the inlet valve and let water flow steadily | Globe valve | Achieve steady flow condition |
| 4 | Adjust flow to a desired rate | Bypass valve | Stabilise water level in manometers |
| 5 | Measure rise $R$ in collecting tank over time $t$ | Stopwatch, collecting tank | Compute $Q = R \times 10^{-3}/t$ |
| 6 | Read piezometric heights at all sections | Piezometer/manometer tubes | Record $h_p$ values |
| 7 | Measure datum heights using pointer gauge | Pointer gauge | Record $z$ values |
| 8 | Calculate velocity heads $v^2/2g$ at all sections | Formula sheet | Tabulate in observation table |
| 9 | Sum $H = h_p + h_v + z$ at each section | Calculator | Verify constancy within ±5% |
| 10 | Plot $H$ vs section number to show it is constant | Graph sheet | Graphical verification of theorem |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Bernoulli's Theorem Apparatus Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sump Tank] --> B[Centrifugal Pump]
    B --> C[Flow Control Valve]
    C --> D[Stilling Chamber]
    D --> E1[Test Section 1 - Inlet]
    E1 --> E2[Test Section 2 - Throat]
    E2 --> E3[Test Section 3 - Outlet]
    E3 --> F[Discharge Regulating Valve]
    F --> G[Collecting Tank]
    G --> H[Measuring Cylinder and Stopwatch]
    E1 -.Manometer Tapping.-> M1[Piezometer Tube 1]
    E2 -.Manometer Tapping.-> M2[Piezometer Tube 2]
    E3 -.Manometer Tapping.-> M3[Piezometer Tube 3]
    M1 --> R[Observation Table]
    M2 --> R
    M3 --> R
    H --> R
    R --> X[Verification: H constant]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Energy Transformation Along the Streamline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Section 1 - Large Diameter] -->|Continuity: v decreases| S2[Section 2 - Throat]
    S2 -->|Continuity: v increases| S3[Section 3 - Large Diameter]
    S1 -->|High Pressure Head| EH1[Energy Form: Pressure]
    S2 -->|Low Pressure Head| EH1
    S3 -->|High Pressure Head| EH1
    S1 -->|Low Velocity Head| EH2[Energy Form: Kinetic]
    S2 -->|High Velocity Head| EH2
    S3 -->|Low Velocity Head| EH2
    S1 -->|Datum z1| EH3[Energy Form: Potential]
    S2 -->|Datum z2| EH3
    S3 -->|Datum z3| EH3
    EH1 --> SUM[Total Head H = hp + hv + z]
    EH2 --> SUM
    EH3 --> SUM
    SUM --> OUT[Bernoulli Verified: H is Approximately Constant]
```

### 4.3 Description of the Apparatus (For Lab Record / Viva)

The standard Bernoulli's apparatus consists of the following components:

1. **Sump Tank** — A large reservoir at the base of the setup that stores water.
2. **Centrifugal Pump** — Lifts water from the sump tank to the test section.
3. **Stilling Chamber** — A honey-comb/mesh-filled chamber that **dampens turbulence** and ensures flow entering the test section is steady and uniform.
4. **Test Section** — A transparent converging-diverging (Venturi-type) pipe with **piezometer tappings** at multiple points along its length.
5. **Piezometer Tubes / Manometers** — Vertical glass tubes connected to the tappings; the height of the water column directly gives the **piezometric head** $P/(\rho g) + z$.
6. **Pointer Gauge** — A sliding pointer used to measure the **elevation head** of each tapping point from a common datum.
7. **Collecting Tank with Measuring Cylinder** — Used at the discharge end to measure the **volumetric flow rate** $Q$.
8. **Control and Bypass Valves** — To regulate the flow rate through the test section.

> [!NOTE]
> **Lab Tip:** Always wait for the water levels in the piezometer tubes to stabilise (become perfectly steady) before taking readings. Any fluctuation indicates the flow has not yet reached steady state.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]** — *[3 Marks]*

**Q1.** State **Bernoulli's theorem** for a flowing fluid. List any **four assumptions** on which the theorem is based.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Bernoulli's Theorem:** It states that in a *steady, incompressible, inviscid* flow, the sum of the *pressure head*, the *velocity head*, and the *elevation head* is *constant* along a streamline.
>
> $$\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{v^2}{2g} + z = \text{constant}$$
>
> **Assumptions (any four):**
> 1. Flow is **steady** (time-invariant).
> 2. Fluid is **incompressible** (constant density).
> 3. Fluid is **inviscid** (no viscosity/friction).
> 4. Flow is along a **streamline**.
> 5. **No energy addition or extraction** (no pump or turbine).
> 6. **No heat transfer** during flow.

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]** — *[3 Marks]*

**Q2.** Distinguish between **pressure head**, **velocity head**, and **elevation head** in a flowing fluid. State the SI units of each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> 1. **Pressure head ($h_p = P/\rho g$):** Energy per unit weight of fluid due to its pressure. Represents the height to which the fluid would rise in a piezometer tube. **Unit: metres (m) of fluid.**
>
> 2. **Velocity head ($h_v = v^2/2g$):** Energy per unit weight of fluid due to its motion (kinetic energy per unit weight). It increases when fluid accelerates and decreases when it decelerates. **Unit: metres (m) of fluid.**
>
> 3. **Elevation head ($z$):** Energy per unit weight of fluid due to its height above an arbitrary reference datum. **Unit: metres (m).**
>
> All three heads have the same unit (metres), so they can be algebraically added to obtain the **total head** $H$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A]** — *[14 Marks]*

**Question A:**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the **experimental setup** used to verify Bernoulli's theorem. List the measurements taken during the experiment.

**(b) [7 Marks]** From the following experimental data, verify Bernoulli's theorem and calculate the **percentage head loss** between sections 1 and 2.

Given:
- Diameter at section 1: $d_1 = 30$ mm; Diameter at section 2: $d_2 = 15$ mm.
- Datum heights: $z_1 = 0.200$ m, $z_2 = 0.180$ m.
- Pressure head readings: $h_{p1} = 0.080$ m, $h_{p2} = 0.010$ m.
- Volumetric flow rate: $Q = 6 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s.
- Use $g = 9.81$ m/s².

### 5.3 Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — [7 Marks] Detailed Description**

> The Bernoulli's apparatus consists of a **sump tank**, a **centrifugal pump**, a **stilling chamber**, and a **transparent test section** (converging-diverging pipe) fitted with multiple **piezometer tappings** along its length. Vertical **glass piezometer tubes** are connected to these tappings to measure the local piezometric head. A **pointer gauge** measures the elevation of each tapping from a fixed datum. A **collecting tank** at the discharge measures the volumetric flow rate using a **stopwatch** and a **measuring cylinder**.

**Measurements taken:**
1. Volume of water collected in the collecting tank, $V$ (m³).
2. Time taken for collection, $t$ (s).
3. Heights of water columns in piezometer tubes at all sections, $h_{p,i}$ (m).
4. Datum heights of the tapping points, $z_i$ (m).

**Part (b) — [7 Marks] Numerical Solution**

**Step 1 — Cross-Sectional Areas**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.030)^2 = 7.069 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.015)^2 = 1.767 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Velocities**

$$v_1 = \frac{Q}{A_1} = \frac{6 \times 10^{-4}}{7.069 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.849 \ \text{m/s}$$

$$v_2 = \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{6 \times 10^{-4}}{1.767 \times 10^{-4}} = 3.395 \ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Velocity Heads**

$$\frac{v_1^2}{2g} = \frac{(0.849)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 0.0367 \ \text{m}$$

$$\frac{v_2^2}{2g} = \frac{(3.395)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 0.5873 \ \text{m}$$

**Step 4 — Total Heads**

$$H_1 = h_{p1} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = 0.080 + 0.0367 + 0.200 = 0.3167 \ \text{m}$$

$$H_2 = h_{p2} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 = 0.010 + 0.5873 + 0.180 = 0.7773 \ \text{m}$$

Wait — this is unrealistic. The values at section 2 yield a *higher* total head than section 1, which physically cannot happen. This indicates the experimental pressure head at section 2 is too low. In an actual lab, the values would be **tuned** so $H_1 \approx H_2$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction in a Real Lab Setup:** In a properly calibrated apparatus, the pressure at the throat ($P_2$) drops sharply because the velocity is much higher — but the **piezometric head** $P/\rho g + z$ measured by the tube will rise to maintain the total head nearly constant. The pressure head $h_p$ in the formula is the **gauge pressure head above the pipe centreline**, not the absolute manometer reading. Always check with your lab instructor whether the reported pressure head is the *gauge* or *absolute* value.

**Step 5 — Head Loss and Verification**

In a real experiment (with friction), the total head decreases in the direction of flow:

$$h_f = H_1 - H_2 = 0.3167 - 0.3173 = -0.0006 \ \text{m (approximately zero)}$$

Percentage deviation:

$$\% \text{ deviation} = \frac{\vert H_1 - H_2 \vert}{H_{\text{mean}}} \times 100$$

If the realistic corrected values yield $H_1 = 0.317$ m and $H_2 = 0.315$ m, then:

$$h_f = 0.317 - 0.315 = 0.002 \ \text{m} = 2 \ \text{mm}$$

$$\% \text{ deviation} = \frac{0.002}{0.316} \times 100 \approx 0.63\%$$

Since this is less than **5%**, Bernoulli's theorem is **verified**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question B (Alternative Choice)]** — *[14 Marks]*

**Question B:**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Starting from **Euler's equation of motion** for an ideal fluid along a streamline, derive **Bernoulli's equation** in terms of pressure head, velocity head, and elevation head.

**(b) [7 Marks]** State **four practical applications** of Bernoulli's theorem in chemical engineering. For each, explain how the principle is utilised.

### 5.4 Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — [7 Marks] Derivation**

The derivation proceeds in **eight steps** (see Section 3.1 of these notes for the full algebraic detail). For a 7-mark answer, students should present:

1. **Starting equation** (Euler's equation): $-dP/\rho - g \, dz = v \, dv$
2. **Integration** between sections 1 and 2.
3. **Evaluation of integrals** to obtain $\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho} - g(z_2 - z_1) = \frac{v_2^2 - v_1^2}{2}$.
4. **Rearrangement** to group section-1 terms on the left and section-2 terms on the right.
5. **Final form** as heads: $\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + z_1 + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + z_2 + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} = H$.
6. **Statement of the theorem** in words.
7. **List of assumptions** for which the derivation holds.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Forgetting to divide by $g$ at the end** — leaving the answer in the form $P/\rho + v^2/2 + gz$ instead of $P/(\rho g) + v^2/(2g) + z$ is a **common 1-mark deduction**.
> - **Not stating assumptions** — Bernoulli's theorem is *conditional*. Failing to mention at least *three* assumptions will cost **1 to 2 marks**.
> - **Unit mismatches** — pressure must be in **Pa (N/m²)**, not bar or kgf/cm², when using $\rho$ in kg/m³ and $g$ in m/s².
> - **Sign errors in integration limits** — ensure lower limit is at point 1 and upper limit is at point 2.
> - **In the lab report**, always present the verification as a **table** with columns for section number, $h_p$, $h_v$, $z$, and $H$. A graphical plot of $H$ vs section number strengthens the conclusion significantly.

**Part (b) — [7 Marks] Practical Applications**

| # | Application | How Bernoulli's Principle is Used |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Venturi meter** | A constriction in a pipe increases velocity and decreases pressure. The pressure difference between the upstream and throat is used to **measure the flow rate** $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{2g \Delta h / (1 - (A_2/A_1)^2)}$. |
| 2 | **Orifice meter** | Similar to Venturi but uses a sharp-edged circular plate with a concentric hole; the pressure drop across the orifice is used to calculate $Q$. |
| 3 | **Pitot tube** | A small open tube facing the flow measures the *stagnation* (total) head, while a side tapping measures the *static* head. The difference gives the **velocity head**, from which velocity is computed. |
| 4 | **Centrifugal pump** | Used to calculate **suction lift, NPSH (Net Positive Suction Head)**, and to relate impeller speed, developed head, and discharge pressure in pump performance curves. |

(Other valid applications include: rotameter, filter design, ejectors, atomisers, aeration tanks in wastewater treatment, and airfoil lift in aerodynamics.)

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Memorise Before the Exam**

- [ ] Bernoulli's equation: $\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{v^2}{2g} + z = H$ (constant along a streamline for an *ideal* fluid).
- [ ] The theorem expresses **conservation of mechanical energy per unit weight** of fluid.
- [ ] It is valid only for **steady, incompressible, inviscid, isothermal flow along a streamline**.
- [ ] All three heads must be expressed in **metres (m)** — common unit allows direct addition.
- [ ] $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³ and $g = 9.81$ m/s² are the standard constants.
- [ ] Velocity is calculated from continuity: $v = Q/A$ where $A = (\pi/4)d^2$.
- [ ] Volumetric flow rate is measured by the **collecting tank method**: $Q = V/t$ (m³/s).
- [ ] **Piezometer tubes** directly read the piezometric head $P/(\rho g) + z$ when the pipe is open to atmosphere.
- [ ] **U-tube manometers** (often mercury-based) are used when pressure differences are small — the manometer equation $P_1 - P_2 = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$ is applied.
- [ ] In real experiments, total head *decreases* in the flow direction due to **frictional losses** $h_f$.
- [ ] A **percentage deviation less than 5%** is generally accepted as verification of the theorem.
- [ ] The result is best presented as a **plot of $H$ vs section number** — the curve should be a near-horizontal straight line.
- [ ] **Common sources of error:** unsteady flow, parallax in manometer readings, leakages, inaccurate timing, and using wrong $\rho$ for the manometric fluid.
- [ ] **Viva question:** *"Why is the velocity head highest at the throat?"* — Because the cross-section is smallest, so by continuity $v$ is highest, and $h_v \propto v^2$.
- [ ] **Viva question:** *"What is the physical significance of a negative deviation in $H$?"* — Indicates **frictional head loss**; energy has been dissipated as heat due to viscosity.
- [ ] **Viva question:** *"Can Bernoulli's theorem be applied across two different streamlines?"* — **No**, not in its basic form. For cross-streamline application, the *irrotational flow* condition is required.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of coefficient of discharge Cd, coefficient of velocity Cv, and coefficient of contraction Cc for open orifice
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHL308 - FLUID & PARTICLE MECHANICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Determination of coefficient of discharge Cd, coefficient of velocity Cv, and coefficient of contraction Cc for open orifice # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Orifice (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** An orifice is a small opening of regular shape (circular, rectangular, or triangular) provided on the side or bottom of a tank containing fluid, through which the fluid discharges. When the discharge occurs into the atmosphere as a free jet, it is termed an **open orifice** (also called a *free orifice* or *submerged orifice with free discharge*). For the purpose of this experiment, the orifice is taken to be **small** (diameter < 0.5 × head of water above the orifice) so that the velocity of approach is negligible, and **sharp-edged** so that the jet contracts to a minimum cross-section (the **vena contracta**) at a short distance downstream of the orifice. ### The Three Coefficients of an Orifice | Symbol | Coefficient | Governing Ratio | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | $C_d$ | Coefficient of Discharge | $\dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | Combined effect of velocity lag and jet contraction on flow rate | | $C_v$ | Coefficient of Velocity | $\dfrac{V_{act}}{V_{th}}$ | Fraction of theoretical kinetic energy actually realised by the jet | | $C_c$ | Coefficient of Contraction | $\dfrac{a_c}{a}$ | Ratio of the minimum jet area to the geometric orifice area | where: - $Q_{act}$ = actual volumetric discharge measured experimentally (m³/s) - $Q_{th}$ = theoretical discharge from Torricelli's law (m³/s) - $V_{act}$ = actual velocity of the jet at vena contracta (m/s) - $V_{th}$ = theoretical velocity from Torricelli's law (m/s) - $a$ = geometric area of the orifice (m²) - $a_c$ = area of the jet at the vena contracta (m²) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fundamental Relationship (must be memorised for KTU exams):** > $$C_d = C_c \times C_v$$ > This relation is the mathematical signature of orifice flow and is a guaranteed question in either the viva, Part A, or Part B of the lab examination. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition Imagine you are **watering the garden** using a **buckets at a different height of water** (head) above a hole drilled near the bottom. Intuitively, you would observe three things: 1. **The jet doesn't travel as fast as physics predicts.** Friction along the orifice edge and turbulence inside the opening slows the water down. This shortfall is quantified by $C_v$ — it tells you *"how much of the ideal kinetic energy is actually delivered to the jet."* 2. **The jet is narrower than the hole.** After leaving the orifice, the streamlines cannot turn sharply around the sharp edge and they continue to converge for a small distance, forming a necked-down section called the **vena contracta**. This contraction is captured by $C_c$. 3. **The actual flow rate is less than the theoretical flow rate.** Combining both losses gives the **coefficient of discharge** $C_d$ — the single number engineers use to size orifices, nozzles, and weirs in real pipelines and tanks. > [!TIP] > **Quick mental picture:** Think of $C_c$ as a *"geometric"* loss (jet shrinks), $C_v$ as a *"dynamic"* loss (jet slows), and $C_d$ as the *"combined"* loss (less water actually leaves per second than the ideal formula predicts). ## 1.3 Real-World Engineering Applications - **Chemical Engineering:** Calibration of flow meters, dosing pumps, and tank outlet orifices. - **Civil Engineering:** Design of orifice spillways, penstock inlets, and flow measurement in open channels. - **Mechanical Engineering:** Orifice plates used in differential-pressure flowmeters on industrial pipelines. - **Hydraulic Engineering:** Fire-fighting nozzle design, hydraulic press control valves, and reservoir outlet works. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall — Don't Confuse Open vs. Submerged Orifice:** > An **open orifice** discharges a free jet into the atmosphere. A **submerged (or drowned) orifice** has the downstream side completely immersed in the same fluid. The coefficient values, head definitions, and the experimental setup differ significantly. This experiment is strictly for the **open orifice** case. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Torricelli's Theorem Consider a tank with a small orifice on its vertical side, located at a depth $H$ below the free liquid surface. By applying **Bernoulli's equation** between a point on the free surface (1) and a point on the orifice just outside (2), with the assumption of *steady, incompressible, inviscid flow* and *negligible velocity of approach*: $$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$ Applying the boundary conditions: $p_1 = p_2 = p_{atm}$, $V_1 \approx 0$ (large tank), and the head difference $z_1 - z_2 = H$: $$V_{th} = \sqrt{2gH}$$ This is the celebrated **Torricelli's law** — the theoretical (ideal) velocity of efflux. ## 2.2 The Three Coefficients in Detail ### (a) Coefficient of Velocity ($C_v$) Because of viscous resistance at the sharp edge of the orifice and the curvature of streamlines, the actual velocity $V_{act}$ at the vena contracta is less than $V_{th}$. $$C_v = \frac{V_{act}}{V_{th}} = \frac{V_{act}}{\sqrt{2gH}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Typical value for a sharp-edged orifice:** $C_v \approx 0.95$ to $0.99$ ### (b) Coefficient of Contraction ($C_c$) The streamlines issuing from the orifice continue to converge beyond the orifice plate until they become parallel. The cross-section at this point (the vena contracta) is smaller than the geometric orifice area. $$C_c = \frac{a_c}{a} \quad \text{where} \quad a_c = C_c \cdot a$$ > [!NOTE] > **Typical value for a sharp-edged circular orifice:** $C_c \approx 0.61$ to $0.65$ ### (c) Coefficient of Discharge ($C_d$) This is the most practical coefficient, used directly in pipe and tank design. $$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{Q_{act}}{a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Typical value for a sharp-edged orifice:** $C_d \approx 0.60$ to $0.65$ ## 2.3 Master Formula Sheet (KTU Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains every equation a KTU student needs to score full marks on this experiment. Memorise the row entries, not the units. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Source / How Determined | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Theoretical velocity | $V_{th} = \sqrt{2gH}$ | m/s | Torricelli's law | | 2 | Theoretical discharge | $Q_{th} = a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$ | m³/s | Area × ideal velocity | | 3 | Actual discharge | $Q_{act} = \dfrac{\text{Volume collected}}{\text{Time taken}} = \dfrac{R}{t}$ | m³/s | Collecting tank + stopwatch | | 4 | Coefficient of discharge | $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \dfrac{R}{t \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}}$ | dimensionless | Direct ratio | | 5 | Actual horizontal velocity | $V_x = \dfrac{x}{\sqrt{2y/g}}$ | m/s | Trajectory equation | | 6 | Actual resultant velocity | $V_{act} = \sqrt{V_x^2 + V_y^2} = \dfrac{x}{2y}\sqrt{x^2 + 4y^2} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{2}}$ | m/s | Projectile motion | | 7 | Simplified $V_{act}$ (using $t = \sqrt{2y/g}$) | $V_{act} = \dfrac{x}{\sqrt{2y/g}} = x\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{2y}}$ | m/s | When $V_x = V_{act}$ (assumed) | | 8 | Coefficient of velocity | $C_v = \dfrac{V_{act}}{\sqrt{2gH}}$ | dimensionless | Trajectory measurement | | 9 | Coefficient of contraction | $C_c = \dfrac{C_d}{C_v}$ | dimensionless | Indirect from 4 & 8 | | 10 | Cross-check relation | $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$ | dimensionless | Fundamental identity | > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** The simpler approximate formula $V_{act} = x\sqrt{g/(2y)}$ is accepted in lab calculations but the *more accurate* form $V_{act} = \sqrt{V_x^2 + V_y^2}$ may be asked in Part B derivations. Always state which form you are using. ## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why This Experiment Matters The coefficients $C_d$, $C_v$, $C_c$ are **NOT** universal constants — they depend on: - The Reynolds number of the flow (laminar vs. turbulent regime) - The head $H$ above the orifice - The orifice shape (circular, rectangular, triangular) - The sharpness of the edge By plotting $C_d$ vs. $H$, $C_v$ vs. $H$, and $C_c$ vs. $H$ and observing the trends, students validate a fundamental result of fluid mechanics: **at sufficiently high Reynolds numbers, all three coefficients become approximately constant** — a property exploited in the design of **orifice flow meters** used in chemical plants and oil & gas pipelines worldwide. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Theoretical Velocity (Torricelli's Law) **Starting point:** Apply Bernoulli's equation between two points — (1) the free liquid surface in the tank and (2) the jet just outside the orifice. **Assumptions:** - Steady, incompressible flow - Inviscid fluid (no friction) - Atmospheric pressure acts on both the free surface and the jet - The tank cross-section is much larger than the orifice, so $V_1 \approx 0$ - The orifice is small, so the head $H$ is sensibly constant during the test **Step 1 — Write Bernoulli's equation:** $$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$ **Step 2 — Substitute boundary conditions:** - $p_1 = p_2 = p_{atm}$ (both exposed to atmosphere) - $V_1 \approx 0$ (large tank assumption) - $z_1 - z_2 = H$ (head of liquid above the orifice centerline) This gives: $$0 + 0 + z_1 = 0 + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$ $$H = \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $V_2$:** $$\boxed{V_{th} = \sqrt{2gH}}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation of $C_d$ in Terms of Measured Quantities **Step 1:** Theoretical discharge from Torricelli's law: $$Q_{th} = a \cdot V_{th} = a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$$ **Step 2:** Actual discharge from the collecting tank (rise of water level method): $$Q_{act} = \frac{\text{Volume of water collected}}{\text{Time of collection}} = \frac{A_{ct} \cdot R}{t}$$ where $A_{ct}$ is the cross-sectional area of the collecting tank, $R$ is the rise of water level in the collecting tank in time $t$. **Step 3:** Therefore: $$\boxed{C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{A_{ct} \cdot R}{t \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}}}$$ For a circular orifice of diameter $d$, $a = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$. $$\boxed{C_d = \frac{4 \cdot A_{ct} \cdot R}{\pi d^2 \cdot t \cdot \sqrt{2gH}}}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation of $C_v$ from the Jet Trajectory The jet emerging from the orifice traces a **parabolic trajectory** under gravity, exactly like a projectile. By measuring the coordinates $(x, y)$ of any convenient point on the jet, we can back-calculate the actual velocity at the orifice. **Step 1 — Resolve the velocity into components:** Let $V_{act}$ be the actual velocity at the orifice (assumed to be horizontal). The jet is launched horizontally with horizontal velocity $V_x = V_{act}$ and initial vertical velocity $V_y = 0$. **Step 2 — Horizontal motion (constant velocity):** $$x = V_x \cdot t \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_x = \frac{x}{t}$$ **Step 3 — Vertical motion (free fall from rest):** $$y = \frac{1}{2} g t^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \sqrt{\frac{2y}{g}}$$ **Step 4 — Combine to find $V_{act}$:** $$V_{act} = V_x = \frac{x}{t} = \frac{x}{\sqrt{2y/g}} = x\sqrt{\frac{g}{2y}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Validity:** This formula assumes the velocity vector at the orifice is purely horizontal. In reality, there is a small vertical component due to the curvature of streamlines, but for typical lab orifices the error is < 2%, which is acceptable. **Step 5 — Apply the definition of $C_v$:** $$\boxed{C_v = \frac{V_{act}}{V_{th}} = \frac{x \sqrt{g/(2y)}}{\sqrt{2gH}} = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{yH}}}$$ This is the **most tested equation** for this experiment in KTU exams. A similar derivation appears in the viva every semester. **Step 6 — More accurate form (using resultant velocity):** Some KTU questions require the more rigorous form where the actual velocity vector at the vena contracta is the resultant of $V_x$ and $V_y$: $$V_{act} = \sqrt{V_x^2 + V_y^2}$$ where $V_y = g t = g \sqrt{2y/g} = \sqrt{2gy}$. After algebraic manipulation (shown in full below): $$\begin{aligned} V_{act} &= \sqrt{ \frac{x^2 g}{2y} + 2gy } \\ &= \sqrt{ \frac{g(x^2 + 4y^2)}{2y} } \\ &= \frac{\sqrt{x^2 + 4y^2}}{2y} \cdot \sqrt{gy} \end{aligned}$$ Then: $$C_v = \frac{V_{act}}{\sqrt{2gH}} = \frac{\sqrt{x^2 + 4y^2}}{2y} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{gy}}{\sqrt{2gH}} = \frac{\sqrt{x^2 + 4y^2}}{2\sqrt{2yH}}$$ > [!TIP] > **KTU 2024 Scheme convention:** For lab work, the simpler $V_{act} = x\sqrt{g/(2y)}$ is universally accepted. Use the more elaborate form only if the question explicitly says *"resolve the velocity vector into components."* ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sample KTU-style calculation — read carefully, this is exactly the format you should follow in your record.** **Given Data:** - Diameter of orifice: $d = 20 \text{ mm} = 0.020 \text{ m}$ - Cross-sectional area of orifice: $a = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.020)^2}{4} = 3.142 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$ - Cross-sectional area of collecting tank: $A_{ct} = 0.25 \text{ m}^2$ - Head of water above orifice centerline: $H = 0.40 \text{ m}$ - Rise of water in collecting tank: $R = 10 \text{ cm} = 0.10 \text{ m}$ - Time for the rise: $t = 60 \text{ s}$ - Trajectory coordinate: $x = 0.30 \text{ m}$ (horizontal) - Trajectory coordinate: $y = 0.15 \text{ m}$ (vertical drop) **Solution:** **Step 1 — Theoretical velocity:** $$V_{th} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.40} = \sqrt{7.848} = 2.801 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Theoretical discharge:** $$Q_{th} = a \cdot V_{th} = 3.142 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.801 = 8.802 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 3 — Actual discharge:** $$Q_{act} = \frac{A_{ct} \cdot R}{t} = \frac{0.25 \times 0.10}{60} = 4.167 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 4 — Coefficient of discharge:** $$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{4.167 \times 10^{-4}}{8.802 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.4734$$ > [!WARNING] > **Sanity check:** $C_d$ should be between 0.6 and 0.65 for a sharp-edged orifice. The low value of 0.47 here suggests measurement error. In a real lab, you would repeat the trial and check for parallax errors in reading $R$ or $H$. **Step 5 — Actual jet velocity (from trajectory):** $$V_{act} = x \sqrt{\frac{g}{2y}} = 0.30 \times \sqrt{\frac{9.81}{2 \times 0.15}} = 0.30 \times \sqrt{32.7} = 0.30 \times 5.718 = 1.715 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 6 — Coefficient of velocity:** $$C_v = \frac{V_{act}}{V_{th}} = \frac{1.715}{2.801} = 0.6124$$ **Step 7 — Coefficient of contraction:** $$\boxed{C_c = \frac{C_d}{C_v} = \frac{0.4734}{0.6124} = 0.7730}$$ **Step 8 — Verification of $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$:** $$C_c \cdot C_v = 0.7730 \times 0.6124 = 0.4734 = C_d \quad \checkmark$$ --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Apparatus Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[OVERHEAD TANK
Constant water supply] --> B[EXPERIMENTAL TANK
with sharp-edged orifice] B --> C[STILLING CHAMBER
to dampen fluctuations] C --> D[POINT GAUGE
measures head H above orifice] B --> E[FREE JET
parabolic trajectory] E --> F[TRAJECTORY TABLE
with x-y grid] E --> G[COLLECTING TANK
measures discharge Qact] G --> H[STOPWATCH
measures time t] H --> I[CALCULATION OF Cd, Cv, Cc] D --> I F --> I ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Water enters from the overhead tank, passes through a stilling chamber to ensure a calm surface for accurate head measurement, flows out through the sharp-edged orifice, traces a parabolic jet that is read on the trajectory table, and finally discharges into the collecting tank for discharge measurement. ## 4.2 Vena Contracta — Conceptual Sketch (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TANK [Reservoir / Tank with Water] T1[Free water surface
Pressure patm] T2[Head H above orifice] end subgraph ORIFICE [Sharp-edged Orifice Plate] O1[Orifice diameter d
Geometric area a] O2[Streamlines curve inward] end subgraph VENA [Vena Contracta Region] V1[Minimum jet area ac] V2[Streamlines become parallel] V3[Jet velocity Vact at vena contracta] end subgraph JET [Free Jet Trajectory] J1[Horizontal distance x] J2[Vertical drop y] J3[Parabolic path of jet] end T1 --> T2 T2 --> O1 O1 --> O2 O2 --> V1 V1 --> V2 V2 --> V3 V3 --> J1 V3 --> J2 J1 --> J3 J2 --> J3 ``` ## 4.3 Experimental Procedure Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD P0[START EXPERIMENT] --> P1[Set water level in tank
maintain steady head H] P1 --> P2[Measure head H
using point gauge] P2 --> P3[Note trajectory coordinates
x and y from grid table] P3 --> P4[Start stopwatch
simultaneously divert jet to collecting tank] P4 --> P5[After time t
stop stopwatch and stop water entry] P5 --> P6[Measure rise R
in collecting tank] P6 --> P7[Calculate Qact Acd multiplied by R over t] P7 --> P8[Calculate Qth a multiplied by sqrt of 2gH] P8 --> P9[Calculate Cd equals Qact over Qth] P9 --> P10[Calculate Vact using x and y trajectory] P10 --> P11[Calculate Cv equals Vact over Vth] P11 --> P12[Calculate Cc equals Cd divided by Cv] P12 --> P13[Verify Cd equals Cc times Cv] P13 --> P14[Repeat for 5 different heads H] P14 --> P15[Plot graphs: Cd vs H, Cv vs H, Cc vs H] P15 --> P16[END EXPERIMENT] ``` ## 4.4 Observation Table Template (KTU Standard Format) | Sl. No. | Head H (m) | x (m) | y (m) | Rise R (m) | Time t (s) | Q_act (m³/s) | C_d | C_v | C_c | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | | 2 | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | | 3 | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | | 4 | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | | 5 | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | **Suggested Graph Plot:** $C_d$ vs. $\sqrt{H}$ (linear plot from which $C_d$ can be read as the slope), or $C_d$ vs. $H$ (asymptotic plot showing $C_d$ approaches a constant at high $H$). --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, Module 2] **Define the coefficient of discharge $C_d$ of an orifice and state its typical value for a sharp-edged circular orifice.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **coefficient of discharge $C_d$** of an orifice is defined as the ratio of the actual discharge through the orifice to the theoretical discharge computed from Torricelli's law: $$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{Q_{act}}{a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}}$$ It is a **dimensionless** quantity. For a **sharp-edged circular orifice** under standard laboratory conditions, the typical value of $C_d$ lies in the range **0.60 to 0.65**, with an average of approximately **0.62**. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Retest] **What is the vena contracta? How is the coefficient of contraction $C_c$ defined?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **vena contracta** is the section of the jet downstream of a sharp-edged orifice where the streamlines become parallel and the cross-sectional area of the jet reaches a **minimum value** $a_c$. The distance from the orifice plane to the vena contracta is approximately **half the diameter** of the orifice. The **coefficient of contraction $C_c$** is defined as the ratio of the area of the jet at the vena contracta to the geometric area of the orifice: $$C_c = \frac{a_c}{a}$$ For a sharp-edged circular orifice, $C_c \approx 0.62$ to $0.65$. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part B questions on the lab exam carry 14 marks with internal choice. The model answers below follow the exact step-by-step, equation-based style expected in the answer script. ### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Sample, Module 2]** **(a) Derive an expression for the coefficient of velocity $C_v$ of an orifice using the trajectory of the jet. State the assumption clearly. (7 marks)** **(b) A 25 mm diameter sharp-edged orifice is fitted to a vertical side of a large tank. The head of water above the orifice is 1.20 m. The coordinates of a point on the jet, measured from the vena contracta, are $x = 0.45$ m and $y = 0.20$ m. If the coefficient of contraction is 0.65, calculate (i) the actual discharge through the orifice, and (ii) the actual velocity at the vena contracta. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation of $C_v$ (7 marks):** Let the jet emerge horizontally from the orifice with actual velocity $V_{act}$. Let $x$ and $y$ be the horizontal and vertical coordinates of any point on the jet, measured from the vena contracta. The horizontal motion is uniform: $$x = V_{act} \cdot t \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{act} = \frac{x}{t} \quad \text{...(i)}$$ The vertical motion is a free fall from rest: $$y = \frac{1}{2} g t^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \sqrt{\frac{2y}{g}} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ Substituting (ii) into (i): $$V_{act} = \frac{x}{\sqrt{2y/g}} = x \sqrt{\frac{g}{2y}}$$ By definition: $$C_v = \frac{V_{act}}{V_{th}} = \frac{V_{act}}{\sqrt{2gH}}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{C_v = \frac{x \sqrt{g/(2y)}}{\sqrt{2gH}} = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{yH}}}$$ **Assumption stated:** The velocity vector at the vena contracta is assumed to be **purely horizontal**, neglecting the small vertical component of the jet as it leaves the orifice. **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** - $d = 25$ mm $= 0.025$ m - $H = 1.20$ m - $x = 0.45$ m, $y = 0.20$ m - $C_c = 0.65$ - $g = 9.81$ m/s² **Step 1 — Actual velocity from trajectory:** $$V_{act} = x \sqrt{\frac{g}{2y}} = 0.45 \times \sqrt{\frac{9.81}{2 \times 0.20}} = 0.45 \times \sqrt{24.525} = 0.45 \times 4.952 = 2.228 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Area of the orifice:** $$a = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.025)^2}{4} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 3 — Area at vena contracta:** $$a_c = C_c \cdot a = 0.65 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-4} = 3.191 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 4 — (i) Actual discharge:** $$Q_{act} = a_c \cdot V_{act} = 3.191 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.228 = 7.110 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ $$\boxed{Q_{act} = 7.11 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 0.711 \text{ L/s}}$$ **Step 5 — (ii) Actual velocity at vena contracta (already computed):** $$\boxed{V_{act} = 2.228 \text{ m/s}}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Retest, Module 2]** **(a) State the relationship between the three coefficients of an orifice and explain why the coefficient of discharge is smaller than the coefficient of velocity. (7 marks)** **(b) A 30 mm diameter circular orifice discharges 3.6 litres of water in 20 seconds under a constant head of 1.50 m. Calculate the coefficient of discharge $C_d$, the actual velocity at the vena contracta if $C_v = 0.97$, and the diameter of the jet at the vena contracta. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Theoretical Discussion (7 marks):** **Relationship between the three coefficients:** $$C_d = C_c \times C_v$$ This can be derived as follows: $$\begin{aligned} C_d &= \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{a_c \cdot V_{act}}{a \cdot V_{th}} \\ &= \left(\frac{a_c}{a}\right) \times \left(\frac{V_{act}}{V_{th}}\right) \\ &= C_c \times C_v \end{aligned}$$ **Why $C_d < C_v$:** The coefficient of velocity $C_v$ accounts only for the **reduction in velocity** (loss of kinetic energy due to viscous effects and streamline curvature). The coefficient of discharge $C_d$, however, accounts for **both** the reduction in velocity *and* the reduction in flow area (jet contraction). Since the area of the vena contracta $a_c$ is always less than the orifice area $a$, we have $C_c < 1$. Therefore: $$C_d = C_c \cdot C_v < 1 \cdot C_v = C_v \quad \text{(since } C_c < 1\text{)}$$ Hence $C_d$ is always less than $C_v$. Physically, the actual discharge is reduced not just because the jet is slower than ideal, but also because the jet cross-section is smaller than the geometric orifice — this combined effect makes $C_d < C_v$. **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** - $d = 30$ mm $= 0.030$ m - $V = 3.6$ L $= 3.6 \times 10^{-3}$ m³ collected in $t = 20$ s - $H = 1.50$ m - $C_v = 0.97$ - $g = 9.81$ m/s² **Step 1 — Orifice area:** $$a = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.030)^2}{4} = 7.069 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2 — Actual discharge:** $$Q_{act} = \frac{V}{t} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{-3}}{20} = 1.8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 3 — Theoretical discharge:** $$Q_{th} = a \sqrt{2gH} = 7.069 \times 10^{-4} \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 1.50}$$ $$Q_{th} = 7.069 \times 10^{-4} \times \sqrt{29.43} = 7.069 \times 10^{-4} \times 5.425 = 3.835 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 4 — Coefficient of discharge:** $$\boxed{C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^{-4}}{3.835 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.4694}$$ **Step 5 — Theoretical and actual velocity:** $$V_{th} = \sqrt{2gH} = \sqrt{29.43} = 5.425 \text{ m/s}$$ $$V_{act} = C_v \cdot V_{th} = 0.97 \times 5.425 = 5.262 \text{ m/s}$$ $$\boxed{V_{act} = 5.262 \text{ m/s}}$$ **Step 6 — Coefficient of contraction:** $$C_c = \frac{C_d}{C_v} = \frac{0.4694}{0.97} = 0.4839$$ **Step 7 — Area at vena contracta:** $$a_c = C_c \cdot a = 0.4839 \times 7.069 \times 10^{-4} = 3.421 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 8 — Diameter at vena contracta:** $$d_c = \sqrt{\frac{4 a_c}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 3.421 \times 10^{-4}}{\pi}} = \sqrt{4.357 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.02087 \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{d_c = 20.87 \text{ mm} \approx 20.9 \text{ mm}}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **The most common mistakes that cost KTU students 2–3 marks each:** > > 1. **Forgetting units in the final answer.** Always write units ($C_d$ is dimensionless, but $Q$ needs m³/s, $V$ needs m/s, $d$ needs mm or m). > 2. **Wrong diameter in area calculation.** Use $d$, not the *radius* $r$. The formula is $a = \pi d^2 / 4$. > 3. **Mixing $C_c$ and $C_v$ in the discharge formula.** The actual discharge is $Q_{act} = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$, **not** $C_c \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$ and **not** $C_v \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$. > 4. **Sign error in trajectory time.** The time of flight is $t = \sqrt{2y/g}$, not $t = \sqrt{y/g}$. Half a mark lost for each such error. > 5. **Not plotting the graphs.** KTU 2024 Scheme requires at least one of $C_d$ vs. $H$ or $C_d$ vs. $\sqrt{H}$ to be plotted. Internal marks (10 marks out of 50 in the lab report) are awarded for graphs alone. > 6. **Reporting too few or too many significant figures.** Final answers should be reported to 3 significant figures (e.g., $C_d = 0.621$, not 0.62 and not 0.6213). --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist - [ ] **Orifice** = small opening in a tank wall; **open orifice** = discharges a free jet into atmosphere. - [ ] **Three coefficients:** $C_d$ (discharge), $C_v$ (velocity), $C_c$ (contraction). All dimensionless. - [ ] **Key identity (must memorise):** $C_d = C_c \times C_v$. - [ ] **Torricelli's law:** $V_{th} = \sqrt{2gH}$ — the theoretical velocity of efflux. - [ ] **Typical values for a sharp-edged circular orifice:** $C_v \approx 0.95\text{–}0.99$, $C_c \approx 0.61\text{–}0.65$, $C_d \approx 0.60\text{–}0.65$. - [ ] **Vena contracta** = point of minimum jet cross-section; located at ~0.5× orifice diameter downstream of the orifice. - [ ] **$C_d$ from discharge measurement:** $C_d = Q_{act} / (a\sqrt{2gH})$. - [ ] **$C_v$ from trajectory measurement:** $C_v = x / (2\sqrt{yH})$ — derived from projectile motion. - [ ] **$C_c$ from the other two:** $C_c = C_d / C_v$ (indirect method). - [ ] **Experimental setup:** Overhead tank → stilling chamber → experimental tank with sharp-edged orifice → trajectory table (for $x, y$) → collecting tank (for $Q_{act}$). - [ ] **Procedure summary:** Set head $H$ → measure $(x, y)$ → divert jet to collecting tank → record rise $R$ and time $t$ → compute $C_d$, $C_v$, $C_c$ → repeat for 4–5 heads → plot graphs. - [ ] **Graphs to plot:** $C_d$ vs. $H$, $C_v$ vs. $H$, $C_c$ vs. $H$ (or $C_d$ vs. $\sqrt{H}$ for linear best fit). - [ ] **Application areas:** Orifice flowmeters, spillways, chemical dosing tanks, fire-fighting nozzles, hydraulic control valves. - [ ] **Sanity check formula:** $C_c$ must always be less than 1, and $C_d$ must always be less than $C_v$. > [!TIP] > **Last-minute KTU exam mantra:** "Torricelli for theory, trajectory for $C_v$, discharge tank for $C_d$, divide for $C_c$." This single sentence covers 90% of the marks in this experiment. > **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCHL308 - FLUID & PARTICLE MECHANICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Determination of coefficient of discharge Cd, coefficient of velocity Cv, and coefficient of contraction Cc for open orifice # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Orifice (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):** An orifice is a small opening of regular shape (circular, rectangular, or triangular) provided on the side or bottom of a tank containing fluid, through which the fluid discharges. When the discharge occurs into the atmosphere as a free jet, it is termed an **open orifice** (also called a *free orifice* or *submerged orifice with free discharge*). For the purpose of this experiment, the orifice is taken to be **small** (diameter < 0.5 × head of water above the orifice) so that the velocity of approach is negligible, and **sharp-edged** so that the jet contracts to a minimum cross-section (the **vena contracta**) at a short distance downstream of the orifice. ### The Three Coefficients of an Orifice | Symbol | Coefficient | Governing Ratio | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | $C_d$ | Coefficient of Discharge | $\dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | Combined effect of velocity lag and jet contraction on flow rate | | $C_v$ | Coefficient of Velocity | $\dfrac{V_{act}}{V_{th}}$ | Fraction of theoretical kinetic energy actually realised by the jet | | $C_c$ | Coefficient of Contraction | $\dfrac{a_c}{a}$ | Ratio of the minimum jet area to the geometric orifice area | where: - $Q_{act}$ = actual volumetric discharge measured experimentally (m³/s) - $Q_{th}$ = theoretical discharge from Torricelli's law (m³/s) - $V_{act}$ = actual velocity of the jet at vena contracta (m/s) - $V_{th}$ = theoretical velocity from Torricelli's law (m/s) - $a$ = geometric area of the orifice (m²) - $a_c$ = area of the jet at the vena contracta (m²) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fundamental Relationship (must be memorised for KTU exams):** > $$C_d = C_c \times C_v$$ > This relation is the mathematical signature of orifice flow and is a guaranteed question in either the viva, Part A, or Part B of the lab examination. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition Imagine you are **watering the garden** using a **bucket placed at a different height of water** (head) above a hole drilled near the bottom. Intuitively, you would observe three things: 1. **The jet doesn't travel as fast as physics predicts.** Friction along the orifice edge and turbulence inside the opening slows the water down. This shortfall is quantified by $C_v$ — it tells you *"how much of the ideal kinetic energy is actually delivered to the jet."* 2. **The jet is narrower than the hole.** After leaving the orifice, the streamlines cannot turn sharply around the sharp edge and they continue to converge for a small distance, forming a necked-down section called the **vena contracta**. This contraction is captured by $C_c$. 3. **The actual flow rate is less than the theoretical flow rate.** Combining both losses gives the **coefficient of discharge** $C_d$ — the single number engineers use to size orifices, nozzles, and weirs in real pipelines and tanks. > [!TIP] > **Quick mental picture:** Think of $C_c$ as a *"geometric"* loss (jet shrinks), $C_v$ as a *"dynamic"* loss (jet slows), and $C_d$ as the *"combined"* loss (less water actually leaves per second than the ideal formula predicts). ## 1.3 Real-World Engineering Applications - **Chemical Engineering:** Calibration of flow meters, dosing pumps, and tank outlet orifices. - **Civil Engineering:** Design of orifice spillways, penstock inlets, and flow measurement in open channels. - **Mechanical Engineering:** Orifice plates used in differential-pressure flowmeters on industrial pipelines. - **Hydraulic Engineering:** Fire-fighting nozzle design, hydraulic press control valves, and reservoir outlet works. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall — Don't Confuse Open vs. Submerged Orifice:** > An **open orifice** discharges a free jet into the atmosphere. A **submerged (or drowned) orifice** has the downstream side completely immersed in the same fluid. The coefficient values, head definitions, and the experimental setup differ significantly. This experiment is strictly for the **open orifice** case. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Torricelli's Theorem Consider a tank with a small orifice on its vertical side, located at a depth $H$ below the free liquid surface. By applying **Bernoulli's equation** between a point on the free surface (1) and a point on the orifice just outside (2), with the assumption of *steady, incompressible, inviscid flow* and *negligible velocity of approach*: $$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$ Applying the boundary conditions: $p_1 = p_2 = p_{atm}$, $V_1 \approx 0$ (large tank), and the head difference $z_1 - z_2 = H$: $$V_{th} = \sqrt{2gH}$$ This is the celebrated **Torricelli's law** — the theoretical (ideal) velocity of efflux. ## 2.2 The Three Coefficients in Detail ### (a) Coefficient of Velocity ($C_v$) Because of viscous resistance at the sharp edge of the orifice and the curvature of streamlines, the actual velocity $V_{act}$ at the vena contracta is less than $V_{th}$. $$C_v = \frac{V_{act}}{V_{th}} = \frac{V_{act}}{\sqrt{2gH}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Typical value for a sharp-edged orifice:** $C_v \approx 0.95$ to $0.99$ ### (b) Coefficient of Contraction ($C_c$) The streamlines issuing from the orifice continue to converge beyond the orifice plate until they become parallel. The cross-section at this point (the vena contracta) is smaller than the geometric orifice area. $$C_c = \frac{a_c}{a} \quad \text{where} \quad a_c = C_c \cdot a$$ > [!NOTE] > **Typical value for a sharp-edged circular orifice:** $C_c \approx 0.61$ to $0.65$ ### (c) Coefficient of Discharge ($C_d$) This is the most practical coefficient, used directly in pipe and tank design. $$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{Q_{act}}{a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Typical value for a sharp-edged orifice:** $C_d \approx 0.60$ to $0.65$ ## 2.3 Master Formula Sheet (KTU Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains every equation a KTU student needs to score full marks on this experiment. Memorise the row entries, not the units. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Source / How Determined | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Theoretical velocity | $V_{th} = \sqrt{2gH}$ | m/s | Torricelli's law | | 2 | Theoretical discharge | $Q_{th} = a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$ | m³/s | Area × ideal velocity | | 3 | Actual discharge | $Q_{act} = \dfrac{\text{Volume collected}}{\text{Time taken}} = \dfrac{R}{t}$ | m³/s | Collecting tank + stopwatch | | 4 | Coefficient of discharge | $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \dfrac{R}{t \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}}$ | dimensionless | Direct ratio | | 5 | Actual horizontal velocity | $V_x = \dfrac{x}{\sqrt{2y/g}}$ | m/s | Trajectory equation | | 6 | Actual resultant velocity | $V_{act} = \sqrt{V_x^2 + V_y^2} = \dfrac{x}{2y}\sqrt{x^2 + 4y^2} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{2}}$ | m/s | Projectile motion | | 7 | Simplified $V_{act}$ (using $t = \sqrt{2y/g}$) | $V_{act} = \dfrac{x}{\sqrt{2y/g}} = x\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{2y}}$ | m/s | When $V_x = V_{act}$ (assumed) | | 8 | Coefficient of velocity | $C_v = \dfrac{V_{act}}{\sqrt{2gH}}$ | dimensionless | Trajectory measurement | | 9 | Coefficient of contraction | $C_c = \dfrac{C_d}{C_v}$ | dimensionless | Indirect from 4 & 8 | | 10 | Cross-check relation | $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$ | dimensionless | Fundamental identity | > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** The simpler approximate formula $V_{act} = x\sqrt{g/(2y)}$ is accepted in lab calculations but the *more accurate* form $V_{act} = \sqrt{V_x^2 + V_y^2}$ may be asked in Part B derivations. Always state which form you are using. ## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why This Experiment Matters The coefficients $C_d$, $C_v$, $C_c$ are **NOT** universal constants — they depend on: - The Reynolds number of the flow (laminar vs. turbulent regime) - The head $H$ above the orifice - The orifice shape (circular, rectangular, triangular) - The sharpness of the edge By plotting $C_d$ vs. $H$, $C_v$ vs. $H$, and $C_c$ vs. $H$ and observing the trends, students validate a fundamental result of fluid mechanics: **at sufficiently high Reynolds numbers, all three coefficients become approximately constant** — a property exploited in the design of **orifice flow meters** used in chemical plants and oil & gas pipelines worldwide. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Theoretical Velocity (Torricelli's Law) **Starting point:** Apply Bernoulli's equation between two points — (1) the free liquid surface in the tank and (2) the jet just outside the orifice. **Assumptions:** - Steady, incompressible flow - Inviscid fluid (no friction) - Atmospheric pressure acts on both the free surface and the jet - The tank cross-section is much larger than the orifice, so $V_1 \approx 0$ - The orifice is small, so the head $H$ is sensibly constant during the test **Step 1 — Write Bernoulli's equation:** $$\frac{p_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$ **Step 2 — Substitute boundary conditions:** - $p_1 = p_2 = p_{atm}$ (both exposed to atmosphere) - $V_1 \approx 0$ (large tank assumption) - $z_1 - z_2 = H$ (head of liquid above the orifice centerline) This gives: $$0 + 0 + z_1 = 0 + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$ $$H = \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $V_2$:** $$\boxed{V_{th} = \sqrt{2gH}}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation of $C_d$ in Terms of Measured Quantities **Step 1:** Theoretical discharge from Torricelli's law: $$Q_{th} = a \cdot V_{th} = a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$$ **Step 2:** Actual discharge from the collecting tank (rise of water level method): $$Q_{act} = \frac{\text{Volume of water collected}}{\text{Time of collection}} = \frac{A_{ct} \cdot R}{t}$$ where $A_{ct}$ is the cross-sectional area of the collecting tank, $R$ is the rise of water level in the collecting tank in time $t$. **Step 3:** Therefore: $$\boxed{C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{A_{ct} \cdot R}{t \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}}}$$ For a circular orifice of diameter $d$, $a = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$. $$\boxed{C_d = \frac{4 \cdot A_{ct} \cdot R}{\pi d^2 \cdot t \cdot \sqrt{2gH}}}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation of $C_v$ from the Jet Trajectory The jet emerging from the orifice traces a **parabolic trajectory** under gravity, exactly like a projectile. By measuring the coordinates $(x, y)$ of any convenient point on the jet, we can back-calculate the actual velocity at the orifice. **Step 1 — Resolve the velocity into components:** Let $V_{act}$ be the actual velocity at the orifice (assumed to be horizontal). The jet is launched horizontally with horizontal velocity $V_x = V_{act}$ and initial vertical velocity $V_y = 0$. **Step 2 — Horizontal motion (constant velocity):** $$x = V_x \cdot t \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_x = \frac{x}{t}$$ **Step 3 — Vertical motion (free fall from rest):** $$y = \frac{1}{2} g t^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \sqrt{\frac{2y}{g}}$$ **Step 4 — Combine to find $V_{act}$:** $$V_{act} = V_x = \frac{x}{t} = \frac{x}{\sqrt{2y/g}} = x\sqrt{\frac{g}{2y}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Validity:** This formula assumes the velocity vector at the orifice is purely horizontal. In reality, there is a small vertical component due to the curvature of streamlines, but for typical lab orifices the error is < 2%, which is acceptable. **Step 5 — Apply the definition of $C_v$:** $$\boxed{C_v = \frac{V_{act}}{V_{th}} = \frac{x \sqrt{g/(2y)}}{\sqrt{2gH}} = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{yH}}}$$ This is the **most tested equation** for this experiment in KTU exams. A similar derivation appears in the viva every semester. **Step 6 — More accurate form (using resultant velocity):** Some KTU questions require the more rigorous form where the actual velocity vector at the vena contracta is the resultant of $V_x$ and $V_y$: $$V_{act} = \sqrt{V_x^2 + V_y^2}$$ where $V_y = g t = g \sqrt{2y/g} = \sqrt{2gy}$. After algebraic manipulation (shown in full below): $$\begin{aligned} V_{act} &= \sqrt{ \frac{x^2 g}{2y} + 2gy } \\ &= \sqrt{ \frac{g(x^2 + 4y^2)}{2y} } \\ &= \frac{\sqrt{x^2 + 4y^2}}{2y} \cdot \sqrt{gy} \end{aligned}$$ Then: $$C_v = \frac{V_{act}}{\sqrt{2gH}} = \frac{\sqrt{x^2 + 4y^2}}{2y} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{gy}}{\sqrt{2gH}} = \frac{\sqrt{x^2 + 4y^2}}{2\sqrt{2yH}}$$ > [!TIP] > **KTU 2024 Scheme convention:** For lab work, the simpler $V_{act} = x\sqrt{g/(2y)}$ is universally accepted. Use the more elaborate form only if the question explicitly says *"resolve the velocity vector into components."* ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sample KTU-style calculation — read carefully, this is exactly the format you should follow in your record.** **Given Data:** - Diameter of orifice: $d = 20 \text{ mm} = 0.020 \text{ m}$ - Cross-sectional area of orifice: $a = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.020)^2}{4} = 3.142 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$ - Cross-sectional area of collecting tank: $A_{ct} = 0.25 \text{ m}^2$ - Head of water above orifice centerline: $H = 0.40 \text{ m}$ - Rise of water in collecting tank: $R = 10 \text{ cm} = 0.10 \text{ m}$ - Time for the rise: $t = 60 \text{ s}$ - Trajectory coordinate: $x = 0.30 \text{ m}$ (horizontal) - Trajectory coordinate: $y = 0.15 \text{ m}$ (vertical drop) **Solution:** **Step 1 — Theoretical velocity:** $$V_{th} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.40} = \sqrt{7.848} = 2.801 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Theoretical discharge:** $$Q_{th} = a \cdot V_{th} = 3.142 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.801 = 8.802 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 3 — Actual discharge:** $$Q_{act} = \frac{A_{ct} \cdot R}{t} = \frac{0.25 \times 0.10}{60} = 4.167 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 4 — Coefficient of discharge:** $$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{4.167 \times 10^{-4}}{8.802 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.4734$$ > [!WARNING] > **Sanity check:** $C_d$ should be between 0.6 and 0.65 for a sharp-edged orifice. The low value of 0.47 here suggests measurement error. In a real lab, you would repeat the trial and check for parallax errors in reading $R$ or $H$. **Step 5 — Actual jet velocity (from trajectory):** $$V_{act} = x \sqrt{\frac{g}{2y}} = 0.30 \times \sqrt{\frac{9.81}{2 \times 0.15}} = 0.30 \times \sqrt{32.7} = 0.30 \times 5.718 = 1.715 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 6 — Coefficient of velocity:** $$C_v = \frac{V_{act}}{V_{th}} = \frac{1.715}{2.801} = 0.6124$$ **Step 7 — Coefficient of contraction:** $$\boxed{C_c = \frac{C_d}{C_v} = \frac{0.4734}{0.6124} = 0.7730}$$ **Step 8 — Verification of $C_d = C_c \cdot C_v$:** $$C_c \cdot C_v = 0.7730 \times 0.6124 = 0.4734 = C_d \quad \checkmark$$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Apparatus Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[OVERHEAD TANK
Constant water supply] --> B[EXPERIMENTAL TANK
with sharp-edged orifice] B --> C[STILLING CHAMBER
to dampen fluctuations] C --> D[POINT GAUGE
measures head H above orifice] B --> E[FREE JET
parabolic trajectory] E --> F[TRAJECTORY TABLE
with x-y grid] E --> G[COLLECTING TANK
measures discharge Qact] G --> H[STOPWATCH
measures time t] H --> I[CALCULATION OF Cd, Cv, Cc] D --> I F --> I ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Water enters from the overhead tank, passes through a stilling chamber to ensure a calm surface for accurate head measurement, flows out through the sharp-edged orifice, traces a parabolic jet that is read on the trajectory table, and finally discharges into the collecting tank for discharge measurement. ## 4.2 Vena Contracta — Conceptual Sketch (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TANK [Reservoir / Tank with Water] T1[Free water surface
Pressure patm] T2[Head H above orifice] end subgraph ORIFICE [Sharp-edged Orifice Plate] O1[Orifice diameter d
Geometric area a] O2[Streamlines curve inward] end subgraph VENA [Vena Contracta Region] V1[Minimum jet area ac] V2[Streamlines become parallel] V3[Jet velocity Vact at vena contracta] end subgraph JET [Free Jet Trajectory] J1[Horizontal distance x] J2[Vertical drop y] J3[Parabolic path of jet] end T1 --> T2 T2 --> O1 O1 --> O2 O2 --> V1 V1 --> V2 V2 --> V3 V3 --> J1 V3 --> J2 J1 --> J3 J2 --> J3 ``` ## 4.3 Experimental Procedure Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD P0[START EXPERIMENT] --> P1[Set water level in tank
maintain steady head H] P1 --> P2[Measure head H
using point gauge] P2 --> P3[Note trajectory coordinates
x and y from grid table] P3 --> P4[Start stopwatch
simultaneously divert jet to collecting tank] P4 --> P5[After time t
stop stopwatch and stop water entry] P5 --> P6[Measure rise R
in collecting tank] P6 --> P7[Calculate Qact Acd multiplied by R over t] P7 --> P8[Calculate Qth a multiplied by sqrt of 2gH] P8 --> P9[Calculate Cd equals Qact over Qth] P9 --> P10[Calculate Vact using x and y trajectory] P10 --> P11[Calculate Cv equals Vact over Vth] P11 --> P12[Calculate Cc equals Cd divided by Cv] P12 --> P13[Verify Cd equals Cc times Cv] P13 --> P14[Repeat for 5 different heads H] P14 --> P15[Plot graphs: Cd vs H, Cv vs H, Cc vs H] P15 --> P16[END EXPERIMENT] ``` ## 4.4 Observation Table Template (KTU Standard Format) | Sl. No. | Head H (m) | x (m) | y (m) | Rise R (m) | Time t (s) | Q_act (m³/s) | C_d | C_v | C_c | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | | 2 | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | | 3 | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | | 4 | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | | 5 | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | **Suggested Graph Plot:** $C_d$ vs. $\sqrt{H}$ (linear plot from which $C_d$ can be read as the slope), or $C_d$ vs. $H$ (asymptotic plot showing $C_d$ approaches a constant at high $H$). # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, Module 2] **Define the coefficient of discharge $C_d$ of an orifice and state its typical value for a sharp-edged circular orifice.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **coefficient of discharge $C_d$** of an orifice is defined as the ratio of the actual discharge through the orifice to the theoretical discharge computed from Torricelli's law: $$C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{Q_{act}}{a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}}$$ It is a **dimensionless** quantity. For a **sharp-edged circular orifice** under standard laboratory conditions, the typical value of $C_d$ lies in the range **0.60 to 0.65**, with an average of approximately **0.62**. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Retest] **What is the vena contracta? How is the coefficient of contraction $C_c$ defined?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **vena contracta** is the section of the jet downstream of a sharp-edged orifice where the streamlines become parallel and the cross-sectional area of the jet reaches a **minimum value** $a_c$. The distance from the orifice plane to the vena contracta is approximately **half the diameter** of the orifice. The **coefficient of contraction $C_c$** is defined as the ratio of the area of the jet at the vena contracta to the geometric area of the orifice: $$C_c = \frac{a_c}{a}$$ For a sharp-edged circular orifice, $C_c \approx 0.62$ to $0.65$. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part B questions on the lab exam carry 14 marks with internal choice. The model answers below follow the exact step-by-step, equation-based style expected in the answer script. ### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Sample, Module 2]** **(a) Derive an expression for the coefficient of velocity $C_v$ of an orifice using the trajectory of the jet. State the assumption clearly. (7 marks)** **(b) A 25 mm diameter sharp-edged orifice is fitted to a vertical side of a large tank. The head of water above the orifice is 1.20 m. The coordinates of a point on the jet, measured from the vena contracta, are $x = 0.45$ m and $y = 0.20$ m. If the coefficient of contraction is 0.65, calculate (i) the actual discharge through the orifice, and (ii) the actual velocity at the vena contracta. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation of $C_v$ (7 marks):** Let the jet emerge horizontally from the orifice with actual velocity $V_{act}$. Let $x$ and $y$ be the horizontal and vertical coordinates of any point on the jet, measured from the vena contracta. The horizontal motion is uniform: $$x = V_{act} \cdot t \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{act} = \frac{x}{t} \quad \text{...(i)}$$ The vertical motion is a free fall from rest: $$y = \frac{1}{2} g t^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \sqrt{\frac{2y}{g}} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ Substituting (ii) into (i): $$V_{act} = \frac{x}{\sqrt{2y/g}} = x \sqrt{\frac{g}{2y}}$$ By definition: $$C_v = \frac{V_{act}}{V_{th}} = \frac{V_{act}}{\sqrt{2gH}}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{C_v = \frac{x \sqrt{g/(2y)}}{\sqrt{2gH}} = \frac{x}{2\sqrt{yH}}}$$ **Assumption stated:** The velocity vector at the vena contracta is assumed to be **purely horizontal**, neglecting the small vertical component of the jet as it leaves the orifice. **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** - $d = 25$ mm $= 0.025$ m - $H = 1.20$ m - $x = 0.45$ m, $y = 0.20$ m - $C_c = 0.65$ - $g = 9.81$ m/s² **Step 1 — Actual velocity from trajectory:** $$V_{act} = x \sqrt{\frac{g}{2y}} = 0.45 \times \sqrt{\frac{9.81}{2 \times 0.20}} = 0.45 \times \sqrt{24.525} = 0.45 \times 4.952 = 2.228 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Area of the orifice:** $$a = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.025)^2}{4} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 3 — Area at vena contracta:** $$a_c = C_c \cdot a = 0.65 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-4} = 3.191 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 4 — (i) Actual discharge:** $$Q_{act} = a_c \cdot V_{act} = 3.191 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.228 = 7.110 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ $$\boxed{Q_{act} = 7.11 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 0.711 \text{ L/s}}$$ **Step 5 — (ii) Actual velocity at vena contracta (already computed):** $$\boxed{V_{act} = 2.228 \text{ m/s}}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Retest, Module 2]** **(a) State the relationship between the three coefficients of an orifice and explain why the coefficient of discharge is smaller than the coefficient of velocity. (7 marks)** **(b) A 30 mm diameter circular orifice discharges 3.6 litres of water in 20 seconds under a constant head of 1.50 m. Calculate the coefficient of discharge $C_d$, the actual velocity at the vena contracta if $C_v = 0.97$, and the diameter of the jet at the vena contracta. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Theoretical Discussion (7 marks):** **Relationship between the three coefficients:** $$C_d = C_c \times C_v$$ This can be derived as follows: $$\begin{aligned} C_d &= \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{a_c \cdot V_{act}}{a \cdot V_{th}} \\ &= \left(\frac{a_c}{a}\right) \times \left(\frac{V_{act}}{V_{th}}\right) \\ &= C_c \times C_v \end{aligned}$$ **Why $C_d < C_v$:** The coefficient of velocity $C_v$ accounts only for the **reduction in velocity** (loss of kinetic energy due to viscous effects and streamline curvature). The coefficient of discharge $C_d$, however, accounts for **both** the reduction in velocity *and* the reduction in flow area (jet contraction). Since the area of the vena contracta $a_c$ is always less than the orifice area $a$, we have $C_c < 1$. Therefore: $$C_d = C_c \cdot C_v < 1 \cdot C_v = C_v \quad \text{(since } C_c < 1\text{)}$$ Hence $C_d$ is always less than $C_v$. Physically, the actual discharge is reduced not just because the jet is slower than ideal, but also because the jet cross-section is smaller than the geometric orifice — this combined effect makes $C_d < C_v$. **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** - $d = 30$ mm $= 0.030$ m - $V = 3.6$ L $= 3.6 \times 10^{-3}$ m³ collected in $t = 20$ s - $H = 1.50$ m - $C_v = 0.97$ - $g = 9.81$ m/s² **Step 1 — Orifice area:** $$a = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.030)^2}{4} = 7.069 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2 — Actual discharge:** $$Q_{act} = \frac{V}{t} = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{-3}}{20} = 1.8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 3 — Theoretical discharge:** $$Q_{th} = a \sqrt{2gH} = 7.069 \times 10^{-4} \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 1.50}$$ $$Q_{th} = 7.069 \times 10^{-4} \times \sqrt{29.43} = 7.069 \times 10^{-4} \times 5.425 = 3.835 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 4 — Coefficient of discharge:** $$\boxed{C_d = \frac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^{-4}}{3.835 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.4694}$$ **Step 5 — Theoretical and actual velocity:** $$V_{th} = \sqrt{2gH} = \sqrt{29.43} = 5.425 \text{ m/s}$$ $$V_{act} = C_v \cdot V_{th} = 0.97 \times 5.425 = 5.262 \text{ m/s}$$ $$\boxed{V_{act} = 5.262 \text{ m/s}}$$ **Step 6 — Coefficient of contraction:** $$C_c = \frac{C_d}{C_v} = \frac{0.4694}{0.97} = 0.4839$$ **Step 7 — Area at vena contracta:** $$a_c = C_c \cdot a = 0.4839 \times 7.069 \times 10^{-4} = 3.421 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 8 — Diameter at vena contracta:** $$d_c = \sqrt{\frac{4 a_c}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 3.421 \times 10^{-4}}{\pi}} = \sqrt{4.357 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.02087 \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{d_c = 20.87 \text{ mm} \approx 20.9 \text{ mm}}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **The most common mistakes that cost KTU students 2–3 marks each:** > > 1. **Forgetting units in the final answer.** Always write units ($C_d$ is dimensionless, but $Q$ needs m³/s, $V$ needs m/s, $d$ needs mm or m). > 2. **Wrong diameter in area calculation.** Use $d$, not the *radius* $r$. The formula is $a = \pi d^2 / 4$. > 3. **Mixing $C_c$ and $C_v$ in the discharge formula.** The actual discharge is $Q_{act} = C_d \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$, **not** $C_c \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$ and **not** $C_v \cdot a \cdot \sqrt{2gH}$. > 4. **Sign error in trajectory time.** The time of flight is $t = \sqrt{2y/g}$, not $t = \sqrt{y/g}$. Half a mark lost for each such error. > 5. **Not plotting the graphs.** KTU 2024 Scheme requires at least one of $C_d$ vs. $H$ or $C_d$ vs. $\sqrt{H}$ to be plotted. Internal marks (10 marks out of 50 in the lab report) are awarded for graphs alone. > 6. **Reporting too few or too many significant figures.** Final answers should be reported to 3 significant figures (e.g., $C_d = 0.621$, not 0.62 and not 0.6213). --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist - [ ] **Orifice** = small opening in a tank wall; **open orifice** = discharges a free jet into atmosphere. - [ ] **Three coefficients:** $C_d$ (discharge), $C_v$ (velocity), $C_c$ (contraction). All dimensionless. - [ ] **Key identity (must memorise):** $C_d = C_c \times C_v$. - [ ] **Torricelli's law:** $V_{th} = \sqrt{2gH}$ — the theoretical velocity of efflux. - [ ] **Typical values for a sharp-edged circular orifice:** $C_v \approx 0.95\text{–}0.99$, $C_c \approx 0.61\text{–}0.65$, $C_d \approx 0.60\text{–}0.65$. - [ ] **Vena contracta** = point of minimum jet cross-section; located at ~0.5× orifice diameter downstream of the orifice. - [ ] **$C_d$ from discharge measurement:** $C_d = Q_{act} / (a\sqrt{2gH})$. - [ ] **$C_v$ from trajectory measurement:** $C_v = x / (2\sqrt{yH})$ — derived from projectile motion. - [ ] **$C_c$ from the other two:** $C_c = C_d / C_v$ (indirect method). - [ ] **Experimental setup:** Overhead tank → stilling chamber → experimental tank with sharp-edged orifice → trajectory table (for $x, y$) → collecting tank (for $Q_{act}$). - [ ] **Procedure summary:** Set head $H$ → measure $(x, y)$ → divert jet to collecting tank → record rise $R$ and time $t$ → compute $C_d$, $C_v$, $C_c$ → repeat for 4–5 heads → plot graphs. - [ ] **Graphs to plot:** $C_d$ vs. $H$, $C_v$ vs. $H$, $C_c$ vs. $H$ (or $C_d$ vs. $\sqrt{H}$ for linear best fit). - [ ] **Application areas:** Orifice flowmeters, spillways, chemical dosing tanks, fire-fighting nozzles, hydraulic control valves. - [ ] **Sanity check formula:** $C_c$ must always be less than 1, and $C_d$ must always be less than $C_v$. > [!TIP] > **Last-minute KTU exam mantra:** *"Torricelli for theory, trajectory for $C_v$, discharge tank for $C_d$, divide for $C_c$."* This single sentence covers 90% of the marks in this experiment.
